topics.title: Determination of Flash and Fire Points of Petroleum Fuels and Oils
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL408 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Flash and Fire Points of Petroleum Fuels and Oils

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2: FLASH AND FIRE POINT DETERMINATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone)**
> The **Flash Point** of a petroleum fuel or lubricating oil is defined as the *lowest temperature* (corrected to a barometric pressure of 101.325 kPa or 760 mm Hg) at which the application of a standard test flame causes the **vapors above the liquid surface to ignite momentarily (flash)** under specified test conditions.
>
> The **Fire Point** is defined as the *lowest temperature* at which the application of a standard test flame causes the **vapors of the oil to ignite and continue to burn steadily for at least 5 seconds** when the flame is passed across the surface.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Pressure Cooker vs. Open Pan" Analogy**
> Imagine heating water in two vessels — one **sealed** (pressure cooker) and one **open** (frying pan). The sealed one traps all the steam, so vapor pressure builds up *faster* at a lower temperature. The open one lets steam escape freely, so you need much *higher heat* before vapors concentrate. This is exactly why:
> - A **Closed Cup** flash point (Pensky-Martens) gives a **lower** flash point temperature.
> - An **Open Cup** flash point (Cleveland) gives a **higher** flash point temperature because vapors escape into the atmosphere.
>
> The **flash** is like a brief spark — fuel is just enough to ignite but not sustain combustion. The **fire point** is when the liquid itself gets hot enough that combustion becomes self-sustaining — like the difference between a matchstick strike and a campfire.

### Key Physical Standards & Metrics

- **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** 101.325 kPa (760 mm Hg)
- **Test Flame Application Interval:** Every 1 °C rise near the flash point (open cup) or 1 °C per stirring cycle (closed cup)
- **Standard Methods:** ASTM D92 (Open Cup / Cleveland), ASTM D93 (Closed Cup / Pensky-Martens), IS 1448 Part 21 & Part 32
- **Fire Point – Flash Point Differential:** Typically **5 °C to 30 °C** for most petroleum oils
- **Units:** Temperature in °C (Celsius)

### Why is This Experiment Critical in Thermal Engineering?

> [!NOTE]
> - **Storage & Transport Safety:** Determines safe handling temperatures for fuels, lubricants, and hydraulic oils.
> - **Fire Hazard Classification:** Categorizes liquids as Class I, II, or III based on flash point (NFPA / IS classification).
> - **Engine Performance:** Indicates volatility of fuels — affects cold-start behavior and vapor lock tendency.
> - **Quality Control:** Detects contamination of lubricating oils with lighter petroleum fractions.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Step-by-Step Logic

When a petroleum oil is heated, molecules at the liquid surface undergo **evaporation**. The escaping molecules form a **vapor-air mixture** above the liquid. For combustion to occur, three conditions must be simultaneously satisfied (the classic **Fire Triangle**):

1. **Fuel** (vapor concentration within flammable limits)
2. **Oxygen** (from atmospheric air)
3. **Heat** (sufficient activation energy to ignite the mixture)

The progression of temperature stages for a heated oil is:

```
Stage 1: Bubble Point (Boiling Begins) → Stage 2: Flash Point (Transient Ignition)
                                                       ↓
                                            Stage 3: Fire Point (Sustained Combustion)
```

### 2.2 Difference Between Open Cup and Closed Cup Methods

| Parameter | Open Cup (COC - ASTM D92) | Closed Cup (PMCC - ASTM D93) |
|---|---|---|
| Vapour Escape | Vapours escape freely to atmosphere | Vapours are confined within the cup lid |
| Flash Point Value | **Higher** (5–30 °C higher) | **Lower** (true lower flammability limit) |
| Test Sample Stirring | NOT stirred | Continuously stirred |
| Heating Rate | 14–17 °C/min, then 5–6 °C/min near flash | 5–6 °C/min throughout |
| Test Flame Interval | Every 2 °C, then every 1 °C near flash | Every 1 °C |
| Best For | Fire point determination & lubricating oils | Flash point of fuels, diesel, kerosene |
| Apparatus Used | Cleveland Open Cup | Pensky-Martens Closed Cup / Abel |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All numerical answers in KTU lab viva and exams derive from these equations:

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{corrected} = T_{observed} + C$ | Barometric pressure correction | °C |
| 2 | $C = 0.03 \times (760 - P_{actual})$ | Correction factor (P in mm Hg) | °C |
| 3 | $C = 0.25 \times (101.325 - P_{kPa})$ | Correction factor (P in kPa) | °C |
| 4 | $\Delta T = T_{fire} - T_{flash}$ | Fire-flash differential | °C |
| 5 | $LFL \approx T_{flash}$ | Lower Flammability Limit proxy | °C |
| 6 | $\text{Fire Hazard Class} = f(T_{flash})$ | NFPA / IS Classification | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorization Tip for KTU Exam:** Always write the formula with **standard atmospheric pressure = 760 mm Hg = 101.325 kPa** as the reference. The correction `C` is added when actual pressure is **lower** than standard (high-altitude labs) and subtracted when it is **higher**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aviation Turbine Fuel (ATF):** Must have flash point > 38 °C (closed cup) for safe air transport.
- **Diesel Engine Lubricating Oils:** Higher fire point means greater resistance to breakdown in hot running engines (SAE 20W-40, 15W-40 grading).
- **Hydraulic Oils in CNC Machines:** Flash point > 200 °C ensures safety in high-pressure hydraulic circuits.
- **Transformer Oils:** Flash point > 140 °C is mandatory (per IS 335) for fire safety in substations.
- **Bitumen & Asphalt:** High fire point (>250 °C) required for road paving and roofing.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Computations

### 3.1 Derivation of the Barometric Pressure Correction Formula

The flash point of a fuel depends on its **vapor pressure**, which is a function of temperature and atmospheric pressure. At lower atmospheric pressure (e.g., high-altitude labs in Ooty, Munnar), the boiling point of liquids decreases, meaning the vapor reaches flammable concentration at a **lower observed temperature**. Conversely, at higher pressure, a higher temperature is needed.

By Clapeyron's thermodynamic relation (linearized Antoine approximation):

$$
\log_{10}(P) = A - \frac{B}{T}
$$

Differentiating with respect to $T$:

$$
\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{B \cdot P}{T^2 \cdot \ln(10)}
$$

For small pressure variations, empirical correlations for petroleum oils yield:

$$
\Delta T_{correction} \approx 0.03 \times (P_{std} - P_{actual}) \quad \text{[°C]}
$$

where the constant **0.03** (°C/mm Hg) is empirically determined for petroleum products.

**Reference value:** $P_{std} = 760 \text{ mm Hg} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Exam Style

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PROBLEM:** During a Cleveland Open Cup test on a sample of lubricating oil, the following data was recorded:
> - Observed Flash Point = 198 °C
> - Observed Fire Point = 215 °C
> - Barometric Pressure during test = 745 mm Hg
>
> **Calculate:**
> (a) The barometric correction factor `C`
> (b) The corrected Flash Point and Fire Point
> (c) The Fire-Flash Differential

---

#### (a) Barometric Correction Factor `C`

$$
C = 0.03 \times (760 - P_{actual})
$$

Substituting $P_{actual} = 745 \text{ mm Hg}$:

$$
C = 0.03 \times (760 - 745)
$$

$$
C = 0.03 \times 15
$$

$$
\boxed{C = 0.45 \text{ °C}}
$$

> Since actual pressure is *lower* than standard, we **add** the correction to observed values.

---

#### (b) Corrected Flash Point

$$
T_{flash,\,corrected} = T_{flash,\,observed} + C
$$

$$
T_{flash,\,corrected} = 198 + 0.45
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{flash,\,corrected} = 198.45 \text{ °C}}
$$

#### Corrected Fire Point

$$
T_{fire,\,corrected} = T_{fire,\,observed} + C
$$

$$
T_{fire,\,corrected} = 215 + 0.45
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{fire,\,corrected} = 215.45 \text{ °C}}
$$

---

#### (c) Fire-Flash Differential $\Delta T$

$$
\Delta T = T_{fire,\,corrected} - T_{flash,\,corrected}
$$

$$
\Delta T = 215.45 - 198.45
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta T = 17 \text{ °C}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A differential of 17 °C indicates good-quality lubricating oil. If $\Delta T$ were less than 5 °C, it would suggest contamination with volatile petroleum fractions. If $\Delta T$ were very large (>30 °C), it would indicate a highly viscous or paraffinic oil.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Apparatus to Use?

| Sample Type | Recommended Test | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Gasoline, Naphtha | **Abel / Closed Cup** | Very volatile; open cup gives unreliable low readings |
| Kerosene, Diesel, ATF | **Pensky-Martens (Closed Cup)** | Standard regulatory requirement (ASTM D93, IS 1448 P-21) |
| Lubricating Oils, Heavy Fuel Oil | **Cleveland Open Cup (COC)** | ASTM D92 standard; covers wider temperature range (up to 400 °C) |
| Bitumen, Asphalt | **Cleveland Open Cup** | High viscosity, requires open cup |
| Transformer Oil | **Pensky-Martens (Closed Cup)** | Mandatory per IS 335 standard |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cleveland Open Cup (COC) Test Apparatus

> [!NOTE]
> The Cleveland Open Cup is the **standard apparatus for fire point determination** in KTU lab curricula. The schematic below shows the functional flow of heating, vapor generation, and flame application.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Thermometer Socket Holder"] --> nodeB["Test Flame Ignition Source"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Brass Cup Holding Oil Sample"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Heating Plate With Gas Burner"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Temperature Controller Knob"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Barometer Inlet For Correction"]
    nodeC --> nodeG["Vapour-Air Mixture Zone"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Atmospheric Vent Open To Air"]
    nodeB --> nodeI["Flame Sweep Mechanism Every 1C"]
    nodeC --> nodeJ["Oil Sample Level 10 mm Below Cup Rim"]
```

**Key Functional Points in the Schematic:**

- **Test Flame Ignition Source:** Small pilot flame that sweeps across the cup surface every 1 °C temperature rise near the flash point.
- **Brass Cup:** Holds the oil sample; mounted on a heating plate.
- **Vapour-Air Mixture Zone:** Open to atmosphere — distinguishes COC from closed cup.
- **Temperature Controller:** Maintains 14–17 °C/min heating rate initially, slowed to 5–6 °C/min near the expected flash point.

---

### 4.2 Pensky-Martens Closed Cup (PMCC) Test Apparatus

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Stirring Motor"] --> nodeB["Stirring Shaft With Paddles"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Closed Brass Test Cup With Lid"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Heating Bath Glycerine Or Silicone Oil"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Gas Burner Or Electric Heater"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["Pilot Flame Ignition Port"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Sliding Shutter Mechanism"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Test Flame Introduction Every 1C"]
    nodeC --> nodeI["Vapour Trap Closed Lid"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Pressure Equalising Vent"]
```

**Key Functional Differences from COC:**

- The **closed lid** traps vapors → lower observed flash point.
- **Continuous mechanical stirring** (90–250 rpm) ensures uniform temperature and vapor saturation.
- **Sliding shutter** opens momentarily (1 second) to admit the test flame into the cup.

---

### 4.3 Process Flow of Flash Point Determination (KTU Lab Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["START"]) --> prep["Step 1: Clean And Dry The Test Cup"]
    prep --> fill["Step 2: Fill Cup With Oil Sample To Marked Level"]
    fill --> mount["Step 3: Mount Cup On Heating Plate With Thermometer"]
    mount --> heat1["Step 4: Heat At 14 To 17 C Per Min"]
    heat1 --> slow["Step 5: Reduce To 5 To 6 C Per Min Near Expected Flash"]
    slow --> flame1["Step 6: Apply Test Flame Every 2 C Then Every 1 C"]
    flame1 --> check{"Step 7: Flash Observed On Surface?"}
    check -->|"No"| flame1
    check -->|"Yes"| record["Step 8: Record Flash Point Temperature"]
    record --> cont["Step 9: Continue Heating For Fire Point"]
    cont --> flame2["Step 10: Apply Test Flame Every 1 C"]
    flame2 --> sustain{"Step 11: Flame Sustained For 5 Seconds?"}
    sustain -->|"No"| flame2
    sustain -->|"Yes"| fire["Step 12: Record Fire Point Temperature"]
    fire --> correct["Step 13: Apply Barometric Correction"]
    correct --> result["Step 14: Report Corrected Flash And Fire Points"]
    result --> stop(["END"])
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Practical Pitfall:** Many students apply the test flame continuously without pausing. Per IS 1448 / ASTM D92, the flame must be passed across the cup in a **smooth, single sweep lasting ~1 second**, then withdrawn. Continuous flame application will artificially ignite the sample and invalidate the test.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3 Mark Short Answer Questions

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Define Flash Point and Fire Point. State two engineering applications where this data is critical.*

**Model Answer:**

- **Flash Point:** The lowest temperature at which the vapors of a heated oil momentarily ignite (flash) when a standard test flame is passed across its surface, under specified conditions of test (corrected to 101.325 kPa).
- **Fire Point:** The lowest temperature at which the oil vapors ignite and continue to burn steadily for **at least 5 seconds** after removal of the test flame.

**Applications:**
1. **Classification of petroleum products** for safe storage and transport (e.g., diesel flash point > 55 °C per IS 1460).
2. **Selection of lubricants** for IC engines — higher fire point ensures resistance to thermal breakdown in hot running zones (piston ring area, bearing housings).

---

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Why does the closed cup flash point differ from the open cup flash point? State which is higher and why.*

**Model Answer:**

The **closed cup flash point is lower** than the open cup flash point (typically by 5–30 °C). 

**Reason:** In a closed cup (Pensky-Martens), the vapors are **trapped inside the lid**, so they accumulate faster above the liquid surface and reach the **lower explosive limit (LEL)** at a lower temperature. In an open cup (Cleveland), vapors escape freely into the atmosphere, so a higher temperature is required to achieve the same vapor concentration at the surface.

> **Q3.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** *State the significance of the Fire-Flash differential ($\Delta T$). What does a small differential indicate?*

**Model Answer:**

The **Fire-Flash Differential** is the difference between the fire point and flash point temperatures. It is a measure of the **safety margin** of a fuel or oil.

- A **small $\Delta T$ (< 5 °C)** indicates that the fuel is **highly volatile** and **hazardous** — fire can easily follow a flash.
- A **large $\Delta T$ (> 30 °C)** indicates a **stable, viscous oil** (e.g., heavy lubricants, bitumen) with low volatility.

---

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Common mistakes students make in this question:
> 1. **Forgetting the barometric correction** — accounts for 2–3 marks lost per question.
> 2. **Reversing the correction sign** (adding when pressure is *higher* than standard).
> 3. **Confusing flash and fire point definitions** — fire point requires **sustained combustion for 5 seconds**, not just ignition.
> 4. **Wrong unit convention** — always report in °C unless specified otherwise.
> 5. **Not specifying ASTM / IS standard** in apparatus descriptions — examiners expect ASTM D92 or IS 1448 P-32.

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** [Internal Choice]

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**
>
> During a Cleveland Open Cup (COC) test on a sample of **lubricating oil (SAE 20W-40)**, the following observations were made:
>
> | Trial | Observed Flash Point (°C) | Observed Fire Point (°C) |
> |---|---|---|
> | 1 | 222 | 240 |
> | 2 | 224 | 242 |
> | 3 | 221 | 239 |
>
> **Barometric pressure during test = 752 mm Hg.**
>
> **(a)** Calculate the corrected mean Flash Point and Fire Point.  
> **(b)** Calculate the Fire-Flash Differential and comment on the quality of the oil.  
> **(c)** List any two safety precautions to be observed during the experiment.

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Calculate the mean observed Flash Point.

$$
\overline{T}_{flash,\,observed} = \frac{222 + 224 + 221}{3}
$$

$$
\overline{T}_{flash,\,observed} = \frac{667}{3} = 222.33 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the mean observed Fire Point.

$$
\overline{T}_{fire,\,observed} = \frac{240 + 242 + 239}{3}
$$

$$
\overline{T}_{fire,\,observed} = \frac{721}{3} = 240.33 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the barometric correction factor `C`.

$$
C = 0.03 \times (760 - P_{actual})
$$

$$
C = 0.03 \times (760 - 752) = 0.03 \times 8
$$

$$
\boxed{C = 0.24 \text{ °C}}
$$

Since actual pressure (752 mm Hg) is **less** than standard (760 mm Hg), the correction is **added**.

**Step 4:** Apply correction.

$$
T_{flash,\,corrected} = 222.33 + 0.24 = \boxed{222.57 \text{ °C}}
$$

$$
T_{fire,\,corrected} = 240.33 + 0.24 = \boxed{240.57 \text{ °C}}
$$

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [4 Marks]

$$
\Delta T = T_{fire,\,corrected} - T_{flash,\,corrected}
$$

$$
\Delta T = 240.57 - 222.57
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta T = 18 \text{ °C}}
$$

**Comment:** A differential of **18 °C** is characteristic of **good-quality lubricating oil**. SAE 20W-40 typically exhibits differentials in the range of **15–25 °C**. The high corrected flash point (>220 °C) confirms suitability for **high-temperature engine applications** (e.g., petrol engine crankcases, gear oils).

##### Model Solution — Part (c) [3 Marks]

**Safety Precautions:**
1. The experiment must be conducted in a **well-ventilated area** or under a **fume hood** to prevent accumulation of flammable vapors.
2. A **Class B / CO₂ fire extinguisher** must be kept within arm's reach throughout the test.
3. The test flame should never be left unattended near the cup; the **gas supply** to the pilot burner must be turned off immediately after each sweep.

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks** [Alternative Choice]

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**
>
> **(a)** With the help of a neat labelled sketch, describe the **Cleveland Open Cup apparatus** used for the determination of flash and fire points. State the relevant IS / ASTM standard. [7 Marks]
>
> **(b)** Explain the **step-by-step experimental procedure** for determining the fire point of a lubricating oil using the Cleveland Open Cup. Mention the heating rates and flame application intervals specified by the standard. [7 Marks]

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Apparatus:** Cleveland Open Cup Tester (as per **ASTM D92 / IS 1448 Part 32**).

**Major Components:**

1. **Test Cup:** A brass cup of standard dimensions (height ~ 35 mm, diameter ~ 65 mm) with a filling mark indicating oil sample level (typically 10 mm below rim).
2. **Heating Plate:** A metal plate (usually cast iron) heated by a **gas burner** or **electric heater** beneath the test cup.
3. **Thermometer:** A calibrated ASTM thermometer (range suitable to expected flash point) inserted through a socket at the cup rim, with bulb immersed in the oil.
4. **Test Flame Application Device:** A small pilot flame (gas jet) mounted on a swinging arm that allows the flame to pass across the cup surface in approximately **1 second**.
5. **Support Stand & Heat Shield:** To protect the operator and stabilize the apparatus.

**Refer to Section 4.1 for the Mermaid schematic of the apparatus.**

**IS / ASTM Standard:** **IS 1448 (Part 32)** : 1992 (Reaffirmed 2014) — equivalent to **ASTM D92 — 18**.

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Experimental Procedure (Cleveland Open Cup — ASTM D92):**

1. **Cleaning:** Wash the test cup with solvent (e.g., carbon tetrachloride or light naphtha) to remove any oil residue from previous tests. Allow to dry thoroughly.
2. **Sample Filling:** Pour the oil sample up to the **filling mark** inside the cup. Position the cup on the heating plate.
3. **Thermometer Placement:** Insert the thermometer with its bulb immersed in the oil but not touching the cup base.
4. **Initial Heating:** Apply heat at a rate of **14–17 °C per minute** until the temperature reaches approximately **55 °C below the expected flash point**.
5. **Reduced Heating Rate:** Slow the heating rate to **5–6 °C per minute** as the expected flash point approaches.
6. **Flame Application:** Pass the test flame across the cup surface at intervals of **every 2 °C** initially, and **every 1 °C** as the flash point is approached. Each sweep should take about **1 second**.
7. **Flash Point Recording:** The temperature at which a **distinct flash appears on the oil surface** is recorded as the **Flash Point** (Trial 1). Repeat with a fresh sample for Trial 2 and Trial 3.
8. **Fire Point Procedure:** Continue heating at 5–6 °C/min and applying the test flame every 1 °C. The **Fire Point** is the temperature at which the oil vapors ignite and the flame **continues to burn steadily for at least 5 seconds** after the test flame is withdrawn.
9. **Barometric Correction:** Apply the correction $C = 0.03 \times (760 - P_{actual})$ to each recorded temperature.
10. **Report:** Average the corrected values from at least 3 trials and report the **mean corrected Flash Point and Fire Point** along with the Fire-Flash Differential.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam (Module 2 — Flash & Fire Point)**

- **Flash Point** = momentary ignition (flash). **Fire Point** = sustained burning for **≥ 5 seconds**.
- **Open Cup (COC) > Closed Cup (PMCC)** for the same oil — closed cup gives lower value due to vapor confinement.
- **ASTM D92** = Open Cup (Cleveland). **ASTM D93** = Closed Cup (Pensky-Martens). **IS 1448 P-32** = Indian standard equivalent of ASTM D92.
- **Heating rates:** 14–17 °C/min initial → 5–6 °C/min near flash point.
- **Test flame interval:** Every 2 °C initially, every 1 °C near flash point.
- **Fire-Flash Differential $\Delta T$:**
  - Small (< 5 °C) → Highly volatile / hazardous fuel
  - Typical (15–25 °C) → Lubricating oil (good quality)
  - Large (> 30 °C) → Heavy oil / bitumen
- **Barometric correction:**
  $$C = 0.03 \times (760 - P_{actual}) \text{ mm Hg}$$
  $$C = 0.25 \times (101.325 - P_{actual}) \text{ kPa}$$
- **Standard reference pressure:** $760 \text{ mm Hg} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$.
- **Lubricating oils:** High flash point (>200 °C) = safer in hot-running engines.
- **Apparatus to remember:** Cleveland Open Cup, Pensky-Martens Closed Cup, Abel's apparatus (for very volatile fuels).
- **Continuous stirring** is required ONLY in closed cup (PMCC); NOT in open cup (COC).
- **Safety:** Always work under fume hood; keep CO₂ fire extinguisher nearby; turn off gas supply immediately after the test.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Viscosity of Lubricating Oils and Fuels and its Variation with Temperature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL408 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Viscosity of Lubricating Oils and Fuels and its Variation with Temperature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Viscosity** is the fundamental property of a fluid that quantifies its internal resistance to flow (deformation) under an applied shear stress. In the context of thermal engineering, viscosity is a **transport property** and is the single most important physical property governing the hydrodynamic behavior of lubricating oils in journal bearings, pistons, gear meshes, and fuel injection systems.

From a continuum mechanics standpoint, viscosity is defined by **Newton's Law of Viscosity**:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

where:
- $\tau$ = shear stress (N/m² or Pa)
- $\mu$ = dynamic (absolute) viscosity (Pa·s or N·s/m²)
- $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ = velocity gradient perpendicular to flow (s⁻¹), also called the **shear rate** ($\dot{\gamma}$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Newton's Law of Viscosity** — For a Newtonian fluid, the shear stress is directly proportional to the shear rate. The constant of proportionality is the dynamic viscosity $\mu$. Lubricating oils, water, air, and most common fuels approximate Newtonian behavior at low shear rates and moderate temperatures.

There are two principal forms of viscosity relevant to this experiment:

**(a) Dynamic Viscosity (μ)**
- Also called **absolute viscosity**.
- SI Unit: **Pa·s** (Pascal-second) or N·s/m².
- CGS Unit: **Poise (P)**, where $1 \text{ P} = 0.1 \text{ Pa·s}$ and $1 \text{ cP} = 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$.

**(b) Kinematic Viscosity (ν)**
- The ratio of dynamic viscosity to fluid density.
- SI Unit: **m²/s**.
- CGS Unit: **Stoke (St)**, where $1 \text{ St} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ and $1 \text{ cSt} = 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$.

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

where $\rho$ is the density of the fluid (kg/m³).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Saybolt viscometer, the Redwood viscometer, and the Engler viscometer are all **empirical (efflux-type) instruments**. They do not measure viscosity directly — they measure the **time (in seconds) for a fixed volume of fluid to flow through a standardized orifice under gravity at a controlled temperature**. Kinematic viscosity is then back-calculated using empirical equations.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The "Honey vs. Water" Mental Model:**

Imagine tipping a glass of water and a jar of honey at the same angle on a table.
- **Water** flows almost instantly, its layers sliding past one another with negligible resistance → **low viscosity** (≈ 1 cSt at 20 °C).
- **Honey** flows sluggishly over many seconds, each layer dragging its neighbor → **high viscosity** (≈ 10,000 cSt at 20 °C).

> This internal "stickiness" between fluid layers is what viscosity quantifies.

**Temperature Analogy — Why Engine Oil Thins on a Hot Day:**

Think of lubricating oil molecules as tangled chains of paper clips connected by weak intermolecular bonds. At low temperatures, the chains are coiled and stiff, locking into place and resisting motion. As temperature rises, thermal agitation shakes the chains apart, allowing them to slide past each other more easily. Hence, **viscosity decreases sharply with rising temperature** — a critical phenomenon in engine lubrication.

A useful rule of thumb: for mineral oils, viscosity roughly **halves for every 10 °C rise in temperature** in the operating range.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Implication:** Engine oils must retain a minimum viscosity at 100 °C (high-temp protection) while not becoming so thick at 0 °C that they starve the bearings on cold start. This trade-off is quantified by the **Viscosity Index (VI)**, a measure of how little an oil's viscosity changes with temperature.

---

### 1.3 Why This Experiment Matters in Thermal Engineering

1. **Bearing Design (Reynolds Equation):** The minimum film thickness $h_0$ in a journal bearing is directly proportional to $\mu \cdot N \cdot r / P$ where $N$ is speed, $r$ is journal radius, and $P$ is load. Wrong viscosity → metal-to-metal contact → seizure.
2. **SAE Viscosity Grades (e.g., 20W-40, 5W-30):** These are defined almost entirely on kinematic viscosity limits at 40 °C and 100 °C.
3. **Fuel Atomization:** Diesel fuel viscosity governs droplet size in injectors — too viscous, poor atomization; too thin, leakage past plunger.
4. **Pumping Power:** Pressure drop in pipelines scales linearly with $\mu$, so viscosity drives pumping costs in oil refineries.

---

### 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics (Bolded)

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value at 40 °C |
|---|---|---|
| Density of standard oil | $\rho$ | **850 – 950 kg/m³** |
| Dynamic viscosity of water | $\mu_{w}$ | **0.653 cP at 40 °C** |
| Gravity acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Standard efflux volume (Saybolt) | $V$ | **60 mL** |
| Standard orifice diameter (Saybolt) | $d$ | **1.765 mm** |

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Efflux Viscometer — Operating Principle

All efflux viscometers (Saybolt, Redwood, Engler) work on the same principle: **measure the time $t$ (in seconds) for a fixed volume $V$ of fluid to drain through a short, standardized capillary orifice under gravity at temperature $T$**.

The underlying physics is **Hagen-Poiseuille flow** through a short capillary, but because the orifice is short (so entrance/exit losses matter) and because the flow is not strictly laminar for all fluids, the relationship is **empirical**, not purely theoretical.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Fundamental Efflux Equation (Hagen-Poiseuille for a Capillary):**
> For fully developed laminar flow in a circular tube:
> $$Q = \frac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$$
> For a vertical capillary driven by gravity, $\Delta P = \rho g L$, giving:
> $$Q = \frac{\pi R^{4} \rho g}{8 \mu}$$
> Since $Q = V/t$, the kinematic viscosity is:
> $$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{\pi R^{4} g \, t}{8 V L}$$
> In practice, this ideal relationship is **corrected empirically** because of entrance effects, kinetic energy corrections, and non-Newtonian behavior, giving the standard calibration equations used in the lab.

### 2.2 The Saybolt Universal Viscometer (Most Common in Indian Labs)

**Construction:**
- An **oil cup** (capacity ≈ 80 mL) with a polished **brass orifice** of standard diameter **1.765 mm** at its base.
- The oil cup sits inside a **water bath** (or oil bath for high temperatures) equipped with a stirrer and electric heater.
- A **thermometer** measures the oil temperature with $\pm 0.1$ °C precision.
- A **Kohlrausch flask** (60 mL bulb with a graduation mark) collects the outflow.
- A **cork stopper** (or a ball-valve) seals the orifice from below.

**Procedure (Operational Logic in Bulleted Steps):**

1. **Clean the oil cup** thoroughly with solvent (petroleum ether), dry it, and ensure the orifice is free of obstruction.
2. **Pour the oil sample** above the upper gauge mark (so the level is correct when temperature is reached).
3. **Insert the cork** in the orifice.
4. **Heat (or cool) the oil** by circulating the bath fluid until the oil reaches the desired test temperature (commonly 40 °C, 60 °C, 80 °C, 100 °C).
5. **Stir gently** to ensure uniform temperature throughout the oil.
6. **Place the 60 mL Kohlrausch flask** beneath the orifice.
7. **Withdraw the cork** and simultaneously **start the stopwatch**.
8. **Stop the stopwatch** the moment the 60 mL mark is reached in the receiving flask.
9. **Record the time in seconds** as the **Saybolt Universal Seconds (SUS)** at that temperature.
10. **Repeat three times** at each temperature; the readings should agree within ±1 % (or ±1 s, whichever is greater). Take the **mean**.

**Empirical Conversion Equation (Saybolt Universal → Kinematic Viscosity):**

For drainage time $t$ between **32 s and 100 s**:

$$\boxed{\nu \; (\text{cSt}) = 0.00226\, t - \frac{1.95}{t}}$$

For $t > 100$ s (less common, less accurate):

$$\nu \; (\text{cSt}) = 0.00226\, t - \frac{0.80}{t}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** Always state the equation you are using and verify that $t$ is within the valid range (32–100 s). If $t < 32$ s, the orifice must be replaced or the test is invalid.

---

### 2.3 The Redwood Viscometer (Used in Many Kerala Engineering Colleges)

**Construction:**
- Similar to Saybolt, but with a slightly **larger orifice** (diameter ≈ 1.62 mm, length ≈ 10 mm for Redwood No. 1; orifice ≈ 3.8 mm for Redwood No. 2 / Admiralty).
- Standard efflux volume = **50 mL**.
- Bath is filled with water for $T < 95\,^\circ\text{C}$ and with a high-boiling oil for higher $T$.

**Empirical Conversion Equation (Redwood No. 1):**

For $t > 100$ s:

$$\nu \; (\text{cSt}) = 0.0026\, t - \frac{1.72}{t}}$$

For $32 \le t \le 100$ s:

$$\nu \; (\text{cSt}) = 0.0026\, t - \frac{0.50}{t}}$$

> The 50 mL of oil in Redwood is collected and the time $t$ in seconds is reported as **Redwood Seconds (RWs)**.

---

### 2.4 Variation of Viscosity with Temperature — The Walther Equation

Viscosity varies with temperature in a strongly **non-linear** (approximately exponential) manner. The standard reference for plotting is the **ASTM Viscosity-Temperature Chart (D341)**, a special graph paper with logarithmic axes that linearizes the relationship.

The mathematical model behind the ASTM chart is the **Walther Equation** (1928):

$$\boxed{\log_{10}\log_{10}(\nu + 0.7) = A - B \log_{10} T}$$

where:
- $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity in **cSt** (centistokes)
- $T$ = absolute temperature in **Kelvin** ($T = 273.15 + t_{^\circ\text{C}}$)
- $A, B$ = constants specific to each oil, determined from two measured viscosity points

> [!NOTE]
> The "+0.7" inside the double logarithm is a numerical offset (a curve-fitting device) that prevents negative arguments for very low viscosities. Walther's equation is purely empirical but remarkably accurate over wide temperature ranges and is the **foundation of the ASTM D341 chart**.

**Simplified Two-Point Form for Lab Use:**

Given two experimentally determined points $(\nu_1, T_1)$ and $(\nu_2, T_2)$, the constants are:

$$B = \frac{\log_{10}\log_{10}(\nu_1 + 0.7) - \log_{10}\log_{10}(\nu_2 + 0.7})}{\log_{10} T_2 - \log_{10} T_1}$$

$$A = \log_{10}\log_{10}(\nu_1 + 0.7) + B \log_{10} T_1$$

Once $A$ and $B$ are known, viscosity at any third temperature $T_3$ is obtained by:

$$\log_{10}\log_{10}(\nu_3 + 0.7) = A - B \log_{10} T_3$$

---

### 2.5 Viscosity Index (VI) — A Measure of Viscosity-Temperature Quality

The **Viscosity Index (VI)** is an **arbitrary, dimensionless number** (ASTM D2270) that classifies lubricants based on how much their viscosity changes with temperature:
- A **paraffinic (wax-based) oil** with low viscosity change → **VI = 100** (reference).
- A **naphthenic (asphaltic) oil** with high viscosity change → **VI = 0** (reference).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Higher VI = better oil** for multi-temperature operation (engines, hydraulics, gearboxes).

**Calculation Procedure (ASTM D2270, Standard Method):**

1. Measure $\nu$ of test oil at **40 °C** and at **100 °C**.
2. From standard reference tables, find:
   - $L$ = kinematic viscosity at 40 °C of a **0-VI oil** having the same 100 °C viscosity as the test oil.
   - $H$ = kinematic viscosity at 40 °C of a **100-VI oil** having the same 100 °C viscosity as the test oil.
3. Compute:

$$\boxed{\text{VI} = \frac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100}$$

where $U$ is the kinematic viscosity of the test oil at 40 °C.

> If VI > 100 (very common for modern synthetic oils), an extension formula is used:
> $$\text{VI} = \frac{10^{N} - 1}{0.00715} + 100, \quad \text{where } N = \frac{\log_{10} H - \log_{10} U}{\log_{10} Y}$$
> with $Y$ being the kinematic viscosity at 100 °C. (Out of syllabus scope but worth mentioning.)

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Master the equations below — these are the only ones tested in KTU ESE questions on this experiment.**

| # | Equation | Meaning / When to Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | Define kinematic viscosity from dynamic | m²/s |
| 2 | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Newton's law of viscosity | Pa |
| 3 | $\nu = 0.00226\, t - 1.95/t$ | **Saybolt → cSt** ($32 \le t \le 100$ s) | cSt |
| 4 | $\nu = 0.00226\, t - 0.80/t$ | **Saybolt → cSt** ($t > 100$ s) | cSt |
| 5 | $\nu = 0.0026\, t - 0.50/t$ | **Redwood No.1 → cSt** ($32 \le t \le 100$ s) | cSt |
| 6 | $\nu = 0.0026\, t - 1.72/t$ | **Redwood No.1 → cSt** ($t > 100$ s) | cSt |
| 7 | $\log\log(\nu + 0.7) = A - B \log T$ | **Walther equation** (T in Kelvin) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{VI} = (L - U)/(L - H) \times 100$ | Viscosity Index from ASTM D2270 | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\mu = \nu \cdot \rho$ | Convert kinematic to dynamic | Pa·s |
| 10 | $Q = \pi R^4 \rho g / (8 \mu)$ | Hagen-Poiseuille ideal flow (background) | m³/s |

> All logarithms are base 10 in these equations. Viscosity in the Walther equation is in **cSt**, and the additive constant is **0.7 cSt**.

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SAE J300 Viscosity Classification:** Engine oils are graded purely by their kinematic viscosity at 100 °C (e.g., SAE 30: $\nu_{100} \approx 9.3$–12.5 cSt).
- **Hydraulic Fluid Selection:** Industrial hydraulics typically require $\nu_{40} \approx 32$ cSt for vane pumps and $\nu_{40} \approx 46$ cSt for piston pumps.
- **Fuel Injection (Diesel):** $\nu_{40}$ of diesel must be 2.0–4.5 cSt to ensure proper atomization; very high viscosity causes injector coking.
- **Turbine Oils:** Specified with VI $\ge$ 95 to maintain film thickness across operating temperature ranges.
