If the question asks for kcal/m³, divide kJ/m³ by 4.186. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sources of Error & Their Remedies | S.No. | Source of Error | Nature | Effect on CV | Remedy | |-------|-----------------|--------|--------------|--------| | 1 | Heat loss to surroundings | Systematic (negative) | Measured CV is **lower** than actual | Use proper lagging; perform radiation correction | | 2 | Incomplete combustion (yellow flame) | Systematic (negative) | Measured CV is **lower** | Adjust air-fuel ratio to obtain blue flame | | 3 | Heat carried away by hot flue gases (above 25 °C) | Systematic (negative) | Measured CV is **lower** | Ensure outlet flue gas is below room temperature | | 4 | Inlet water temperature fluctuations | Random | Inconsistent $\Delta T$ | Use a constant-head water tank and let flow stabilise | | 5 | Gas meter zero error / leakage | Systematic | Erroneous $V_m$ | Calibrate gas meter; check for leaks with soap solution | | 6 | Condensate not drained (water pools in exchanger) | Systematic (negative) | Heat not transferred to water | Ensure condensate drain is open | | 7 | Thermometer parallax | Random | Misread temperatures | Read thermometers at eye level, use magnifying lens | | 8 | Variations in atmospheric pressure | Random | STP correction error | Record barometric pressure for each trial | ## 3.5 Derivation of Radiation/Heat Loss Correction (Optional, for Higher Marks) If the steady-state $\Delta T$ shows a small drift indicating heat loss, the **radiation correction** $R$ can be applied using: $$R = \frac{(\Delta T_2 - \Delta T_1) \times t}{(\theta_2 - \theta_1)}$$ Where: - $\Delta T_1, \Delta T_2$ = temperature rise at times $\theta_1, \theta_2$ respectively - $t$ = time interval during which rate of cooling is observed (after burner is turned off) The **corrected temperature rise** is: $$\Delta T_{\text{corrected}} = \Delta T_{\text{observed}} + R$$ This corrected value is then used in the master CV formula. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Junker's Gas Calorimeter — Block-Level Functional Architecture The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete functional architecture of a Junker's Gas Calorimeter, showing all major subsystems, their interactions, and the energy flow path from gas to water. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SUP["GAS SUPPLY SUBSYSTEM"] nodeGasCyl["GAS CYLINDER OR CITY GAS LINE"] nodeReg["PRESSURE REGULATOR AND FLOW CONTROL VALVE"] nodeMeter["WET OR DRY GAS METER"] nodeGasCyl --> nodeReg --> nodeMeter end subgraph COMB["COMBUSTION SUBSYSTEM"] nodeBurner["BUNSEN TYPE GAS BURNER"] nodeChamber["CYLINDRICAL COMBUSTION CHAMBER WITH HELICAL COPPER COIL"] nodeFlueOut["FLUE GAS OUTLET TO CONDENSATE TRAP"] nodeBurner --> nodeChamber --> nodeFlueOut end subgraph HX["HEAT EXCHANGER SUBSYSTEM"] nodeCoil["HELICAL COPPER TUBING SURROUNDING FLUE GAS PATH"] nodeCond["CONDENSATE COLLECTION VESSEL"] nodeCoil --> nodeCond end subgraph WAT["WATER CIRCULATION SUBSYSTEM"] nodeHead["CONSTANT HEAD WATER TANK WITH OVERFLOW"] nodeInTh["INLET THERMOMETER T1"] nodeOutTh["OUTLET THERMOMETER T2"] nodeWM["WATER METER OR COLLECTION TANK"] nodeHead --> nodeInTh --> nodeCoil --> nodeOutTh --> nodeWM end subgraph OBS["OBSERVATION AND DATA SUBSYSTEM"] nodeBar["BAROMETER FOR P_atm"] nodeTherm["AMBIENT THERMOMETER FOR t_g"] nodeClock["STOPWATCH FOR TIME t"] end nodeMeter --> nodeBurner nodeChamber -.HEAT TRANSFER.-> nodeCoil nodeBar -.P_atm.-> nodeMeter nodeTherm -.t_g.-> nodeMeter nodeClock -.TIMING.-> nodeWM classDef supply fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 classDef comb fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#ff9900,color:#000 classDef hx fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000 classDef wat fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#009933,color:#000 classDef obs fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#6600cc,color:#000 class nodeGasCyl,nodeReg,nodeMeter supply class nodeBurner,nodeChamber,nodeFlueOut comb class nodeCoil,nodeCond hx class nodeHead,nodeInTh,nodeOutTh,nodeWM wat class nodeBar,nodeTherm,nodeClock obs ``` ## 4.2 Energy Flow — Sankey-like Concept Map ```mermaid flowchart LR A["CHEMICAL ENERGY
IN GAS FUEL
Q_in = Vg x CV"] --> B["HEAT ABSORBED
BY WATER
Q_w = m_w c_w dT"] A --> C["HEAT LOSS
TO SURROUNDINGS
Q_loss"] A --> D["HEAT IN FLUE GAS
IF NOT FULLY COOLED
Q_flue"] A --> E["INCOMPLETE
COMBUSTION LOSS
Q_incomplete"] classDef in fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 classDef out fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000 classDef loss fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 class A in class B out class C,D,E loss ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** > - In the **first diagram**, trace the path: GAS CYLINDER → REGULATOR → METER → BURNER (combustion occurs here) → CHAMBER (heat generated) → COIL (heat transferred to water) → WATER METER (mass measured). > - In the **second diagram**, note that the **IDEAL** case has $Q_{\text{loss}} = Q_{\text{flue}} = Q_{\text{incomplete}} = 0$, making $Q_{\text{in}} = Q_{\text{water}}$. All practical errors increase the right-side losses and reduce the measured CV. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]** ### Q1. Define Higher Calorific Value (HCV) and Lower Calorific Value (LCV) of a gaseous fuel. Why is HCV always greater than LCV? **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **HCV (Higher or Gross Calorific Value):** The total heat liberated when **1 m³ of gaseous fuel** is completely burnt in excess air and the products of combustion are cooled to the original fuel temperature, with the **water vapour formed during combustion condensed back to liquid**. [1 mark] - **LCV (Lower or Net Calorific Value):** The total heat liberated when 1 m³ of gaseous fuel is completely burnt and the products of combustion are cooled to the original fuel temperature, but with the **water vapour allowed to remain in the vapour state** (i.e., the latent heat of condensation is **not** recovered). [1 mark] - **HCV > LCV:** The difference between HCV and LCV is the **latent heat of vaporisation of the water formed from the hydrogen content of the fuel**. Since this latent heat is included in HCV but excluded in LCV, HCV is always numerically greater than LCV. [1 mark] --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model] What is a Junker's gas calorimeter? List its main components. **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Junker's gas calorimeter** is a continuous-flow laboratory apparatus used to determine the **higher calorific value (HCV)** of gaseous fuels by burning a measured volume of the gas and absorbing the released heat in a steady stream of water. [1 mark] **Main Components:** [2 marks — list any 4 of the following] 1. Bunsen-type gas burner 2. Combustion chamber with helical copper heat-exchanger coil 3. Constant-head water supply tank 4. Inlet and outlet water thermometers (T₁ and T₂) 5. Wet or dry gas meter 6. Condensate collection vessel 7. Flue gas outlet 8. Pressure regulator and flow control valve --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > **[KTU ESE Module Examination - Model Paper Pattern]** ### Question Choice A (14 Marks) **Q.A.** **(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a Junker's gas calorimeter for determining the calorific value of a gaseous fuel. State the assumptions made. **[7 marks]** **(b)** In a Junker's gas calorimeter experiment, the following data were observed: | Data | Value | |------|-------| | Volume of gas burned | $V_m = 0.20$ m³ | | Barometric pressure | $P_{\text{atm}} = 750$ mm Hg | | Gas temperature | $t_g = 30$ °C | | Gas meter pressure (gauge) | $P_g = -4$ mm Hg (vacuum) | | Mass of water heated | $m_w = 22$ kg | | Inlet water temperature | $T_1 = 18$ °C | | Outlet water temperature | $T_2 = 30$ °C | Calculate: (i) The Higher Calorific Value of the gas in kJ/m³, and (ii) The Lower Calorific Value, given that the gas contains 22% H₂ by volume (at STP) and the average molecular weight of the gas is 24. **[7 marks]** #### Model Solution for Q.A: **(a) Construction and Working — [7 marks]** **Construction (3.5 marks):** The Junker's gas calorimeter consists of a vertical cylindrical **combustion chamber** (1) made of thin metal, surrounded by a **helical copper tube** (2) through which water flows. A **Bunsen-type gas burner** (3) is fitted at the bottom of the chamber where the gas is admitted. **Cold water** enters from a **constant-head tank** (4) at the bottom of the helical coil, with an **inlet thermometer T₁** (5) measuring its temperature. After absorbing heat, the water exits at the top, with an **outlet thermometer T₂** (6). The **flue gases** pass through the helical passages and exit from the top (7) at a temperature below room temperature, ensuring complete condensation of water vapour. A **condensate trap** (8) collects the condensed water. The entire assembly is enclosed in an **insulating jacket** (9) to minimise heat loss. The volume of gas burned is measured by a **gas meter** (10), and the mass of water heated is collected in a **measuring tank** (11) for a measured time. **Working (3.5 marks):** 1. The gas is admitted to the burner and ignited. Air is drawn in to give complete combustion. 2. A steady flow of water is established through the helical coil from the constant-head tank. 3. The system is allowed to reach **steady state**, indicated by constant readings of T₁ and T₂. 4. Once steady, the volume of gas consumed in a fixed time interval, the mass of water collected, and both temperatures are recorded. 5. The calorific value is calculated from the energy balance equation. **Assumptions:** - No heat loss to surroundings (adiabatic operation). - Combustion is complete. - Specific heat of water is constant at 4.186 kJ/kg·K. - Gas behaves as an ideal gas. **(b) Numerical Solution — [7 marks]** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Determine absolute gas meter pressure and temperature. $$P_m = P_{\text{atm}} + P_g = 750 + (-4) = 746 \text{ mm Hg}$$ $$T_m = 273 + 30 = 303 \text{ K}$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply STP correction. $$V_{\text{stp}} = V_m \times \frac{P_m}{P_{\text{stp}}} \times \frac{T_{\text{stp}}}{T_m}$$ $$V_{\text{stp}} = 0.20 \times \frac{746}{760} \times \frac{273}{303}$$ $$V_{\text{stp}} = 0.20 \times 0.9816 \times 0.9010 = 0.1769 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Calculate temperature rise. $$\Delta T = T_2 - T_1 = 30 - 18 = 12 \text{ K}$$ **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Calculate HCV. $$\text{HCV} = \frac{m_w \times c_w \times \Delta T}{V_{\text{stp}}}$$ $$\text{HCV} = \frac{22 \times 4.186 \times 12}{0.1769}$$ $$\boxed{\text{HCV} = 6247.6 \text{ kJ/m}^3}$$ **Step 5 (2.5 marks):** Calculate LCV. For 100 m³ of gas, volume of H₂ = 22 m³. Mass of H₂ = $\dfrac{22}{22.4} \times 2 = 1.964$ kg Mass of remaining gas = $\dfrac{78}{22.4} \times 24 = 83.571$ kg Total mass = $1.964 + 83.571 = 85.535$ kg Mass % of H₂ = $\dfrac{1.964}{85.535} \times 100 = 2.296\%$ Now, applying the LCV formula: $$\text{LCV} = \text{HCV} - \frac{9H}{100} \times h_{fg}$$ $$\text{LCV} = 6247.6 - \frac{9 \times 2.296}{100} \times 2257$$ $$\text{LCV} = 6247.6 - 0.2066 \times 2257$$ $$\text{LCV} = 6247.6 - 466.4$$ $$\boxed{\text{LCV} = 5781.2 \text{ kJ/m}^3}$$ --- ### Question Choice B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice **Q.B.** **(a)** Explain the principle of a gas calorimeter. Derive the expression for the calorific value of a gaseous fuel in terms of the mass of water heated, the temperature rise, and the volume of gas burned. **[7 marks]** **(b)** A sample of coal gas is tested in a Junker's calorimeter. The following readings are taken: | Data | Value | |------|-------| | Gas meter reading (initial) | 0.000 m³ | | Gas meter reading (final) | 0.100 m³ | | Barometric pressure | 758 mm Hg | | Gas temperature | 25 °C | | Water collected in 4 minutes | 18 kg | | Inlet water temperature | 22 °C | | Outlet water temperature | 34 °C | | Gas meter pressure | 760 mm Hg (atmospheric) | If the gas contains 20% H₂ by volume, calculate: (i) HCV, (ii) LCV. **[7 marks]** #### Model Solution for Q.B: **(a) Principle and Derivation — [7 marks]** **Principle (3 marks):** The Junker's gas calorimeter works on the principle of the **steady-flow energy equation** at constant pressure. A measured volume of gas is burnt completely, and the heat released is transferred continuously to a steady stream of water. By measuring the **mass of water heated, the temperature rise of water, and the volume of gas burned**, the calorific value is calculated by equating the heat released by the gas to the heat absorbed by the water. **Derivation (4 marks):** Let: - $V_g$ = volume of gas (at STP) burned in time $t$ - $m_w$ = mass of water heated in time $t$ - $c_w$ = specific heat of water - $T_1, T_2$ = inlet and outlet water temperatures Heat released by combustion of gas: $$Q_{\text{released}} = V_g \times \text{CV}$$ Heat absorbed by water: $$Q_{\text{absorbed}} = m_w \times c_w \times (T_2 - T_1)$$ Applying energy balance (assuming no heat loss): $$V_g \times \text{CV} = m_w \times c_w \times (T_2 - T_1)$$ $$\boxed{\text{CV} = \frac{m_w \, c_w \, (T_2 - T_1)}{V_g}}$$ **(b) Numerical Solution — [7 marks]** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Volume and conditions. $$V_m = 0.100 - 0.000 = 0.100 \text{ m}^3, \quad P_m = 758 \text{ mm Hg}, \quad T_m = 273 + 25 = 298 \text{ K}$$ $$P_{\text{stp}} = 760 \text{ mm Hg}, \quad T_{\text{stp}} = 273 \text{ K}$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** STP correction. $$V_{\text{stp}} = 0.100 \times \frac{758}{760} \times \frac{273}{298} = 0.100 \times 0.9974 \times 0.9161 = 0.0914 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Temperature rise. $$\Delta T = 34 - 22 = 12 \text{ K}$$ **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** HCV calculation. $$\text{HCV} = \frac{18 \times 4.186 \times 12}{0.0914} = \frac{904.176}{0.0914} = 9892.5 \text{ kJ/m}^3$$ **Step 5 (2.5 marks):** LCV calculation. For 100 m³ of gas: H₂ volume = 20 m³, Other gas volume = 80 m³. Mass of H₂ = $\dfrac{20}{22.4} \times 2 = 1.786$ kg For the coal gas, since molecular weight of remaining gas is not given, assume it is roughly similar to CH₄ (MW = 16) for the typical coal-gas composition: Mass of other gas = $\dfrac{80}{22.4} \times 16 = 57.143$ kg Total mass = $1.786 + 57.143 = 58.929$ kg Mass % of H₂ = $\dfrac{1.786}{58.929} \times 100 = 3.03\%$ $$\text{LCV} = 9892.5 - \frac{9 \times 3.03}{100} \times 2257$$ $$\text{LCV} = 9892.5 - 615.4$$ $$\boxed{\text{LCV} = 9277.1 \text{ kJ/m}^3}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:** > > 1. **Forgetting STP Correction (–2 to –3 marks):** Many students directly substitute $V_m$ (the metered volume) into the CV formula without correcting to STP. This is the **single most common mistake** and the examiner always checks this. **Always perform the STP correction first.** > > 2. **Gauge vs. Absolute Pressure (–1 mark):** Students often use the barometric pressure directly without adding/subtracting the gauge pressure of the gas meter. If the gas meter has a vacuum (negative gauge), students often forget to subtract it, leading to an erroneously high CV. > > 3. **Confusing Mass % vs. Volume % of H₂ (–2 marks):** The HCV–LCV conversion formula uses **mass percentage of hydrogen**, not volume percentage. A blind substitution of volume % directly into the formula will give a **wrong LCV** and lose substantial marks. > > 4. **Using $c_w = 1$ kcal/kg·K without unit consistency (–1 mark):** When the answer is required in kJ/m³, the value $c_w = 4.186$ kJ/kg·K must be used. Mixing $c_w = 1$ kcal/kg·K with kJ units gives a numerically wrong result. > > 5. **Forgetting to subtract gas meter vacuum in the pressure correction (–1 mark):** A gauge vacuum of 4 mm Hg means $P_m = P_{\text{atm}} - 4$, **not** $P_{\text{atm}} + 4$. Sign convention matters! > > 6. **Stating the result in the wrong units (–0.5 mark):** If the question says "in kcal/m³" but the student gives "kJ/m³", marks are deducted. Always check the unit specified in the question. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme** ### A. Core Concepts (Must-Know) - ✅ **Calorific value** = heat released per unit mass (solids/liquids) or **per unit volume at STP** (gases). - ✅ **HCV (Gross CV):** Water of combustion is **condensed**; latent heat **included**. - ✅ **LCV (Net CV):** Water of combustion remains as **vapour**; latent heat **excluded**. - ✅ **HCV > LCV** always; the difference equals the latent heat of water formed from fuel hydrogen. ### B. Apparatus & Components - ✅ **Junker's calorimeter** is the **standard continuous-flow** apparatus for gaseous fuels. - ✅ Key components: **Burner, Combustion chamber, Helical copper coil, Constant-head water tank, Inlet/Outlet thermometers, Gas meter, Condensate trap, Insulating jacket**. - ✅ The helical coil ensures **maximum heat transfer** and cools flue gases **below 25 °C** to ensure complete condensation. ### C. Key Equations (Memorise These!) - ✅ $\text{CV} = \dfrac{m_w \, c_w \, \Delta T}{V_{\text{stp}}}$ (Master equation) - ✅ $V_{\text{stp}} = V_m \times \dfrac{P_m}{760} \times \dfrac{273}{T_m}$ (STP correction) - ✅ $\text{LCV} = \text{HCV} - \dfrac{9H}{100} \times 2257$ (HCV-LCV relation; $h_{fg}$ in kJ/kg) - ✅ $c_w = 4.186$ kJ/kg·K (use this when answer is in kJ; or 1 kcal/kg·K for kcal) - ✅ $h_{fg} = 2257$ kJ/kg (latent heat of vaporisation of water at 100 °C) ### D. Numerical & Conversion Tricks - ✅ Convert **Volume % H₂ to Mass % H₂** using molar mass (H₂ = 2 kg/kmol). - ✅ Use **22.4 m³/kmol** as molar volume at STP. - ✅ Total mass of gas = (Volume fraction of H₂ × 2/22.4) + (Volume fraction of other gases × MW/22.4) per m³. - ✅ Mass % H₂ = (Mass of H₂ / Total mass) × 100. ### E. Sources of Error & Remedies (Viva Voce Favourites) - ✅ **Heat loss to surroundings** → Use lagging. - ✅ **Incomplete combustion** → Adjust air-fuel ratio for blue flame. - ✅ **Hot flue gas exit** → Slow down gas flow rate; ensure outlet temp < room temp. - ✅ **Condensate accumulation** → Open drain valve. - ✅ **Gas meter leakage** → Check with soap solution. - ✅ **Thermometer parallax** → Read at eye level. ### F. Real-World Application Tie-Ins (Impress the Examiner) - ✅ LPG billing is based on **calorific value per standard cubic metre**. - ✅ Adulteration of LPG with inert gases (N₂, CO₂) reduces CV — calorimeter detects this. - ✅ City Gas Distribution (CGD) networks use on-line calorimeters for tariff determination. - ✅ Biogas quality assessment in anaerobic digestion plants. ### G. Typical KTU Viva Voce Questions - ✅ *Why is the copper coil helical and not straight?* → To increase residence time and surface area for heat transfer. - ✅ *Why must the flue gas exit below 25 °C?* → To ensure complete condensation of water vapour (essential for measuring HCV). - ✅ *Why use a constant-head water tank?* → To maintain a constant, steady water flow rate. - ✅ *What happens if the flame is yellow instead of blue?* → Incomplete combustion → lower measured CV. - ✅ *Why is the gas meter at atmospheric pressure, and what if it has a slight vacuum?* → The vacuum must be subtracted from atmospheric pressure to get the absolute meter pressure for the STP correction. ### H. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Pattern Reminder - ✅ **Continuous Evaluation (CE):** Viva voce on this experiment, **5 marks** for procedure understanding, **5 marks** for result accuracy, **5 marks** for viva. - ✅ **University Practical Exam:** Perform the experiment, calculate CV, answer 2 viva questions, **typically 50 marks total** for this lab. - ✅ **Linked Theory (PCT405 / Thermal Engineering-I):** This topic is also covered as a theory question in **Part A or Part B** of the main theory paper, often worth **3 to 7 marks**. > [!NOTE] > **Final Exam Strategy:** Memorise the **master equation**, the **STP correction formula**, and the **HCV–LCV relationship**. Practise at least 3 numerical problems before the exam, including the unit conversion from volume % H₂ to mass % H₂. This is a **high-scoring topic** if you avoid the common pitfalls listed in the warning callout above.

topics.title: Familiarisation of Various Systems and Subsystems of Petrol Engine/MPFI Engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUL408 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Familiarisation of Various Systems and Subsystems of Petrol Engine/MPFI Engine # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Petrol Engine** (also called a Spark-Ignition / S.I. Engine) is an internal combustion engine that operates on the Otto cycle, where a mixture of petrol (gasoline) and air is compressed and ignited by an external spark source. An **MPFI (Multi-Point Fuel Injection) Engine** is an advanced version in which fuel is injected *individually* into the intake port of **each cylinder** by a dedicated injector, replacing the conventional carburetor-based fuel delivery. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The "Familiarisation" experiment is **not a calculation-based experiment**. It is a *cognitive/identification-based lab exercise* where students physically identify, name, locate, and explain the function of every subsystem mounted on the cut-section test rig of an MPFI petrol engine. ## 1.2 Engine as a System: The "Living Organism" Analogy To make the systems instantly memorable, think of the petrol engine as a **human body**: | Engine Subsystem | Human Body Analogy | Function | |---|---|---| | **Fuel Supply System (MPFI)** | Mouth & Stomach (feeding energy food) | Delivers atomized fuel in correct ratio | | **Air Intake System** | Lungs (breathing air) | Supplies filtered air for combustion | | **Ignition System** | Nervous System (electrical spark) | Times and triggers combustion | | **Cooling System** | Skin/Sweat glands (temperature regulation) | Removes excess heat | | **Lubrication System** | Blood circulation (life fluid) | Reduces friction between moving parts | | **Exhaust System** | Excretory system (waste disposal) | Expels burnt gases safely | | **Starting System** | Wake-up alarm (initial impulse) | Cranks the engine to start | | **ECU** | Brain (central controller) | Monitors sensors, controls actuators | > [!NOTE] > **Key Metric to Remember:** A standard 4-stroke petrol engine completes its working cycle in **2 full crank revolutions (720°)** and requires the air-fuel mixture in a stoichiometric ratio of approximately **14.7 : 1** by mass (denoted **λ = 1**, lambda). ## 1.3 Why "Familiarisation" Matters in KTU Lab Exams In the KTU 2024 scheme lab evaluation, the examiner will typically: - Point to a physical component and ask: *"Name this part and state its function."* - Ask you to draw a labelled block diagram of the fuel/ignition/cooling system. - Ask: *"What is the difference between a carburetor and an MPFI system?"* Thus, this experiment tests **CO1 (Knowledge of engine systems)** and **CO2 (Application of identification skills)** of PCAUL408. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Knowledge Sheet ## 2.1 The Master Framework: Nine Subsystems of an MPFI Petrol Engine Every modern MPFI engine can be decomposed into **nine functional subsystems**. Mastering this taxonomy is the single highest-yield action for your lab record and viva. ### Subsystem 1: Fuel Supply System (MPFI) - **Components:** Fuel tank, fuel pump (electric, in-tank), fuel filter, fuel rail (common rail), fuel pressure regulator, individual injectors (one per cylinder), fuel lines (high-pressure). - **Working Logic:** 1. Electric fuel pump draws fuel from tank. 2. Filter removes contaminants. 3. Fuel rail maintains constant pressure (~ **3.0 – 3.5 bar** above manifold pressure). 4. ECU energizes the injector solenoid for a precise *injection duration* (in milliseconds). 5. Fuel is sprayed *just upstream of the intake valve*, atomizing finely. - **Why MPFI is superior to carburetion:** - Better fuel atomization → complete combustion. - Individual cylinder metering → optimized AFR per cylinder. - Eliminates fuel wetting of manifold walls (no cold-start hesitation). ### Subsystem 2: Air Intake System - **Components:** Air filter (paper/foam element), intake manifold (plenum + runners), throttle body (butterfly valve), Idle Air Control Valve (IACV). - **Function:** Delivers filtered, metered air. The throttle valve (foot-pedal controlled) regulates airflow rate, which is the *primary* load control input. ### Subsystem 3: Ignition System - **Type:** Distributor-less Ignition System (DLI) – standard in MPFI engines. - **Components:** Battery (12 V), ignition coil (per cylinder or paired), crankshaft position sensor (CKP), camshaft position sensor (CMP), spark plug, ECU. - **Working Logic:** 1. CKP sensor detects piston position → sends to ECU. 2. ECU calculates **ignition advance angle** (typically 5°–35° BTDC depending on RPM/load). 3. Coil generates high voltage (**20,000 – 40,000 V**). 4. Spark jumps plug gap, igniting compressed mixture. - **Critical Concept:** Ignition timing is **not fixed**; it is dynamically calculated from a 3D **Ignition Advance Map** stored in ECU ROM. ### Subsystem 4: Cooling System - **Type:** Liquid (water) cooling – closed-loop with thermostat. - **Components:** Water jacket (around cylinders), water pump (belt-driven), radiator, cooling fan (electric or mechanical), thermostat valve, expansion tank, coolant (ethylene glycol + water mix). - **Operating Temp:** Thermostat opens at **~88 °C – 92 °C**, maintaining optimal engine temperature. ### Subsystem 5: Lubrication System - **Type:** Wet-sump, pressure-fed. - **Components:** Oil pan (sump), oil pump (gear/rotor type), oil filter, oil galleries, pressure relief valve, dipstick, warning light. - **Typical Oil Pressure:** **2.5 – 4.5 bar** at operating RPM (idle: ~1.0 bar). ### Subsystem 6: Exhaust System - **Components:** Exhaust manifold, catalytic converter (TWC – Three-Way Catalyst), muffler (silencer), oxygen sensor (Lambda sensor, before and after cat). - **Function:** Routes burnt gases, reduces emissions (CO, HC, NOx) via catalytic action, dampens noise. ### Subsystem 7: Starting System - **Components:** Starter motor (DC series motor), starter solenoid, ignition switch, battery. - **Working:** Turning the key energizes the solenoid, which pushes the *Bendix drive* gear to mesh with the flywheel ring gear, cranking the engine at **~200–300 RPM** until combustion sustains rotation. ### Subsystem 8: Power Transmission / Auxiliary - **Components:** Flywheel, clutch (manual) / torque converter (auto), gearbox, driveshaft, differential. - **Function:** Transmits engine torque to wheels with gear-ratio multiplication. ### Subsystem 9: Electronic Control Unit (ECU) - **Function:** The *brain* — receives inputs from ~20+ sensors (MAP, MAF, TPS, CKP, CMP, O2, IAT, CLT, knock sensor), runs algorithms from ROM/Flash memory, and controls actuators (injectors, ignition coil, fuel pump relay, IACV). ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet (Most Asked in Viva) | Feature | Carburetor Engine | MPFI Engine | |---|---|---| | Fuel delivery | Single point (venturi suction) | One injector per cylinder | | Mixing point | Intake manifold | At intake port (near valve) | | Air-fuel ratio control | Mechanical (jets) | Electronic (ECU) | | Cold-start behavior | Choke required | ECU enriches pulse width | | Fuel efficiency | Lower (~12–14 kmpl) | Higher (~16–20 kmpl) | | Emission control | Poor | Excellent (with cat + O2 sensor) | | Altitude compensation | Manual jet change | Automatic (MAP sensor) | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Spec Sheet | Parameter | Typical Value / Formula | Unit | |---|---|---| | Stoichiometric AFR (petrol) | 14.7 : 1 | mass ratio | | Compression ratio (modern petrol) | 9 : 1 to 12 : 1 | — | | Firing order (4-cyl inline) | 1-3-4-2 | — | | Crank revolutions per cycle | 2 (= 720°) | rev / cycle | | Idle speed | 700 – 900 | RPM | | Fuel rail pressure | 3.0 – 3.5 (multi-point) | bar | | Spark plug gap | 0.8 – 1.1 | mm | | Ignition coil secondary voltage | 20,000 – 40,000 | V | | Thermostat opening temperature | 88 – 92 | °C | | Oil pressure (warm idle) | ≥ 1.0 | bar | | Oil pressure (2000 RPM) | 2.5 – 4.5 | bar | | Otto cycle thermal efficiency | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$ | dimensionless | | Indicated Power (4-stroke) | $IP = \dfrac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n}{2}$ | kW | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world Engineering Utility:** Understanding these subsystems is foundational for *automotive diagnostics*, *engine calibration*, *emission testing (BS-VI norms in India)*, and *designing hybrid powertrains*. The MPFI system is a direct ancestor of modern GDI (Gasoline Direct Injection) engines. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough: MPFI Fuel Injection Sequence ## 3.1 Derivation of Injection Pulse Width Logic The ECU calculates **injector ON-time (pulse width, $t_i$)** using a base map and several correction factors. This is the *core algorithm* of the MPFI system and a frequent viva question. ### Step 1: Base Pulse Width from 3D Map The ECU reads the base injection time $t_b$ (in ms) from a lookup table indexed by: - **Engine speed $N$** (RPM) - **Air mass per cycle $m_a$** (kg/cycle), derived from MAP sensor. ### Step 2: Apply Target AFR Factor Target stoichiometric AFR = **14.7**. For richer mixtures (cold start, WOT), the ECU multiplies by an enrichment factor $K_{afr}$: $$ t_{afr} = t_b \cdot \frac{14.7}{AFR_{target}} $$ ### Step 3: Apply Coolant Temperature Correction $K_{clt}$ Cold engine → richer mixture required. $K_{clt}$ ranges from **1.5** (at -20 °C) down to **1.0** (at 80 °C). ### Step 4: Apply Battery Voltage Compensation $K_{bat}$ Inductor (solenoid) opening time is voltage-dependent. At low voltage (e.g., 11 V), pulse width is extended to compensate for slow injector opening. ### Step 5: Final Pulse Width Equation $$ t_i = t_b \cdot K_{afr} \cdot K_{clt} \cdot K_{bat} \cdot K_{start} $$ where $K_{start}$ is the cranking enrichment factor (typically 1.5 – 2.0 during starting). ### Step 6: Fuel Mass Injected per Cycle Once $t_i$ is known, the mass of fuel injected is: $$ m_f = \dot{m}_{inj} \cdot t_i $$ where $\dot{m}_{inj}$ is the *static flow rate* of the injector (e.g., **200 cc/min at 3 bar**). > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Insight:** The ECU performs this calculation **for every cylinder, every cycle, 25–50 times per second**. It is essentially a *real-time optimal-control problem* — and it's been doing it since the 1980s on cars like the Bosch L-Jetronic systems. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style) > **Problem:** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke MPFI petrol engine runs at 3000 RPM. The MAP sensor reads 0.6 bar (absolute), intake temperature is 30 °C, volumetric efficiency is 85%, and the engine displaces 1.2 L. Calculate (a) the air mass per cycle per cylinder, and (b) the stoichiometric fuel mass per cycle for the whole engine. *(Take R = 0.287 kJ/kg·K, $\gamma = 1.4$.)* ### Part (a): Air Mass per Cycle per Cylinder **Step 1 — Volume per cylinder:** $$ V_c = \frac{V_d}{n} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{4} = 3.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step 2 — Apply volumetric efficiency:** $$ V_{eff} = \eta_v \cdot V_c = 0.85 \times 3.0 \times 10^{-4} = 2.55 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step 3 — Use ideal gas law $pV = mRT$:** $$ m_{a,cyl} = \frac{p \cdot V_{eff}}{R \cdot T} $$ Convert units: $p = 0.6 \times 10^5$ Pa, $T = 303$ K, $R = 287$ J/kg·K. $$ m_{a,cyl} = \frac{0.6 \times 10^5 \times 2.55 \times 10^{-4}}{287 \times 303} $$ **Step 4 — Numerator evaluation:** $$ 0.6 \times 10^5 \times 2.55 \times 10^{-4} = 15.3 \text{ (Pa·m}^3 \text{)} $$ **Step 5 — Denominator evaluation:** $$ 287 \times 303 = 86{,}961 $$ **Step 6 — Final division:** $$ m_{a,cyl} = \frac{15.3}{86{,}961} = 1.76 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg} = 0.176 \text{ g/cycle/cyl} $$ ### Part (b): Stoichiometric Fuel Mass (Whole Engine) **Step 7 — Air mass per cycle (all 4 cylinders):** $$ m_{a,tot} = 4 \times 1.76 \times 10^{-4} = 7.04 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/cycle} $$ **Step 8 — Apply stoichiometric AFR = 14.7:** $$ m_f = \frac{m_{a,tot}}{14.7} = \frac{7.04 \times 10^{-4}}{14.7} = 4.79 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/cycle} $$ $$ \boxed{m_f \approx 47.9 \text{ mg per engine cycle (all 4 cylinders)}} $$ **Step 9 — Convert to hourly fuel rate:** Cycles per second = N/2 for 4-stroke = 3000/2/60 = 25 cycles/s. $$ \dot{m}_f = 4.79 \times 10^{-5} \times 25 = 1.198 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s} = 4.31 \text{ kg/hr} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Always explicitly state the assumption *stoichiometric combustion ($\lambda = 1$)*. Omitting this is a common reason for 1-mark loss in 3-mark numericals. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Block Diagram: MPFI Engine System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Driver Input
Accelerator Pedal] --> B[Throttle Body
TPS Sensor] B --> C[Intake Manifold
Plenum] C --> D1[Injector Cyl 1] C --> D2[Injector Cyl 2] C --> D3[Injector Cyl 3] C --> D4[Injector Cyl 4] E[Fuel Tank] --> F[Electric Fuel Pump] F --> G[Fuel Filter] G --> H[Fuel Rail
3.0 to 3.5 bar] H --> D1 H --> D2 H --> D3 H --> D4 H --> I[Fuel Pressure
Regulator] D1 --> J1[Cylinder 1
Combustion] D2 --> J2[Cylinder 2
Combustion] D3 --> J3[Cylinder 3
Combustion] D4 --> J4[Cylinder 4
Combustion] K[Battery 12V] --> L[Ignition Coil] L --> M1[Spark Plug 1] L --> M2[Spark Plug 2] L --> M3[Spark Plug 3] L --> M4[Spark Plug 4] J1 --> N[Exhaust Manifold] J2 --> N J3 --> N J4 --> N N --> O[Catalytic Converter
TWC] O --> P[Muffler] Q[ECU Brain] --> D1 Q --> D2 Q --> D3 Q --> D4 Q --> L Q --> F R[CKP Sensor] --> Q S[O2 Lambda Sensor] --> Q T[Coolant Temp Sensor] --> Q U[Knock Sensor] --> Q ``` ## 4.2 Subsystem Interaction Map ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph S1[Fuel Supply] FT[Fuel Tank] --> FP[Pump] --> FF[Filter] --> FR[Common Rail] --> INJ[Injectors] end subgraph S2[Air Intake] AF[Air Filter] --> TB[Throttle Body] --> IM[Intake Manifold] end subgraph S3[Combustion Core] INJ --> CV[Intake Valve] IM --> CV CV --> CY[Cylinder] SP[Spark Plug] --> CY CY --> EV[Exhaust Valve] end subgraph S4[Exhaust] EV --> EM[Exhaust Manifold] --> CAT[Cat Converter] --> MU[Muffler] end subgraph S5[Cooling Loop] WP[Water Pump] --> WJ[Water Jacket] --> RA[Radiator] --> TH[Thermostat] --> WP end subgraph S6[Lubrication] OP[Oil Pump] --> OF[Oil Filter] --> OG[Oil Galleries] --> EN[Engine Parts] --> SB[Sump] --> OP end CY --> WJ CY --> OG ``` ## 4.3 ECU Closed-Loop Control Logic ```mermaid flowchart TB A[MAP Sensor
Manifold Pressure] --> ECU B[IAT Sensor
Intake Air Temp] --> ECU C[CLT Sensor
Coolant Temp] --> ECU D[TPS Sensor
Throttle Position] --> ECU E[CKP Sensor
Crank Angle] --> ECU F[CMP Sensor
Cam Position] --> ECU G[O2 Sensor
Lambda] --> ECU H[Knock Sensor] --> ECU ECU[ECU Central Control Unit] --> OUT1[Fuel Injector Pulse Width] ECU --> OUT2[Ignition Coil Dwell Time] ECU --> OUT3[Idle Air Control Valve] ECU --> OUT4[Fuel Pump Relay] ECU --> OUT5[Evaporative Purge Valve] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Viva:** When asked to draw the MPFI system, always start with the **fuel path (tank → pump → filter → rail → injectors)** and the **air path (filter → throttle → manifold → valve → cylinder)** as two converging streams. Label pressures, sensor positions, and the ECU as a central hub. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **List the major subsystems of an MPFI petrol engine and state the primary function of each in one line.** **Model Answer:** | # | Subsystem | Primary Function | |---|---|---| | 1 | Fuel Supply (MPFI) | Delivers metered atomized fuel into intake port of each cylinder | | 2 | Air Intake | Supplies filtered, metered air to cylinders via throttle | | 3 | Ignition | Generates timed high-voltage spark to ignite mixture | | 4 | Cooling | Removes excess combustion heat to maintain ~90 °C | | 5 | Lubrication | Reduces friction and wear between moving parts | | 6 | Exhaust | Expels burnt gases and reduces emissions | | 7 | Starting | Cranks engine via starter motor until self-sustaining | | 8 | Power Transmission | Transmits engine torque to drive wheels | | 9 | ECU | Central electronic control of injectors, ignition, pumps | ### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Differentiate between a carburetor and an MPFI fuel injection system. (Any 3 points)** **Model Answer:** 1. **Delivery Point:** Carburetor uses a single venturi at the manifold entry; MPFI uses one injector *per cylinder* at the intake port. 2. **Control Method:** Carburetor is purely mechanical (float, jets); MPFI is electronically controlled by the ECU via pulse-width modulation. 3. **Fuel Atomization:** MPFI gives finer atomization since injection occurs just above the intake valve, improving cold-start and reducing wall wetting. 4. **Emissions:** MPFI combined with a 3-way catalyst and oxygen sensor meets BS-VI norms; carbureted engines cannot. 5. **Maintenance:** Carburetor requires periodic tuning/idle adjustment; MPFI is self-calibrating and largely maintenance-free. --- ## Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper] **(a)** With the help of a neat labelled block diagram, explain the working of the **MPFI fuel supply system** of a petrol engine. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the **distributor-less ignition system (DLI)** used in modern MPFI engines. Discuss the role of the crankshaft position sensor and ignition advance. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): The MPFI fuel supply system consists of the following components working in sequence: 1. **Fuel Tank:** Stores petrol. Has a *vent* and an in-tank electric fuel pump immersed in fuel. 2. **Electric Fuel Pump (Roller-vane type):** Delivers fuel at **3.0–3.5 bar**. It is energized by the ECU via a fuel pump relay. 3. **Fuel Filter:** Pleated paper element; replaces particulates ≥ 10 µm. 4. **Fuel Lines (High-Pressure Hose):** Reinforced rubber or nylon to handle pressure pulses. 5. **Fuel Rail (Common Rail):** A tubular manifold that distributes fuel equally to all injectors; acts as a small accumulator dampening pressure spikes. 6. **Fuel Pressure Regulator:** Diaphragm-type; maintains constant pressure *differential* across injector tip. Excess fuel returns to tank via return line. 7. **Injectors (Solenoid type):** One per cylinder. The ECU energizes the solenoid, lifting a needle valve; fuel sprays in a *fine cone* through 4–12 holes. 8. **ECU:** Calculates injection timing (synchronized with intake stroke) and pulse width (typically 2–10 ms at idle). **Working Sequence (Cycle):** - ECU receives CKP signal → identifies which cylinder is on intake stroke. - ECU sends a 12 V pulse of calculated width to the corresponding injector. - Injector opens, spraying fuel onto the back of the closed intake valve (forming a *puddle* that vaporizes when the valve opens). - Unused cylinder injectors remain closed. > **Block Diagram (Must be drawn in exam):** > Tank → Pump → Filter → Rail → [Injector 1, Injector 2, Injector 3, Injector 4] → Pressure Regulator → Return to Tank > ECU controls: Pump Relay + 4 Injector Coils > Sensors feeding ECU: CKP, MAP, O2, CLT, TPS #### Model Solution for (b): The **Distributor-Less Ignition System (DLI)** eliminates the mechanical distributor, replacing it with *electronic triggering* and *individual coils* (either one coil per cylinder, called **COP – Coil on Plug**, or a wasted-spark paired coil). **Working Steps:** 1. **Crankshaft Position (CKP) Sensor:** A magnetic pickup (reluctor) or Hall-effect sensor near the timing ring on the crankshaft. It generates a square-wave pulse for every tooth; a missing tooth identifies TDC reference. 2. **Camshaft Position (CMP) Sensor:** Identifies the compression stroke vs. exhaust stroke (since CKP alone cannot distinguish the two in a 4-stroke). 3. **ECU Computation:** Using RPM (from CKP frequency) and load (from MAP), the ECU looks up the **ignition advance** from a 3D map (Advance vs. RPM × MAP). 4. **Dwell Time Control:** The ECU pre-energizes the coil for a calculated *dwell* (typically 2–4 ms) to build up magnetic flux. 5. **Spark Discharge:** When dwell ends, the ECU collapses the magnetic field; the coil secondary induces **20,000–40,000 V** across the spark plug gap, ionizing the air-fuel mixture. **Why Ignition Advance is Needed:** The mixture does not burn instantaneously; it takes ~3 ms. To have peak pressure occur **~12°–15° ATDC** (for maximum work), the spark must fire *before* TDC. The advance angle depends on: - Higher RPM → more advance (less time available). - Higher load → less advance (slower flame speed in denser mixture). - Knock detected → retard timing immediately. **Dynamic Adjustment:** Closed-loop *knock control* uses a piezo knock sensor on the block. If knocking is detected, the ECU retards spark by 2°–5° in 1° steps until knock disappears. ### Question B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper – Alternative] **(a)** Explain the **water cooling system** of a petrol engine with a neat sketch. What is the role of the thermostat and why is it critical for engine efficiency? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Describe the **wet-sump lubrication system**. What are the typical oil pressure values at idle and at 2000 RPM? What happens if oil pressure drops below the safe limit? **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): The water cooling system is a *closed-loop, pressurized liquid circuit*: **Components:** - **Water Jacket:** Cavities cast into the cylinder head and block surrounding the combustion chamber. - **Water Pump:** Centrifugal type, belt-driven off the crankshaft; circulates coolant. - **Thermostat:** Wax-pellet type valve located at the engine outlet. **Opens at 88 °C – 92 °C.** - **Radiator:** Aluminum core with tubes and fins; heat exchange with ambient air. - **Cooling Fan:** Electric (ECU-controlled) or viscous (temperature-sensed). - **Expansion Tank (Reserve Tank):** Allows for thermal expansion; maintains system pressure. - **Radiator Cap:** Holds ~1.0 bar pressure, raising coolant boiling point to ~110 °C. **Coolant Flow Paths:** - **Cold engine (thermostat closed):** Coolant circulates only through the *bypass loop* around the intake manifold → engine warms up rapidly. - **Hot engine (thermostat open):** Coolant flows from engine → thermostat → radiator top tank → down through tubes (cooled by fan-induced airflow) → bottom tank → water pump → engine jacket. **Why the Thermostat is Critical:** 1. **Rapid Warm-Up:** Forces closed-loop circulation, bringing engine to operating temperature (90 °C) in < 5 minutes. 2. **Optimal Combustion:** ECU's fuel maps and ignition advance are calibrated for 90 °C; off-design temps cause rich mixture and retarded timing. 3. **Emission Control:** Catalytic converter needs ~400 °C; cold engines produce high HC/CO. 4. **Fuel Economy:** Cold engine → 15–20% higher fuel consumption. 5. **Prevents Overcooling:** Without thermostat, the engine may run too cold → incomplete fuel vaporization → oil contamination (fuel dilutes oil). #### Model Solution for (b): **Wet-Sump Lubrication System:** - **Oil Sump (Pan):** Reservoir at the engine bottom, holding **3.5 – 5.0 L** of oil. - **Oil Pump:** Gear-type or rotor-type, mechanically driven off the crankshaft. Typical delivery: **30 – 60 L/min** at 3000 RPM. - **Oil Strainer (Pickup):** Coarse mesh at sump bottom; first-stage filtration. - **Oil Filter:** Replaceable spin-on cartridge; removes particles ≥ 25 µm; contains anti-drainback valve. - **Pressure Relief Valve:** Spring-loaded; opens at ~4.5 bar to bypass oil to sump if filter clogs. - **Oil Galleries:** Internal drillings in block/head feeding main bearings, camshaft, valve train. - **Dipstick:** Manual level check. - **Oil Pressure Warning Light/Switch:** Activates if pressure falls below **0.5 bar** at idle. **Pressure Specifications:** | Condition | Oil Pressure (typical) | |---|---| | Cold start (idle) | 1.5 – 2.5 bar | | Warm idle | 1.0 – 1.5 bar | | 2000 RPM (warm) | 2.5 – 4.5 bar | | Max safe continuous | 5.0 bar | **Consequences of Low Oil Pressure:** - Insufficient hydrodynamic film → metal-to-metal contact in bearings. - **Main bearing failure** → catastrophic engine seizure. - Cam lobe wear → valve timing loss. - Piston scuffing → cylinder wall damage. - The warning light must *never* be ignored; KTU viva: *"What is the first thing you check if the oil light comes on?"* → "Stop the engine immediately and check oil level with dipstick." > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing MPFI with Direct Injection (GDI):** MPFI injects into the *port*; GDI injects *directly* into the cylinder. Examiners will deduct 2–3 marks for this confusion. > 2. **Forgetting to mention ECU:** In the MPFI working, students often omit the ECU's role. Always mention "ECU calculates and energizes the injector solenoid." > 3. **Mixing up 2-stroke and 4-stroke firing:** A 4-stroke engine fires once every **2 revolutions**, not 1. Saying "1 revolution" loses 1 mark. > 4. **Wrong spark plug voltage range:** Saying "12 V spark" is a major error. The coil steps up to **20,000–40,000 V**. > 5. **Confusing WET sump with DRY sump:** Almost all road cars use wet sump; dry sump is for racing/4WD. > 6. **No units in numericals:** KTU examiners *strictly* deduct 0.5 mark for missing units. > 7. **Drawing sketches without labels:** A block diagram without labels gets only 50% marks. Always label *every* component and arrow. --- ## 5. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] Petrol engine = Spark-Ignition (S.I.) engine operating on the **Otto cycle**. - [x] MPFI = **Multi-Point Fuel Injection** — one injector *per cylinder* at the intake port. - [x] Stoichiometric AFR for petrol = **14.7 : 1** (λ = 1). - [x] 4-stroke cycle completes in **720°** (2 crank revolutions). - [x] Firing order for typical 4-cyl inline: **1-3-4-2**. - [x] Fuel rail pressure in MPFI: **3.0 – 3.5 bar** above manifold. - [x] Spark plug secondary voltage: **20,000 – 40,000 V**. - [x] Spark plug gap: **0.8 – 1.1 mm**. - [x] Thermostat opening temp: **88 – 92 °C**. - [x] Engine idle speed: **700 – 900 RPM**. - [x] Oil pressure (warm, 2000 RPM): **2.5 – 4.5 bar**. - [x] Compression ratio (modern petrol): **9 : 1 to 12 : 1**. - [x] **Nine subsystems** to remember: Fuel, Air, Ignition, Cooling, Lubrication, Exhaust, Starting, Transmission, **ECU**. - [x] ECU is the *brain*; sensors are *eyes/ears*; injectors/ignition coils are *muscles*. - [x] DLI (Distributor-Less Ignition) → uses **CKP + CMP** sensors, no moving distributor. - [x] **Knock sensor** → retards timing if detonation detected. - [x] Catalytic converter (TWC) → reduces **CO, HC, NOx** simultaneously. - [x] Wet sump is standard; dry sump is for motorsport only. - [x] **Lab Record Must-Haves:** (i) Block diagram of MPFI fuel system, (ii) Block diagram of DLI ignition, (iii) Labelled sketch of cooling system, (iv) Component identification table, (v) Comparison table: Carburetor vs MPFI. - [x] **Viva Gold Sentences:** - "MPFI improves fuel atomization by injecting fuel at the intake port, not in the manifold." - "ECU uses a closed-loop lambda control to keep AFR near stoichiometric." - "Thermostat ensures rapid warm-up for optimum efficiency and emissions." - "Knock sensor protects the engine from detonation by retarding spark advance." > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU 2024 Lab Tip:** When you enter the lab exam, *first* spend 2 minutes observing the cut-section engine. Mentally trace the **fuel path** and the **air path** until they meet at the **intake valve**. This single mental map will let you answer 80% of component-identification questions confidently.

topics.title: Familiarisation of Various Systems and Parts of Diesel Engine/Turbocharged Engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL408 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarisation of Various Systems and Parts of Diesel Engine/Turbocharged Engine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Diesel Engine (CI Engine)** is an internal combustion heat engine that operates on the **Diesel Cycle** (a modified constant-pressure heat addition cycle), in which ignition of the fuel charge is achieved by the high temperature of the compressed air charge, without the need for an external ignition source. The fuel, typically **High-Speed Diesel (HSD)**, is injected directly into the combustion chamber at or near the end of the compression stroke.

A **Turbocharged Engine** is a reciprocating internal combustion engine equipped with a **Turbocharger (TC)** — a centrifugal compressor-turbine assembly driven by the kinetic and thermal energy of the exhaust gases — to force a higher mass of intake air (above atmospheric density) into the combustion chamber, thereby increasing the **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)** efficiency and the **Brake Mean Effective Pressure (BMEP)** output of the engine.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment is the foundational lab exercise under *Module Experiment 2*. Students must be able to *physically identify*, *name*, and *describe the function* of every major subassembly of a 4-stroke, multi-cylinder, water-cooled diesel engine, both in its naturally aspirated (NA) and turbocharged (TC) configurations.

## 1.2 Working Principle — Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Lungs of a Human Body"**
> 
> Think of a diesel engine as a hardworking human body:
> - The **Cylinder Block** is the **chest cavity** (where the heart of the action happens).
> - The **Piston** is the **diaphragm**, pumping rhythmically up and down.
> - The **Fuel Injector** is the **mouth** that feeds controlled food (fuel) at the precise moment needed.
> - The **Turbocharger** is like a **second pair of lungs** that has been trained by an athlete — it forces more oxygen (air) into the chest than a normal breath would allow, letting the body work much harder and longer without fatigue.
> - The **Cooling System** is the body's **sweat glands and blood circulation**, removing excess heat to keep temperature steady.
> - The **Lubrication System** is the **synovial fluid** in joints, ensuring every moving part slides effortlessly without grinding.
> - The **Governing System** is the **brain's motor cortex**, deciding exactly how much fuel to feed based on how hard the accelerator pedal (load) is pressed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction — NA vs TC Engine**
> 
> - **Naturally Aspirated (NA) Engine:** Air enters the cylinder purely due to the *vacuum* created by the descending piston. Density ≈ 1.2 kg/m³ (at sea level).
> - **Turbocharged (TC) Engine:** A turbine-compressor unit driven by exhaust energy *forces* air into the cylinder. Density can be **1.8 to 2.5 kg/m³**, increasing power output by **30% to 50%** for the same engine displacement.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Engine Metrics

The following constants and metrics are essential for the KTU lab viva and record writing:

- **Compression Ratio of Diesel Engine:** $r = 14$ to $22$ (typically around $17.5$ for medium-duty CI engines).
- **Air-Fuel Ratio (by mass):** $\text{AFR} \approx 18:1$ to $25:1$ (stoichiometric ≈ 14.5:1; diesel runs **lean**).
- **Ignition Temperature of Diesel:** $\approx 250^\circ\text{C}$ to $350^\circ\text{C}$ (achieved by adiabatic compression).
- **Density of standard intake air:** $\rho_{air} \approx 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at **NTP (Normal Temperature and Pressure: 25°C, 1 atm)**.
- **Turbocharger typical boost pressure:** $0.5$ to $1.5$ bar (gauge) above atmospheric.
- **Coolant (water + Ethylene Glycol mix) ratio:** typically **50:50** for corrosion protection.
- **Engine oil (SAE grade):** typically **15W-40** for tropical conditions like Kerala.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Engine Systems

A modern multi-cylinder diesel engine is composed of **seven (7) distinct functional systems**, each performing a specific task. Understanding these systems is the *primary learning outcome* of this KTU lab experiment.

> [!NOTE]
> The mnemonic **"F-A-E-C-L-S-G"** helps recall the seven systems: **F**uel, **A**ir Intake & Exhaust, **E**lectrical & Starting, **C**ooling, **L**ubrication, **S**afety & Governing.

### 2.1.1 System 1 — Fuel Supply System (FSS)

**Function:** To deliver clean, metered, and high-pressure diesel fuel to each cylinder at the precise moment of injection.

**Major Components & Their Roles:**

| S.No | Component | Function | Typical Specification |
|:----:|:----------|:---------|:----------------------|
| 1 | Diesel Fuel Tank | Storage of fuel, sediment settling | Capacity 50–500 L |
| 2 | Fuel Lift Pump (Feed Pump) | Transfers fuel from tank to injection pump | Mechanical, diaphragm type |
| 3 | Fuel Filters (Primary & Secondary) | Remove dust, water, particulates | 10 µm (primary) & 5 µm (secondary) |
| 4 | Injection Pump (FIE Pump) | Generates high pressure for injection | 200–1100 bar (common rail) |
| 5 | High-Pressure Pipes | Carry fuel from pump to injectors | Hardened steel, Ø 6 mm |
| 6 | Fuel Injector | Atomises and injects fuel into cylinder | 5–7 spray holes, multi-hole type |
| 7 | Return Line & Overflow Valve | Returns excess fuel to tank | Maintains rail pressure |

**Two Subsystems to Know:**
- **Conventional (Inline / Rotary) FIE Pump System:** Uses a jerk pump, plunger-barrel, and delivery valve. Used in older Kirloskar/Cummins engines.
- **Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDi):** A high-pressure accumulator (rail) feeds electronically controlled injectors. Used in modern BS-VI/Euro-VI engines.

### 2.1.2 System 2 — Air Intake & Exhaust System

**Function:** To supply clean intake air and expel combustion gases efficiently with minimum back-pressure.

| S.No | Component | Function |
|:----:|:----------|:---------|
| 1 | Air Cleaner (Dry/Wet type) | Removes dust (efficiency > 99.5%) |
| 2 | Intake Manifold | Distributes air to all cylinders |
| 3 | Turbocharger Compressor | Pressurises intake air |
| 4 | Intercooler (Charge Cooler) | Cools compressed air, increasing density |
| 5 | Intake Valve (Poppet type) | Allows air into cylinder during suction stroke |
| 6 | Exhaust Manifold | Collects exhaust from cylinders |
| 7 | Exhaust Valve (Poppet type) | Releases burnt gases |
| 8 | Silencer / Muffler | Dampens noise (typically 25–35 dB reduction) |
| 9 | Catalytic Converter / DPF | Reduces NOx, CO, HC, PM emissions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Viva Favourite Question:** *"What is the function of the Intercooler?"*
> 
> **Answer:** The intercooler is a heat exchanger placed between the turbocharger compressor outlet and the engine intake manifold. It cools the compressed charge air (which is heated during compression, often up to 150–180°C). Cooling the air **increases its density** (per the ideal gas law), allowing more oxygen mass to enter the cylinder, which directly translates into more power and reduced knock tendency. Cooling effectiveness is typically **60–75%**.

### 2.1.3 System 3 — Cooling System

**Function:** To maintain the engine at optimum operating temperature (typically **80°C to 95°C** at the cylinder head outlet).

**Two Types:**

1. **Air-Cooling System:** Used in small engines (motorcycles, lawnmowers). Fins on cylinder block surface dissipate heat to surrounding air via forced draught from a fan.
2. **Water-Cooling (Liquid Cooling) System:** Used in all lab diesel engines.

**Components of Water-Cooling System:**

- **Water Jacket:** Hollow passages cast into the cylinder block and head.
- **Water Pump (Centrifugal type):** Circulates coolant at 1500–3000 L/hr.
- **Thermostat (Wax-pellet type):** Opens at 82°C, fully open at 95°C.
- **Radiator (Core type):** Heat exchanger with honeycomb tubes and fins.
- **Cooling Fan:** Draws air through radiator.
- **Expansion Tank / Reserve Tank:** Accommodates coolant expansion.
- **Temperature Gauge & Sender Unit:** Monitors coolant temperature.

### 2.1.4 System 4 — Lubrication System

**Function:** To reduce friction and wear between moving parts, seal combustion gases, cool internal components, and prevent corrosion.

**Two Lubrication Methods:**

1. **Splash Lubrication:** Connecting rod dips into oil sump. Used in very small engines.
2. **Pressure Lubrication (Forced Feed):** Used in all multi-cylinder engines. An **oil pump** forces oil through galleries to bearings, camshaft, valve gear, and turbocharger bearings.

**Key Components:**

- **Sump (Oil Pan):** Reservoir at the bottom of the crankcase.
- **Oil Pump (Gear / Rotor type):** Discharge pressure ≈ 3–5 bar at idle, 4–6 bar at rated speed.
- **Oil Filter (Full-flow type):** Removes metallic and carbon particles.
- **Oil Pressure Gauge / Warning Lamp:** Senses pressure; lamp glows below 1.0 bar.
- **Dipstick:** Manual oil-level indicator.
- **Oil Cooler (Water-cooled type):** Maintains oil at 90–110°C.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Quick Fact:** The **turbocharger rotor** is lubricated and cooled by engine oil supplied through a dedicated feed line from the main oil gallery. On shutdown, the oil supply stops while the rotor continues to spin (up to **1,00,000 rpm**). If the engine is shut down after a hard run, residual heat can "coke" the oil in the bearing housing, leading to turbocharger failure. This is the reason for the **engine-idle-cool-down rule** (idle 30–60 seconds before shutdown).

### 2.1.5 System 5 — Electrical & Starting System

**Function:** To crank the engine for starting and provide electrical power to accessories and ignition (for petrol engines).

**Key Components:**

- **12V / 24V Lead-Acid Battery:** Provides cranking current (typically 400–800 CCA).
- **Starter Motor (DC series motor):** Cranks engine at 150–200 rpm.
- **Alternator (AC Generator with rectifier):** Charges battery and powers electricals.
- **Glow Plugs (For cold starting):** Pre-heat the combustion chamber air.
- **Solenoid Switch / Ignition Switch:** Engages starter.

### 2.1.6 System 6 — Governing System

**Function:** To maintain engine speed constant under varying loads, by automatically adjusting the fuel quantity.

**Types in Lab Engines:**

- **Mechanical (Centrifugal) Governor:** Uses spinning flyweights to act on the fuel rack.
- **Hydraulic Governor:** Uses engine oil pressure for precise control (used in large gensets).
- **Electronic Governor (ECU-based):** Modern, used with CRDi systems.

### 2.1.7 System 7 — Turbocharger (the heart of this experiment)

**Function:** A **turbomachine** that recovers waste energy from exhaust gases and uses it to compress intake air, increasing the engine's volumetric efficiency ($\eta_v$) from ≈ 78–85% (NA) to ≈ 95–180% (TC).

**Components of a Turbocharger (in flow order):**

1. **Exhaust Manifold → Turbine Inlet Scroll** (volute): Collects pulsating exhaust gases.
2. **Turbine Wheel (Radial Inward Flow):** Energy extraction stage. Made of **Inconel** super-alloy. Spins at up to **2,00,000 rpm**.
3. **Turbine Outlet / Exhaust Housing:** Directs spent gases to the exhaust pipe.
4. **Common Shaft (Rotor):** Couples turbine to compressor, supported on floating bearings.
5. **Compressor Wheel (Radial Outward Flow):** Draws and compresses fresh air. Made of aluminum alloy.
6. **Compressor Housing / Volute:** Directs compressed air to the intercooler.
7. **Bearing Housing (Journal Bearings):** Supports the rotating assembly, lubricated by engine oil.
8. **Wastegate (Optional):** Bypasses excess exhaust around the turbine to control boost pressure.
9. **Variable Geometry Turbo (VGT) Vanes:** Adjustable vanes that alter exhaust flow characteristics (used in modern diesel cars).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table summarises all key equations relevant to this experiment. The pipe symbol is intentionally replaced with `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| S.No | Parameter | Formula | Units | Application |
|:----:|:----------|:--------|:------|:------------|
| 1 | Compression Ratio | $r = \dfrac{V_s + V_c}{V_c} = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$ | — | Diesel: 14–22 |
| 2 | Air-Fuel Ratio (by mass) | $\text{AFR} = \dfrac{m_a}{m_f}$ | — | Lean operation: 18–25 |
| 3 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v = \dfrac{m_{a,\text{actual}}}{m_{a,\text{theoretical}}} = \dfrac{\rho_a}{\rho_s} \cdot \dfrac{T_s}{T_a}$ | — | TC: 95–180%; NA: 78–85% |
| 4 | Ideal Gas Equation | $pV = mR T$ | Pa·m³/kg·K | Manifold pressure calculation |
| 5 | Mass of Air Inducted (per cycle) | $m_a = \eta_v \cdot V_s \cdot \rho_a$ | kg/cycle | Cylinder filling |
| 6 | Boost Pressure Ratio | $\pi_b = \dfrac{p_{boost}}{p_{atm}}$ | — | TC: 1.5–2.5 typical |
| 7 | Indicated Power (4-stroke) | $IP = \dfrac{p_{im} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n}{60}$ for multi-cyl | kW | Lab calculation |
| 8 | Brake Power | $BP = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60\,000}$ | kW | Dynamometer test |
| 9 | BMEP | $p_{bm} = \dfrac{60\,000 \cdot BP}{L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n}$ | bar | Engine output indicator |
| 10 | BSFC | $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{m_f}{BP}$ | kg/kWh | Efficiency indicator |
| 11 | Air Charge Density after Intercooler | $\rho_{IC} = \dfrac{p_{boost}}{R T_{IC}}$ | kg/m³ | Density improvement |
| 12 | Intercooler Effectiveness | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{T_{comp} - T_{IC}}{T_{comp} - T_{amb}}$ | — | Typically 0.60–0.75 |
| 13 | Turbocharger Work Balance | $W_T = W_C \Rightarrow \dot{m}_{exh} \cdot c_{p,exh} \cdot \Delta T_{exh} = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_{p,a} \cdot \Delta T_a$ | W | Energy balance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Units Convention (KTU Standard):**
> - Pressure: **bar** (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa)
> - Temperature: **°C** (for display) or **K** (for equations)
> - Volume: **cc** or **L** (1 L = 10⁻³ m³)
> - Gas constant for air: $R = 287 \text{ J/kg·K}$

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Marine Industry:** Large 2-stroke marine diesel engines use turbocharging to deliver megawatts of power per cylinder.
- **Heavy Commercial Vehicles (HCV):** Trucks, buses — virtually all modern HCV diesel engines are turbocharged.
- **Power Generation:** Diesel Generator Sets (DG Sets) of 5 kVA to 2500 kVA rely on turbocharging for compactness.
- **Off-Road / Earthmoving:** JCB, CAT, Komatsu machinery uses **Variable Geometry Turbochargers (VGT)** for instantaneous boost response.
- **Defence & Railways:** Diesel locomotives (e.g., Indian Railways WDP-4) use turbocharged engines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Why a Turbocharger Increases Air Mass (Density Argument)

The fundamental principle behind turbocharging is increasing the **mass** of air inducted per cycle, which directly increases the fuel that can be burned and hence the energy released per cycle.

**Step 1 — Base Equation (Naturally Aspirated):**

For a naturally aspirated engine, the mass of air inducted per cycle is:

$$m_{a,NA} = \eta_{v,NA} \cdot V_s \cdot \rho_{atm}$$

where $V_s$ is the swept volume (m³), $\rho_{atm}$ is the atmospheric density ($\approx 1.2$ kg/m³ at NTP), and $\eta_{v,NA}$ is the volumetric efficiency of the NA engine (typically 0.78 to 0.85).

**Step 2 — With Turbocharger (Isentropic Compression):**

The turbocharger compresses intake air from $p_1 = p_{atm}$ to $p_2 = p_{boost}$, where $p_{boost} = \pi_b \cdot p_{atm}$ and $\pi_b$ is the boost pressure ratio.

Assuming **isentropic compression** (ideal case), the temperature after compression is:

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{\dfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} = T_1 \cdot \pi_b^{\dfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}$$

For air, $\gamma = 1.4$, hence $\dfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \dfrac{0.4}{1.4} \approx 0.2857$.

**Step 3 — Density after Compression (no intercooler):**

Using the ideal gas law $p = \rho R T$:

$$\rho_2 = \dfrac{p_2}{R T_2} = \dfrac{\pi_b \cdot p_{atm}}{R \cdot T_1 \cdot \pi_b^{0.2857}} = \dfrac{\pi_b^{1 - 0.2857}}{R T_1 / p_{atm}} \cdot \rho_1$$

This simplifies to:

$$\rho_2 = \rho_1 \cdot \pi_b^{0.7143}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Illustration (KTU-style):**

Let $p_{atm} = 1.013$ bar, $T_1 = 300$ K, boost ratio $\pi_b = 2.0$.

Compute temperature after compression:

$$T_2 = 300 \cdot 2.0^{0.2857}$$

$$2.0^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \cdot \ln 2} = e^{0.2857 \cdot 0.6931} = e^{0.1980} \approx 1.219$$

$$T_2 = 300 \cdot 1.219 = 365.7 \text{ K} \quad (\approx 92.7^\circ\text{C})$$

Density after compression (no intercooler):

$$\rho_2 = \dfrac{2 \cdot 1.013 \times 10^5}{287 \cdot 365.7} = \dfrac{2.026 \times 10^5}{1.0496 \times 10^5} = 1.930 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Density after Intercooler (cooling back to 320 K):**

$$\rho_{IC} = \dfrac{p_2}{R T_{IC}} = \dfrac{2.026 \times 10^5}{287 \cdot 320} = \dfrac{2.026 \times 10^5}{91\,840} = 2.206 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 6 — Comparison with NA Engine:**

$$\dfrac{m_{a,TC}}{m_{a,NA}} = \dfrac{\rho_{IC}}{\rho_{atm}} = \dfrac{2.206}{1.2} \approx 1.84$$

Hence, **the turbocharged engine with intercooling inducts ~84% more air mass per cycle** than the naturally aspirated one. This explains the **~50–80% increase in power output** observed in practice (the difference is partly lost to higher back-pressure, mechanical friction, and reduced volumetric efficiency at high manifold pressures).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Without the intercooler, the air would be hot (≈ 365.7 K in our calculation). Hot air is less dense and can lead to detonation-like phenomena. Intercooling recovers the density loss caused by compression heating.

## 3.2 Derivation — Turbocharger Energy Balance (Turbine drives Compressor)

The turbocharger shaft power is supplied by the exhaust gases to the turbine, and consumed by the compressor. At steady state, these must balance.

**Step 1 — Compressor Power Required:**

$$\dot{W}_C = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_{p,a} \cdot (T_2 - T_1)$$

where $\dot{m}_a$ is the air mass flow rate (kg/s), $c_{p,a} = 1005$ J/kg·K for air, and $T_2 - T_1$ is the temperature rise across the compressor.

**Step 2 — Turbine Power Available:**

$$\dot{W}_T = \dot{m}_{exh} \cdot c_{p,exh} \cdot (T_{exh,1} - T_{exh,2})$$

where $c_{p,exh} \approx 1140$ J/kg·K (for hot exhaust gases), and the temperature drop across the turbine provides the work.

**Step 3 — Equate (Energy Balance):**

$$\dot{m}_a \cdot c_{p,a} \cdot (T_2 - T_1) = \eta_m \cdot \dot{m}_{exh} \cdot c_{p,exh} \cdot (T_{exh,1} - T_{exh,2})$$

where $\eta_m$ is the mechanical efficiency of the turbocharger (typically 0.6 to 0.75, accounting for bearing friction and windage losses).

**Step 4 — Numerical Example:**

Suppose $\dot{m}_a = 0.10$ kg/s, $T_2 - T_1 = 65.7$ K (from our earlier example).
LHS = $0.10 \times 1005 \times 65.7 = 6602.85$ W ≈ 6.60 kW.

Suppose $\dot{m}_{exh} = 0.105$ kg/s (slightly more than air due to fuel), $T_{exh,1} - T_{exh,2} = 60$ K, $\eta_m = 0.70$.
RHS = $0.70 \times 0.105 \times 1140 \times 60 = 5027.4$ W ≈ 5.03 kW.

The difference (1.57 kW) is **insufficient** to drive the compressor at the desired boost. This indicates the need for either:
- A larger turbine area
- Higher exhaust mass flow (load increase)
- A smaller compressor

This is the principle behind **turbocharger matching**, a key concept in engine design.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Identifying Each System from a Cut-Section Diagram

In the KTU lab record, students are often given a photograph or cut-section diagram of an engine and asked to label components. The following matrix maps visual location to system:

| Region on Engine | Components to Identify | Belongs to Which System |
|:-----------------|:----------------------|:-----------------------|
| Top of cylinder head | Rocker arm, Valve springs, Push rod, Camshaft | Valve Gear / Lubrication |
| Side of cylinder head | Injector, Glow plug, Decompression lever | Fuel / Starting |
| Top of cylinder head (center) | Injector holder, Leak-off pipe | Fuel |
| Side of block (mid) | Lifting eye, Water jacket plug | Cooling |
| Bottom of block | Oil pan (sump), Drain plug | Lubrication |
| Side of block (lower) | Oil filter housing, Oil pressure sender | Lubrication |
| Front of engine | Fan belt, Water pump pulley, Crankshaft pulley | Cooling / Lubrication |
| Rear of engine | Flywheel, Ring gear, Starter motor mount | Starting |
| Top of engine (centerline) | Inlet manifold, Exhaust manifold, Turbo | Air Intake / Exhaust |
| Side-mounted on exhaust | Turbocharger with linkages | Turbocharger |
| Front of radiator | Cooling fan, Fan shroud | Cooling |
| Engine block (side) | Dipstick, Oil filler cap | Lubrication |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
