> *"The cooling curve is the locus of points showing the variation of the temperature difference between the engine coolant/oil and the surrounding atmosphere, plotted against elapsed time after the engine is switched off, under the continued action of the cooling system."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Patient After Surgery"

Imagine a patient running a high fever (steady-state high temperature). The doctor switches off the infection source (engine shutdown) but keeps the cooling blanket and IV fluids running (water pump still operational). A nurse records the patient's body temperature every 5 minutes.

- **Initial phase**: Temperature drops rapidly because the active cooling system is aggressive.
- **Final phase**: Temperature slowly creeps down to room temperature as the body re-equilibrates with the environment.

If you plot **Temperature vs. Time**, you get an **exponential decay curve** — exactly the cooling curve of an IC engine. The "fever" represents the engine's steady-state operating temperature, the "blanket" represents the water jacket, and the "room" represents ambient air.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used in This Experiment:**
> - **Specific heat of water, c_w = 4.187 kJ/(kg·K)**
> - **Specific heat of engine oil, c_oil ≈ 1.9 – 2.1 kJ/(kg·K)**
> - **Density of water, ρ_w = 1000 kg/m³**
> - **Density of engine oil, ρ_oil ≈ 880 kg/m³**
> - **Ambient temperature, T_∞ ≈ 30 °C – 38 °C** (Kerala lab conditions)

---

## 1.3 Why This Experiment is in the KTU 2024 Lab Syllabus

The cooling curve experiment bridges **steady-state heat transfer theory** with **dynamic transient thermal behavior**. It validates **Newton's Law of Cooling** in a real engineering system and gives the student hands-on exposure to:

- Multi-component heat exchangers
- Exponential decay modelling
- Time-constant identification (used in control systems)
- Thermal inertia in reciprocating machinery

> [!TIP]
> The cooling curve is the **dual** of the heating/loading curve used to estimate the friction horsepower (FHP) by Willan's line method — together they form the full thermal characterization of an engine.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Newton's Law of Cooling

Sir Isaac Newton's Law of Cooling (1701) states that the rate of loss of heat by a body is **directly proportional** to the temperature difference between the body and its surroundings.

For an engine at temperature $T$ and ambient at $T_\infty$:

$$Q = h \cdot A \cdot (T - T_\infty)$$

where:
- $Q$ = instantaneous rate of heat transfer **(W)**
- $h$ = overall convective heat transfer coefficient **(W/m²K)**
- $A$ = effective heat-transfer surface area **(m²)**
- $(T - T_\infty)$ = temperature difference **(K or °C)**

Equating this to the rate of thermal energy storage in the engine mass:

$$m \cdot c \cdot \frac{dT}{dt} = -h \cdot A \cdot (T - T_\infty)$$

The negative sign indicates temperature **decreases** with time.

---

## 2.2 Logical Steps — Building the Cooling Curve Equation

**Step 1 — Governing Differential Equation (Newton's Law in differential form):**
$$m \cdot c \cdot \frac{dT}{dt} = -h \cdot A \cdot (T - T_\infty)$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange and separate variables:**
$$\frac{dT}{(T - T_\infty)} = -\frac{h \cdot A}{m \cdot c} \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Define the cooling constant:**
$$k = \frac{h \cdot A}{m \cdot c} \quad \text{(units: s}^{-1}\text{)}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate both sides** from $T_0$ (steady-state temperature at $t = 0$) to $T$ at time $t$:
$$\int_{T_0}^{T} \frac{dT}{(T - T_\infty)} = -\int_{0}^{t} k \, dt$$

**Step 5 — Resulting exponential form (THE CORE EQUATION):**
$$T - T_\infty = (T_0 - T_\infty) \cdot e^{-k t}$$

**Step 6 — Take natural logarithm to linearize:**
$$\ln(T - T_\infty) = \ln(T_0 - T_\infty) - k t$$

This is a straight line: $y = c - k t$, where the **slope = $-k$** and **intercept = $\ln(T_0 - T_\infty)$**.

---

## 2.3 Two-Zone Cooling Model (KTU Board Favourite)

A real IC engine does NOT follow a single exponential. The curve has **two regimes**:

| Zone | Dominant Mechanism | Cooling Rate | Heat Transfer Mode |
|------|-------------------|-------------|--------------------|
| **Zone I** (0 – ~10 min) | Active water-circulation in jacket | Fast decay | Forced convection |
| **Zone II** (>10 min) | Oil film, fins, natural draft | Slow decay | Natural convection + radiation |

Hence we treat each zone as having its own cooling constant:
- $k_1$ = cooling constant for water/jacket zone
- $k_2$ = cooling constant for oil/metal mass zone

The **overall cooling curve** is the superposition (stitching) of these two exponentials.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — every formula below has appeared in KTU End Semester Examinations.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Used For |
|---|---------|----------------|-------|----------|
| 1 | $Q = h A (T - T_\infty)$ | Instantaneous heat loss rate | W | Newton's Law |
| 2 | $k = \dfrac{h A}{m c}$ | Cooling constant | s⁻¹ | Linearizing slope |
| 3 | $T - T_\infty = (T_0 - T_\infty) e^{-k t}$ | Exponential decay | °C | Plotting curve |
| 4 | $\ln(T - T_\infty) = \ln(T_0 - T_\infty) - k t$ | Linearized form | dimensionless | Finding $k$ from graph slope |
| 5 | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{k}$ | Thermal time constant | s | Time to reach 36.8% of $(T_0 - T_\infty)$ |
| 6 | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k} = \dfrac{0.693}{k}$ | Half-life (cooling) | s | Time to halve $\Delta T$ |
| 7 | $Q_{\text{total}} = m c (T_0 - T_\infty)$ | Total heat stored | J | Total energy dissipated |
| 8 | $\dot{Q}_{\text{avg}} = m c k (T_{\text{avg}} - T_\infty)$ | Avg heat loss rate | W | Energy balance check |
| 9 | $h = \dfrac{k \, m \, c}{A}$ | Heat transfer coefficient | W/m²K | From experimental $k$ |
| 10 | $\dot{m}_w c_w \Delta T_w = \dot{m}_o c_o \Delta T_o + Q_{\text{rad}}$ | Energy balance | W | Steady-state validation |

**Key Inequalities / Boundary Conditions:**
- $T > T_\infty$ always (cooling is by definition positive).
- $\vert T - T_\infty \vert$ decreases monotonically.
- $k$ is **positive**; slope of $\ln(T - T_\infty)$ vs $t$ is **negative**.
- For Zone I: $k_1 > k_2$ (forced convection is faster).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why Cooling Curve Matters |
|-------------|---------------------------|
| **Automotive R&D** | Designing radiator sizing and thermostat opening temperatures |
| **Aerospace engines** | Thermal management for restart-after-quench scenarios |
| **Marine engines** | Predicting cool-down time for maintenance access |
| **Power plant diesels** | Scheduling overhauls and thermal cycling fatigue analysis |
| **Hybrid vehicles** | Managing engine block temperature during off-times |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **time constant $\tau$** of an engine (typically 10–25 minutes for water-cooled IC engines) directly informs the **after-run coolant pump duration** in modern cars — keeping water circulating for ~3–5 $\tau$ ensures safe engine cool-down and prevents localized hot-spot damage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Newton's Law to Linear Plot

### Step 1: State the Physical Law

A body loses heat to its surroundings at a rate proportional to the temperature difference:

$$\frac{dQ}{dt} = -h A (T - T_\infty)$$

### Step 2: Express Heat Loss via Internal Energy Change

The heat lost by the engine equals the drop in its internal thermal energy. For an engine mass $m$ with average specific heat $c$:

$$\frac{dQ}{dt} = m c \frac{dT}{dt}$$

### Step 3: Equate the Two Expressions

Setting Step 1 equal to Step 2:

$$m c \frac{dT}{dt} = -h A (T - T_\infty)$$

### Step 4: Rearrange to Standard Form

Bring all temperature terms to the left and time terms to the right:

$$\frac{dT}{(T - T_\infty)} = -\frac{h A}{m c} \, dt$$

### Step 5: Define the Cooling Constant

Let us denote the constant group by a single symbol $k$ for mathematical elegance:

$$k = \frac{h A}{m c}$$

So the differential equation becomes:

$$\frac{dT}{(T - T_\infty)} = -k \, dt$$

### Step 6: Set Up the Limits of Integration

- At $t = 0$ (engine just shut off): $T = T_0$ (steady-state working temperature)
- At $t = t$ (any subsequent instant): $T = T(t)$

### Step 7: Perform the Integration

Integrating the left side from $T_0$ to $T$, and the right side from $0$ to $t$:

$$\int_{T_0}^{T} \frac{dT}{(T - T_\infty)} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

### Step 8: Evaluate Both Integrals

The left-hand side is a standard logarithmic integral:

$$\left[ \ln(T - T_\infty) \right]_{T_0}^{T} = -k \cdot t$$

### Step 9: Apply the Limits Explicitly

$$\ln(T - T_\infty) - \ln(T_0 - T_\infty) = -k t$$

### Step 10: Simplify Using Logarithm Rules

$$\ln\left( \frac{T - T_\infty}{T_0 - T_\infty} \right) = -k t$$

### Step 11: Exponentiate Both Sides

$$\frac{T - T_\infty}{T_0 - T_\infty} = e^{-k t}$$

### Step 12: Final Exponential Form (THE MASTER EQUATION)

$$\boxed{T - T_\infty = (T_0 - T_\infty) \, e^{-k t}}$$

### Step 13: Linearized Form for Experimental Plot

Taking natural log of Step 12:

$$\boxed{\ln(T - T_\infty) = \ln(T_0 - T_\infty) - k t}$$

This is a straight line $Y = C + M \cdot t$ where:
- $Y = \ln(T - T_\infty)$
- $C = \ln(T_0 - T_\infty)$ (intercept on Y-axis)
- $M = -k$ (slope of the line)

### Step 14: Calculate the Time Constant

The **time constant** $\tau$ is defined as the time required for $(T - T_\infty)$ to decay to $\dfrac{1}{e} \approx 36.8\%$ of its initial value $(T_0 - T_\infty)$.

Setting $T - T_\infty = \dfrac{T_0 - T_\infty}{e}$ in the master equation:

$$\frac{T_0 - T_\infty}{e} = (T_0 - T_\infty) \, e^{-k \tau}$$

Dividing both sides by $(T_0 - T_\infty)$:

$$\frac{1}{e} = e^{-k \tau}$$

Taking natural log of both sides:

$$-1 = -k \tau$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\tau = \frac{1}{k}}$$

### Step 15: Half-Life (Bonus Derivation)

Time for the temperature difference to drop to half:

$$\frac{T - T_\infty}{T_0 - T_\infty} = \frac{1}{2}$$

Taking log:

$$\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -k t_{1/2}$$

$$-\ln 2 = -k t_{1/2}$$

$$\boxed{t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} = \frac{0.6931}{k}}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A single-cylinder water-cooled diesel engine is run to a steady-state jacket water-outlet temperature of **85 °C**. The ambient temperature is **32 °C**. After shutdown, the cooling water continues to circulate. The following readings are recorded:

| Time $t$ (min) | Water-outlet $T$ (°C) |
|---------------|----------------------|
| 0 | 85 |
| 2 | 78 |
| 4 | 72 |
| 6 | 67 |
| 8 | 63 |
| 10 | 60 |
| 12 | 57 |
| 15 | 53 |
| 20 | 49 |

**Find:** (i) Cooling constant $k$ in min⁻¹, (ii) Time constant $\tau$, (iii) Temperature after 30 minutes.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Compute temperature difference $\theta = T - T_\infty = T - 32$:**

| $t$ (min) | $T$ (°C) | $\theta = T - 32$ |
|-----------|----------|-------------------|
| 0 | 85 | 53 |
| 2 | 78 | 46 |
| 4 | 72 | 40 |
| 6 | 67 | 35 |
| 8 | 63 | 31 |
| 10 | 60 | 28 |
| 12 | 57 | 25 |
| 15 | 53 | 21 |
| 20 | 49 | 17 |

**Step 2 — Compute $\ln \theta$:**

| $t$ | $\theta$ | $\ln \theta$ |
|-----|----------|--------------|
| 0 | 53 | 3.9703 |
| 2 | 46 | 3.8286 |
| 4 | 40 | 3.6889 |
| 6 | 35 | 3.5553 |
| 8 | 31 | 3.4340 |
| 10 | 28 | 3.3322 |
| 12 | 25 | 3.2189 |
| 15 | 21 | 3.0445 |
| 20 | 17 | 2.8332 |

**Step 3 — Use Two-Point Method for Slope:**

Using endpoints $(0, 3.9703)$ and $(20, 2.8332)$:

$$\text{Slope} = \frac{2.8332 - 3.9703}{20 - 0} = \frac{-1.1371}{20} = -0.0569 \text{ min}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Identify Cooling Constant:**

Since slope $= -k$:

$$k = 0.0569 \text{ min}^{-1} = 5.69 \times 10^{-2} \text{ min}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Time Constant:**

$$\tau = \frac{1}{k} = \frac{1}{0.0569} = 17.57 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 6 — Temperature at $t = 30$ min:**

$$\theta_{30} = 53 \cdot e^{-0.0569 \times 30} = 53 \cdot e^{-1.707}$$

$$e^{-1.707} = 0.1815$$

$$\theta_{30} = 53 \times 0.1815 = 9.62 \text{ °C}$$

$$T_{30} = \theta_{30} + T_\infty = 9.62 + 32 = 41.62 \text{ °C}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check:** $\theta$ should drop to $53/e = 19.49$ °C at $t = \tau = 17.57$ min. From the table, at $t = 20$ min, $\theta = 17$ °C, which is very close to $19.49 \cdot e^{-0.0569 \times 2.43} = 17.0$ °C. ✓

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — From Graph to Quantities

| Graphical Quantity | Mathematical Operation | Engineering Quantity Obtained |
|--------------------|------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Slope of $\ln(T - T_\infty)$ vs $t$ line | $-(\text{slope})$ | Cooling constant $k$ |
| Y-intercept of same line | $e^{\text{intercept}}$ | Initial temperature difference $(T_0 - T_\infty)$ |
| Time at $\theta = \theta_0 / e$ | Read from graph | Time constant $\tau$ |
| Asymptote of $T$ vs $t$ curve | Horizontal line at end | Ambient temperature $T_\infty$ |
| Area under $T$ vs $t$ curve | Numerical integration | Total heat dissipated (in J per unit mass) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Experimental Setup

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of the cooling curve experiment, showing all sensors, signal paths, and data reduction steps.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG[IC Engine Under Test] -->|Heat to jacket| JKT[Water Jacket]
    ENG -->|Heat to sump| OIL[Engine Oil Sump]
    ENG -->|Convection and radiation to air| AIR[Ambient Air Tinf]
    JKT -->|Circulated by pump| PUMP[Coolant Pump]
    PUMP -->|Cooled in| RAD[Radiator and Fan]
    RAD -->|Return cool water| JKT
    T1[Thermocouple T1 Water Inlet] --> JKT
    T2[Thermocouple T2 Water Outlet] --> JKT
    T3[Thermocouple T3 Oil Sump] --> OIL
    T4[Sensor T4 Ambient Air] --> AIR
    T1 --> DAQ[Data Logger and Stopwatch]
    T2 --> DAQ
    T3 --> DAQ
    T4 --> DAQ
    DAQ -->|Record at fixed intervals| TAB[Observation Table]
    TAB -->|Plot T minus Tinf vs t| CRV[Cooling Curve]
    TAB -->|Plot ln T minus Tinf vs t| LIN[Linearized Plot]
    CRV -->|Identify two zones| ZONE[Zone I Water and Zone II Oil]
    LIN -->|Measure slope| KCALC[Calculate k]
    KCALC -->|Invert| TAU[Calculate Time Constant tau]
    KCALC -->|Multiply with m c| HTC[Calculate h A product]
```

---

## 4.2 Cooling Curve Shape — Schematic of $T$ vs $t$ Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine at Steady State T0 at t=0] -->|Forced water cooling| B[Zone I Rapid Exponential Drop]
    B -->|After pump off or near Tinf| C[Transition Knuckle Point]
    C -->|Natural convection and oil dissipation| D[Zone II Slow Asymptotic Decay]
    D -->|Engine fully cooled| E[T equals Tinf Horizontal Asymptote]
    F[Vertical Drop at t=0] -.->|Initial condition| A
```

**Interpretation of the curve regions:**

1. **Vertical initial line at $t = 0$:** Represents the steady-state temperature $T_0$ just before shutdown.
2. **Zone I (steep descent):** Water jacket and pump-driven forced convection; slope is steepest.
3. **Knuckle point:** Inflection where water cooling becomes less dominant and oil/ambient takes over.
4. **Zone II (gentle tail):** Slow exponential approach to ambient; dominated by oil, fins, and radiation.
5. **Horizontal asymptote:** Final state where $T = T_\infty$ and heat transfer ceases.

---

## 4.3 Semi-Log Linearized Plot — $\ln(T - T_\infty)$ vs $t$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Data Reduction Step] --> B[Compute theta = T minus Tinf for each t]
    B --> C[Compute ln theta]
    C --> D[Plot ln theta on Y axis versus t on X axis]
    D --> E{Is the plot a straight line?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Validates Newton Law; slope equals minus k]
    E -->|No, two segments| G[Two Zones: Water Zone k1 and Oil Zone k2]
    F --> H[Best fit line yields k1 and k2]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Compute tau equals 1 over k for each zone]
    I --> J[Compute hA equals k times m times c]
```

---

## 4.4 State Diagram — Engine Thermal States During the Experiment

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ColdStart: Engine OFF and Cold
    ColdStart --> Running: Cranking and Ignition
    Running --> SteadyState: After ~30 minutes of loading
    SteadyState --> CoolingPhase1: Switch OFF fuel and load
    CoolingPhase1 --> KnucklePoint: Water jacket temp near Tinf
    KnucklePoint --> CoolingPhase2: Oil and metal mass dominate
    CoolingPhase2 --> FullyCooled: T equals Tinf plus 1 degree C
    FullyCooled --> [*]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**Q: Define the cooling curve of an IC engine. Why is it plotted as the temperature difference $(T - T_\infty)$ rather than the absolute temperature $T$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The cooling curve of an IC engine is the plot of the working temperature of the engine (typically jacket water-outlet or oil-sump temperature) versus elapsed time after the engine is shut off, while the cooling system continues to operate.

**(1 mark for definition)**

It is plotted as $(T - T_\infty)$ rather than $T$ because:
- Newton's Law of Cooling is fundamentally a relation in terms of the **driving temperature difference**, not absolute temperature.
- Plotting $(T - T_\infty)$ gives an **exponential decay** that can be linearized via logarithm — allowing extraction of the cooling constant $k$ from the slope.
- It also **automatically normalizes** for ambient temperature variation between experiments/labs.

**(2 marks for the two reasons)**

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**Q: What is the thermal time constant of an IC engine? How is it related to the cooling constant?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The thermal time constant $\tau$ of an IC engine is defined as the time required for the temperature difference $(T - T_\infty)$ to decay to $\dfrac{1}{e} \approx 36.8\%$ of its initial steady-state value $(T_0 - T_\infty)$ after engine shutdown.

**(1 mark for definition)**

Mathematically, from Newton's Law of Cooling:

$$T - T_\infty = (T_0 - T_\infty) e^{-k t}$$

Setting $t = \tau$ and $T - T_\infty = (T_0 - T_\infty)/e$:

$$\tau = \frac{1}{k}$$

**(2 marks for derivation and final relation)**

> [!NOTE]
> A typical water-cooled IC engine has $\tau \approx 15$–$25$ minutes, whereas an air-cooled engine has $\tau \approx 30$–$60$ minutes due to lower $hA$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2, 14 Marks]`

**(a) Derive Newton's Law of Cooling in differential form and obtain the exponential decay equation for the cooling curve of an IC engine.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) An IC engine is operated to a steady-state jacket-outlet water temperature of 92 °C. After shutdown, the following readings are observed with ambient at 30 °C. Determine the cooling constant, time constant, and predict the temperature after 25 minutes.** **(7 marks)**

| $t$ (min) | 0 | 3 | 6 | 9 | 12 | 15 | 20 |
|-----------|---|---|---|---|----|----|----|
| $T$ (°C) | 92 | 83 | 76 | 70 | 66 | 62 | 58 |

---

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State Newton's Law:
$$\frac{dQ}{dt} = -hA(T - T_\infty)$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Equate to internal energy change:
$$mc \frac{dT}{dt} = -hA(T - T_\infty)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Define cooling constant $k = hA/(mc)$:
$$\frac{dT}{T - T_\infty} = -k \, dt$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Set up limits and integrate:
$$\int_{T_0}^{T} \frac{dT}{T - T_\infty} = -\int_{0}^{t} k \, dt$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Evaluate integrals:
$$\ln(T - T_\infty) - \ln(T_0 - T_\infty) = -k t$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Rearrange to exponential form:
$$T - T_\infty = (T_0 - T_\infty) e^{-k t}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Linearized form and slope interpretation:
$$\ln(T - T_\infty) = \ln(T_0 - T_\infty) - k t$$
Plot of $\ln(T - T_\infty)$ vs $t$ gives slope $= -k$.

##### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute $\theta = T - T_\infty = T - 30$ and $\ln \theta$:

| $t$ | $T$ | $\theta$ | $\ln \theta$ |
|-----|-----|----------|--------------|
| 0 | 92 | 62 | 4.127 |
| 3 | 83 | 53 | 3.970 |
| 6 | 76 | 46 | 3.829 |
| 9 | 70 | 40 | 3.689 |
| 12 | 66 | 36 | 3.584 |
| 15 | 62 | 32 | 3.466 |
| 20 | 58 | 28 | 3.332 |

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply least-squares slope formula (or two-point method):

Using endpoints $(0, 4.127)$ and $(20, 3.332)$:

$$k = -\text{slope} = -\left(\frac{3.332 - 4.127}{20 - 0}\right) = -\left(\frac{-0.795}{20}\right) = 0.0398 \text{ min}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Time constant:
$$\tau = \frac{1}{k} = \frac{1}{0.0398} = 25.13 \text{ min}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Temperature at $t = 25$ min:
$$\theta_{25} = 62 \cdot e^{-0.0398 \times 25} = 62 \cdot e^{-0.995}$$

$$e^{-0.995} = 0.3698$$

$$\theta_{25} = 62 \times 0.3698 = 22.93 \text{ °C}$$

$$T_{25} = 22.93 + 30 = 52.93 \text{ °C} \approx 53 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Result statement:
- Cooling constant $k \approx 0.04 \text{ min}^{-1}$
- Time constant $\tau \approx 25 \text{ min}$
- Predicted temperature after 25 min $\approx 53 \text{ °C}$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 2, 14 Marks]`

**(a) With a neat sketch, explain the experimental procedure to obtain the cooling curve of a multi-cylinder water-cooled IC engine in the laboratory. List the instruments required.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) The slope of the $\ln(T - T_\infty)$ vs $t$ plot for the cooling curve of an engine was found to be $-0.025 \text{ min}^{-1}$. If the engine mass is 250 kg with average specific heat 500 J/(kg·K) and effective heat transfer area is 1.2 m², determine: (i) the cooling constant, (ii) the time constant, (iii) the overall heat transfer coefficient $h$.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Experimental Procedure (7 marks)

**Apparatus Required (2 marks):**
1. Multi-cylinder water-cooled IC engine test rig with eddy-current or rope brake dynamometer
2. Jacket water inlet and outlet thermometers (mercury-in-glass or RTD)
3. Oil sump thermometer
4. Ambient air thermometer
5. Stopwatch (resolution 1 second)
6. Water flow rotameter
7. Tachometer (for monitoring engine speed during running)

**Procedure (5 marks):**

1. **Start the engine** and allow it to warm up under no-load for ~5 minutes to circulate oil.
2. **Apply rated load** and run until all temperatures (water inlet, outlet, oil sump) become constant — this is the **steady state**. Note these as $T_{0,\text{water}}$ and $T_{0,\text{oil}}$.
3. **Record ambient temperature** $T_\infty$ using a thermometer placed away from any heat source.
4. **Stop the engine abruptly** — shut off fuel supply and remove load. Simultaneously **start the stopwatch** ($t = 0$).
5. **Do NOT stop the cooling water pump** — let it continue running.
6. **Record temperature readings** at intervals of 1–2 minutes for the first 15 minutes, then at 5-minute intervals thereafter.
7. **Continue until** $T \approx T_\infty + 1$ °C.
8. **Tabulate** $t$, $T_{\text{water-out}}$, $T_{\text{oil}}$, $T_\infty$.
9. **Plot two curves:** $T$ vs $t$ and $\ln(T - T_\infty)$ vs $t$.
10. **Find slope** of linearized plot → cooling constant $k$.

**Expected Observation:** Two distinct zones — fast (water jacket) and slow (oil mass).

##### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Slope $= -k = -0.025 \text{ min}^{-1}$
- $m = 250 \text{ kg}$
- $c = 500 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$
- $A = 1.2 \text{ m}^2$

**(i) Cooling constant (2 marks):**
$$k = 0.025 \text{ min}^{-1} = \frac{0.025}{60} \text{ s}^{-1} = 4.167 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**(ii) Time constant (2 marks):**
$$\tau = \frac{1}{k} = \frac{1}{4.167 \times 10^{-4}} = 2400 \text{ s} = 40 \text{ min}$$

**(iii) Overall heat transfer coefficient (3 marks):**

From the definition of cooling constant:

$$k = \frac{hA}{mc} \implies h = \frac{k \, m \, c}{A}$$

Converting $k$ to s⁻¹:
$$h = \frac{(4.167 \times 10^{-4}) \times 250 \times 500}{1.2}$$

$$h = \frac{52.0875}{1.2} = 43.41 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Answers:**
- $k = 0.025 \text{ min}^{-1}$ or $4.167 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}^{-1}$
- $\tau = 40 \text{ min}$
- $h \approx 43.4 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$ (typical for natural convection on an engine block)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU Board Examiner Insights):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract ambient temperature** — Plotting $T$ vs $t$ directly instead of $(T - T_\infty)$ vs $t$. This loses 1–2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Sign error on slope** — Writing slope $= +k$ instead of slope $= -k$. Always double-check: as $t$ increases, $\ln(T - T_\infty)$ decreases → slope is negative.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency** — Mixing minutes and seconds in the same calculation. Pick one and stick to it; if $k$ is in min⁻¹, then $\tau$ is in minutes.
> 4. **Ignoring two zones** — Fitting a single straight line to data that clearly has two slopes. Real cooling curves need two $k$ values: $k_1$ for water-jacket and $k_2$ for oil/metal mass.
> 5. **Not stating Newton's Law before deriving** — Always begin the derivation with a verbal statement of the law and a clear definition of $k$.
> 6. **Forgetting to draw the schematic** — In procedure questions, a missing/neatly-labelled setup diagram can cost up to 2 marks even if the text is perfect.
> 7. **Ambiguity in $T_\infty$** — Using "room temperature" loosely. Specify the exact location (e.g., "1 m from the engine, shielded from sunlight") where the ambient sensor was placed.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cooling Curve of IC Engines**

### Key Definitions
- [ ] Cooling curve = plot of $(T - T_\infty)$ vs $t$ after engine shutdown.
- [ ] Steady-state temperature = temperature at $t = 0$, just before shutdown.
- [ ] Cooling constant $k$ = positive constant in Newton's Law; slope of $\ln(T - T_\infty)$ vs $t$ is $-k$.
- [ ] Time constant $\tau = 1/k$ = time for $\Delta T$ to decay to $36.8\%$ of initial.
- [ ] Half-life $t_{1/2} = 0.693/k$ = time for $\Delta T$ to halve.
- [ ] Overall heat transfer coefficient $h = (k \cdot m \cdot c) / A$.

### Core Equations
- [ ] $T - T_\infty = (T_0 - T_\infty) \, e^{-k t}$
- [ ] $\ln(T - T_\infty) = \ln(T_0 - T_\infty) - k t$
- [ ] $Q = h A (T - T_\infty)$
- [ ] $k = hA / (mc)$

### Experimental Must-Knows
- [ ] Run engine to steady state before shutdown.
- [ ] **Continue water circulation** after shutdown.
- [ ] Record ambient $T_\infty$ simultaneously.
- [ ] Plot **two graphs**: $T$ vs $t$ AND $\ln(T - T_\infty)$ vs $t$.
- [ ] Identify **two zones** (water-dominated and oil/metal-dominated).
- [ ] Use **endpoints or least-squares method** to find slope.
- [ ] Always report $k$ with **consistent units** (min⁻¹ or s⁻¹).

### Typical Numerical Values (Memorize for Sanity Check)
- [ ] Water-cooled engine: $k \approx 0.04$–$0.07 \text{ min}^{-1}$, $\tau \approx 15$–$25$ min.
- [ ] Air-cooled engine: $k \approx 0.015$–$0.03 \text{ min}^{-1}$, $\tau \approx 30$–$60$ min.
- [ ] Engine block $h$ for natural convection: $5$–$15 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$.
- [ ] Engine block $h$ with forced air: $25$–$50 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$.

### Examiner Triggers
- [ ] If asked for "cooling constant" → either slope or computed value, with units.
- [ ] If asked for "time constant" → $\tau = 1/k$, never skip derivation.
- [ ] If asked for "heat transfer coefficient" → need $m$, $c$, $A$ — always mention assumption of uniform $c$.
- [ ] If asked to "draw the curve" → always show asymptote, knuckle, and label both zones.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Mnemonic for the Lab:**
> *"**L**og the **D**ifference, **F**ind the **S**lope, **I**nvert to **T**ime."*
> (Log the difference $T - T_\infty$; Find the slope; the slope is $-k$; Invert to get the time constant.)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Valve Timing Diagram of IC Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUL408 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Valve Timing Diagram of IC Engines # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Valve Timing Diagram** is a graphical representation plotted against **crank angle (degrees)** on the X-axis and **valve lift / valve status (open or closed)** on the Y-axis. It illustrates the exact instants at which the intake and exhaust valves of a reciprocating Internal Combustion (IC) engine open and close relative to the piston position (TDC and BDC) during the complete **four-stroke cycle (720°)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** > In the KTU 2024 scheme lab course *PCAUL408 – Thermal Engineering Lab*, Module Experiment 2 specifically tests the student's ability to **draw, label, and interpret** the valve timing diagram for a given 4-stroke petrol/diesel engine using the actual cam-shaft specifications or manufacturer data. Marks are heavily awarded for **correct angle placement**, **arrow directions on lift curves**, and **naming each event precisely (IVO, IVC, EVO, EVC)**. The diagram fundamentally answers four critical questions for an examiner: 1. **When** does the intake valve open? 2. **When** does the intake valve close? 3. **When** does the exhaust valve open? 4. **When** does the exhaust valve close? And, more importantly, **why** these events occur slightly *before* or *after* TDC/BDC and not exactly at the dead centers. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doorman of a Theatre" Imagine a busy single-door theatre hall: - A **doorman (intake valve)** must let audiences (air-fuel mixture) *in* before the show starts, and must close the door only *after* the last latecomer has settled inside. - Another **doorman (exhaust valve)** must open *slightly before* the audience (burnt gases) leaves the hall, and shut *after* the last puff of smoke has escaped. If both doormen worked *only* at exact TDC/BDC, audiences would be cramped, gases would be trapped, and the engine would suffocate. So engineers deliberately make the valves open *early* and close *late* — a phenomenon called **valve overlap** and **lead/lag angles**. > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Takeaway:** > - **Opening Early = Lead angle** (valve starts action before the dead center) > - **Closing Late = Lag angle** (valve finishes action after the dead center) > - **Valve Overlap** = period when both intake and exhaust valves are simultaneously open (occurs at TDC between exhaust and intake strokes). ## 1.3 Standard Reference Angles (Typical 4-Stroke Petrol Engine) For a standard **4-stroke petrol engine**, the conventional valve timing angles are: | Event | Notation | Typical Angle | |---|---|---| | Intake Valve Opens | **IVO** | 10°–20° **before TDC** (BTDC) of suction stroke | | Intake Valve Closes | **IVC** | 30°–50° **after BDC** (ABDC) of suction stroke | | Exhaust Valve Opens | **EVO** | 30°–50° **before BDC** (BBDC) of expansion stroke | | Exhaust Valve Closes | **EVC** | 10°–20° **after TDC** (ATDC) of exhaust stroke | | Valve Overlap | — | Sum of IVO lead + EVC lag (≈ 20°–40°) | For **diesel engines**, these angles are **slightly different** because of higher compression ratios and absence of throttle (load control by fuel quantity). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four-Stroke Engine Sequence (Foundation Recap) Before interpreting the timing diagram, the student must internalize the **720° cycle** sequence: 1. **Suction Stroke (0° → 180°):** Piston moves TDC → BDC, intake valve open, fresh charge drawn in. 2. **Compression Stroke (180° → 360°):** Piston moves BDC → TDC, both valves closed, charge compressed. 3. **Expansion / Power Stroke (360° → 540°):** Combustion pushes piston TDC → BDC, both valves closed. 4. **Exhaust Stroke (540° → 720°):** Piston moves BDC → TDC, exhaust valve open, burnt gases expelled. > [!NOTE] > **Critical Point:** Although the engine fires every **two revolutions (720°)**, **each valve event is referenced to its own dead-center position**. For example, IVO is *before TDC of the suction stroke*, not before TDC of the power stroke. ## 2.2 Detailed Event-by-Event Logic ### 2.2.1 Intake Valve Opens (IVO) — Typically 10°–20° BTDC - **Why early?** At TDC of suction stroke, the piston has zero velocity and the cylinder pressure is at its lowest. Opening the valve slightly *before* TDC creates a wider flow passage by the time the piston begins to descend, increasing **volumetric efficiency**. - **Effect on diagram:** Lift curve of intake valve rises from baseline ~10°–20° before the first TDC. ### 2.2.2 Intake Valve Closes (IVC) — Typically 30°–50° ABDC - **Why late?** Even after BDC, the descending piston creates inertia in the incoming charge. The rushing air-fuel mixture possesses **kinetic energy**, so the valve remains open to allow this "ram effect" to push extra charge into the cylinder. - **Effect on diagram:** Lift curve falls back to baseline 30°–50° after BDC of the suction stroke. ### 2.2.3 Exhaust Valve Opens (EVO) — Typically 30°–50° BBDC - **Why early?** Around 70–80% of expansion stroke, the burnt gas pressure is still 3–4 bar. Opening the exhaust valve *before BDC* allows a **blow-down** — a sudden release of high-pressure gas that performs useful work on the piston during its final travel, while also preventing heat damage to the valve. - **Effect on diagram:** Lift curve of exhaust valve rises ~30°–50° before BDC of the power stroke. ### 2.2.4 Exhaust Valve Closes (EVC) — Typically 10°–20° ATDC - **Why late?** The exhaust gas has high inertia. Closing the valve slightly after TDC exploits the **scavenging effect** — the upward-moving piston and the inertia of escaping gases together expel residual gases, leaving a clean cylinder for the next charge. - **Effect on diagram:** Lift curve falls back to baseline 10°–20° after TDC of the exhaust stroke. ### 2.2.5 Valve Overlap - **Definition:** The angular interval (in crank degrees) during which **both intake and exhaust valves are simultaneously open** at TDC between the exhaust and suction strokes. - **Magnitude:** = IVO (lead angle) + EVC (lag angle) - **Typical value:** 20°–40° for petrol engines. - **At low engine speeds:** Overlap is generally *good* (better scavenging). - **At high engine speeds:** Excessive overlap causes **fresh charge to escape directly out the exhaust** — a phenomenon called **short-circuiting**. Hence high-performance engines use **variable valve timing (VVT)**. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Full Form | Typical Value (Petrol) | Typical Value (Diesel) | Unit | |---|---|---|---|---| | $\alpha_{IVO}$ | Intake Valve Open Lead Angle | **10°–20°** BTDC | **5°–15°** BTDC | degrees (°) | | $\alpha_{IVC}$ | Intake Valve Close Lag Angle | **30°–50°** ABDC | **25°–45°** ABDC | degrees (°) | | $\alpha_{EVO}$ | Exhaust Valve Open Lead Angle | **30°–50°** BBDC | **35°–55°** BBDC | degrees (°) | | $\alpha_{EVC}$ | Exhaust Valve Close Lag Angle | **10°–20°** ATDC | **5°–15°** ATDC | degrees (°) | | $\theta_{ov}$ | Valve Overlap Angle | $\alpha_{IVO} + \alpha_{EVC}$ | $\alpha_{IVO} + \alpha_{EVC}$ | degrees (°) | | $N$ | Engine Speed | 1500–6000 | 1000–4000 | rpm | | $\theta_{cycle}$ | One Complete Cycle (4-stroke) | **720°** | **720°** | degrees (°) | | $\eta_{vol}$ | Volumetric Efficiency (related to IVC) | 70%–85% | 80%–92% | — | > [!WARNING] > **Critical Examination Note:** > KTU examiners frequently deduct marks if students write "**angle before TDC of exhaust stroke**" instead of "**angle before TDC of suction stroke**" for IVO. Always reference the stroke in which the valve is **performing its function**, not the stroke in which the crank position occurs. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Engine Design:** Helps designers determine cam profile, valve spring stiffness, and timing chain/belt specifications. - **Performance Tuning:** Racing engineers adjust valve overlap to optimize torque at specific RPM bands. - **Diagnostics:** A worn timing chain can shift the diagram and degrade performance — mechanics compare actual readings against the manufacturer's published valve timing chart. - **Emissions Control:** Reducing overlap lowers HC emissions during idling; modern VVT systems dynamically adjust overlap. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Constructing the Valve Timing Diagram — Exhaustive Procedure The construction of the valve timing diagram is the **most frequently asked 14-mark question** in KTU university examinations for this lab course. The following exhaustive 9-step method ensures a flawless answer. ### Step 1: Draw the Crank Angle Axis Draw a horizontal X-axis representing **crank angle from 0° to 720°**. Mark the four dead centers: - **0°** — TDC at start of suction stroke - **180°** — BDC at end of suction / start of compression - **360°** — TDC at end of compression / start of power - **540°** — BDC at end of power / start of exhaust - **720°** — TDC at end of exhaust (= 0° of next cycle) ### Step 2: Draw the Cylinder Pressure Reference Curve Sketch the classic **p-V cycle** shape projected on the timing diagram for visual aid: - Atmospheric pressure line for suction. - Rising curve during compression. - Peak at combustion (after TDC of power stroke by ~10°–15°). - Falling curve during expansion. - Exhaust at slightly above atmospheric. > *This step is optional in part-answer but strongly recommended for full marks.* ### Step 3: Plot the Intake Valve Lift Curve Starting from the baseline (closed = 0 lift) at $\alpha_{IVO}$ degrees **before** 0° TDC: $$\text{Intake opens at crank angle} = 360° - \alpha_{IVO} \quad (\text{if measured from start of exhaust})$$ But conventionally, **relative to 0° TDC of suction stroke**: $$\text{Intake opens at angle} = -\alpha_{IVO} \quad \text{i.e., } \alpha_{IVO} \text{ BTDC}$$ The lift rises smoothly (parabolic/cam profile) to a **maximum lift of ~10 mm** in the middle of the suction stroke and falls back to zero at: $$\text{Intake closes at angle} = 180° + \alpha_{IVC}$$ ### Step 4: Plot the Exhaust Valve Lift Curve The exhaust valve lift curve begins to rise at: $$\text{Exhaust opens at angle} = 540° - \alpha_{EVO}$$ The lift curve peaks at the middle of the exhaust stroke and falls back to zero at: $$\text{Exhaust closes at angle} = 720° + \alpha_{EVC}$$ ### Step 5: Mark Valve Overlap Region The overlap region is the angular interval near **720° (= 0°)** where both lift curves are above zero: $$\theta_{ov} = \alpha_{IVO} + \alpha_{EVC}$$ ### Step 6: Identify the Four Events with Arrow Direction **Arrow Convention:** - **Vertical upward arrow ↑** at the start of each lift curve = valve **opening**. - **Vertical downward arrow ↓** at the end of each lift curve = valve **closing**. ### Step 7: Label Every Stroke Between the dead centers, write the stroke names: - 0°–180°: **Suction** - 180°–360°: **Compression** - 360°–540°: **Expansion / Power** - 540°–720°: **Exhaust** ### Step 8: Numerical Worked Example **Problem:** A 4-stroke petrol engine has the following valve timing specification: - IVO = 12° BTDC of suction stroke - IVC = 40° ABDC of suction stroke - EVO = 45° BBDC of power stroke - EVC = 14° ATDC of exhaust stroke **Find (a)** valve overlap, **(b)** the crank angle at which maximum intake air-flow occurs (assume mid-suction). **Solution:** **(a) Valve Overlap:** $$\begin{aligned} \theta_{ov} &= \alpha_{IVO} + \alpha_{EVC} \\ \theta_{ov} &= 12° + 14° \\ \theta_{ov} &= 26° \end{aligned}$$ **(b) Crank angle at maximum intake flow (mid-suction stroke):** $$\begin{aligned} \theta_{mid,intake} &= \frac{0° + (180° + \alpha_{IVC} - \alpha_{IVO})}{2} \\ &= \frac{0° + (180° + 40° - 12°)}{2} \\ &= \frac{0° + 208°}{2} \\ &= 104° \text{ from TDC of suction stroke} \end{aligned}$$ This corresponds to **104° ATDC of suction stroke** (approximately mid-stroke, accounting for the asymmetric open/close times). ### Step 9: Final Verification Checklist | Verification Item | Status | |---|---| | All four events labeled with correct notation (IVO/IVC/EVO/EVC) | ✓ | | Stroke names correctly placed between dead centers | ✓ | | Arrow directions show opening (↑) and closing (↓) | ✓ | | Overlap region shaded or hatched distinctly | ✓ | | Numerical values written next to each event | ✓ | | Unit (degrees) mentioned on every angle | ✓ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analytical Matrix — Why Each Angle Is What It Is:** > > | Event | Lead/Lag | Justification (Engineering Reasoning) | > |---|---|---| > | IVO (BTDC) | Lead | Increase volumetric efficiency by widening port when piston starts descending | > | IVC (ABDC) | Lag | Exploit ram effect — kinetic energy of incoming charge fills cylinder beyond geometric displacement | > | EVO (BBDC) | Lead | Blow-down high-pressure gas before TDC, recover piston work and protect valve from overheating | > | EVC (ATDC) | Lag | Scavenging — inertia of exhaust gas carries residual gases out even as piston rises | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Valve Timing System The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **functional interactions** of every component that contributes to the valve timing phenomenon. This block-level representation is preferred over attempting a physical hand-drawn diagram inside a text-based engine. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Crankshaft Rotation
0 deg to 720 deg] --> B[Timing Chain or Belt] B --> C[Camshaft Synchronized at Half Engine Speed] C --> D[Intake Cam Lobe Profile] C --> E[Exhaust Cam Lobe Profile] D --> F1[Intake Valve Tappet Lifter] E --> F2[Exhaust Valve Tappet Lifter] F1 --> G1[Intake Valve Stem Vertical Motion] F2 --> G2[Exhaust Valve Stem Vertical Motion] G1 --> H1[Intake Port Opens to Cylinder] G2 --> H2[Exhaust Port Opens to Cylinder] H1 --> I[Four Stroke Cycle Completion] H2 --> I I --> J{Valve Overlap Zone
Near TDC Exhaust to Suction} J --> K[Scavenging of Residual Gases] J --> L[Volumetric Efficiency Improvement] K --> M[Engine Performance Output] L --> M subgraph ReferenceAngles[KTU Reference Angle Block] R1[IVO Lead Angle 10 to 20 deg BTDC] R2[IVC Lag Angle 30 to 50 deg ABDC] R3[EVO Lead Angle 30 to 50 deg BBDC] R4[EVC Lag Angle 10 to 20 deg ATDC] R5[Overlap Angle IVO plus EVC] end D -.governs.-> R1 D -.governs.-> R2 E -.governs.-> R3 E -.governs.-> R4 R1 --> R5 R4 --> R5 ``` ## 4.2 Annotated Schematic — Valve Timing Diagram (ASCII Representation) The following is a textual schematic representation that mirrors the actual hand-drawn diagram students are expected to produce in the exam: ``` Intake Valve Exhaust Valve Lift (mm) Lift (mm) | | 10 mm | ___________ | ___________ | / \ | / \ | / \ | / \ 0 mm |__/_______________\_________|___________/_______________\____ | ↑ ↓ | ↑ ↓ ↑ | IVO IVC | EVO EVC (720°) | |___|___________|__________|___________|___________________|___ Crank Angle 0° 180° 360° 540° 720° TDC BDC TDC BDC TDC | | | | | SUCTION COMPRESSION POWER EXHAUST STROKE STROKE STROKE |--Overlap--| (at TDC end of exhaust) ``` ## 4.3 Cam-Action Cause-Effect Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Piston at TDC] --> B[Cam Lobe Base Circle Aligns with Tappet] B --> C[Valve Remains Closed
No Lift] D[Piston Moves Away from TDC] --> E[Cam Lobe Rotates Nose Toward Tappet] E --> F[Tappet Pushed Upward] F --> G[Valve Lift Increases] G --> H[Port Opens
Gas Exchange Begins] H --> I[Lift Peaks at Cam Nose] I --> J[Lift Decreases as Cam Flank Rotates Away] J --> K[Valve Returns to Seat via Spring] K --> L[Port Sealed] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define valve overlap. Why is it necessary in a 4-stroke IC engine?** **Model Answer:** Valve overlap is the angular interval (measured in crank degrees) during which both the intake valve and the exhaust valve are simultaneously open at the TDC position between the exhaust and suction strokes. $$\theta_{ov} = \alpha_{IVO} + \alpha_{EVC}$$ It is necessary because: 1. It enables **scavenging** — the upward-moving piston and inertia of exhaust gases expel residual burnt gases. 2. It improves **volumetric efficiency** at low and medium engine speeds by allowing fresh charge to enter while exhaust gases are still leaving. 3. It cools the exhaust valve and cylinder head by drawing in cool fresh charge across hot surfaces. --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **List any four valve timing events in a 4-stroke petrol engine with their typical angular values.** **Model Answer:** | Event | Notation | Typical Angle | |---|---|---| | Intake Valve Opens | IVO | 12° BTDC of suction stroke | | Intake Valve Closes | IVC | 40° ABDC of suction stroke | | Exhaust Valve Opens | EVO | 45° BBDC of power stroke | | Exhaust Valve Closes | EVC | 14° ATDC of exhaust stroke | ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **(a)** With the help of a neat labeled sketch, explain the valve timing diagram of a 4-stroke petrol engine. Discuss the significance of valve overlap. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The valve timing of a 4-stroke petrol engine is as follows: - IVO = 10° BTDC of suction stroke - IVC = 35° ABDC of suction stroke - EVO = 40° BBDC of power stroke - EVC = 12° ATDC of exhaust stroke Calculate **(i)** the valve overlap angle and **(ii)** the total angle during which both valves remain closed. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution to Part (a):** The valve timing diagram is plotted with crank angle (0°–720°) on the X-axis and valve lift on the Y-axis. The diagram shows: - **Intake valve lift curve:** Rises from zero 10° before TDC of suction stroke, peaks during the middle of the suction stroke, and returns to zero 35° after BDC of the suction stroke. Thus the intake valve remains open for an angular duration of: $$\theta_{IV} = 180° + \alpha_{IVO} + \alpha_{IVC} = 180° + 10° + 35° = 225°$$ - **Exhaust valve lift curve:** Rises from zero 40° before BDC of the power stroke, peaks during the middle of the exhaust stroke, and returns to zero 12° after TDC of the exhaust stroke. Thus the exhaust valve remains open for: $$\theta_{EV} = 180° + \alpha_{EVO} + \alpha_{EVC} = 180° + 40° + 12° = 232°$$ **Significance of Valve Overlap:** 1. **Improved volumetric efficiency** — fresh charge enters while exhaust gases are still flowing out, creating a small pressure differential that aids scavenging. 2. **Internal cooling** of the exhaust valve seat and cylinder head. 3. **Better idle quality** at low speeds. 4. **Risk:** At very high speeds, excessive overlap causes fresh charge to escape, reducing volumetric efficiency. This is mitigated in modern engines using **Variable Valve Timing (VVT)** systems. **Model Solution to Part (b):** **(i) Valve Overlap:** $$\begin{aligned} \theta_{ov} &= \alpha_{IVO} + \alpha_{EVC} \\ &= 10° + 12° \\ &= 22° \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) Total Angle When Both Valves Closed:** Total cycle = 720°. Both valves closed = 720° − (Intake valve open angle + Exhaust valve open angle − Overlap). $$\begin{aligned} \theta_{IV} &= 180° + 10° + 35° = 225° \\ \theta_{EV} &= 180° + 40° + 12° = 232° \\ \theta_{both\,closed} &= 720° - (\theta_{IV} + \theta_{EV} - \theta_{ov}) \\ &= 720° - (225° + 232° - 22°) \\ &= 720° - 435° \\ &= 285° \end{aligned}$$ **Verification:** Both valves closed occurs during compression (180°–360°) and expansion (360°–540°) = 360°. With corrections for IVO/EVC lead-lag outside these strokes, total ≈ 285°. ✓ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **(a)** Explain why the intake valve of a 4-stroke engine opens before TDC and closes after BDC. Justify your answer using gas dynamics principles. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Sketch and explain the valve timing diagram of a 4-stroke diesel engine. Compare it with the petrol engine diagram. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution to Part (a):** **Intake Valve Opens Before TDC (IVO with Lead Angle):** At exactly TDC, the piston has zero velocity, and the cylinder pressure equals atmospheric (or crankcase) pressure. If the valve opens only at TDC, the available flow area during the early part of the suction stroke is small, reducing the mass of air-fuel mixture inducted. By opening the valve $\alpha_{IVO}$ degrees before TDC, the intake port is already partially uncovered when the piston begins to descend. This increases the **effective flow area**, lowers the cylinder pressure earlier in the stroke (enhancing the pressure differential $\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{cyl}$), and improves **volumetric efficiency**. **Intake Valve Closes After BDC (IVC with Lag Angle):** At BDC, the piston velocity is zero again, but the incoming charge has built up significant **kinetic energy** (ram effect). Due to inertia, this momentum continues to push fresh charge into the cylinder even as the piston begins to ascend. Closing the valve $\alpha_{IVC}$ degrees after BDC exploits this ram effect, packing additional charge and increasing the **volumetric efficiency** beyond 100% in well-designed intake systems. **Gas Dynamics Justification:** Using Bernoulli's principle for the intake manifold: $$P_{atm} + \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2 = P_{cyl} + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_{cyl}^2$$ where $v$ is the velocity in the intake port. During the suction stroke, $v$ is high, so $P_{cyl} < P_{atm}$, drawing in charge. The longer the valve stays open (especially with IVC lag), the more total charge enters. **Model Solution to Part (b):** **Diesel Engine Valve Timing Diagram (Sketch Description):** A diesel engine valve timing diagram differs from petrol in the following aspects: | Parameter | Petrol Engine | Diesel Engine | |---|---|---| | IVO Lead | 10°–20° BTDC | 5°–15° BTDC (smaller) | | IVC Lag | 30°–50° ABDC | 25°–45° ABDC (smaller) | | EVO Lead | 30°–50° BBDC | 35°–55° BBDC (larger) | | EVC Lag | 10°–20° ATDC | 5°–15° ATDC (smaller) | | Overlap | 20°–40° | 10°–30° (smaller) | | Injection Timing | Spark at 10°–30° BTDC | Fuel injection at 5°–25° BTDC (higher compression, no throttle) | **Key Differences and Reasons:** 1. **Smaller overlap in diesel** — diesels operate with excess air; scavenging is less critical. Excessive overlap would allow fresh air to escape, reducing the trapped mass. 2. **Larger EVO lead in diesel** — higher expansion pressure (due to higher compression ratio ~17:1) means more work can be recovered by blow-down. 3. **Smaller IVO lead in diesel** — diesels are typically larger and slower, requiring less aggressive volumetric enhancement. 4. **No throttle in diesel** — load is controlled by fuel quantity, so intake depression is minimal. The complete diagram follows the same 0°–720° construction logic, with curves labeled using IVO, IVC, EVO, EVC and overlap zone shaded at the TDC end of the exhaust stroke. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Stroke Mis-attribution:** Writing "IVO before TDC of compression stroke" instead of "TDC of suction stroke" — costs 1–2 marks. > 2. **Arrow Direction Error:** Drawing the closing arrow pointing upward instead of downward — costs 1 mark per occurrence. > 3. **Overlap Definition:** Forgetting that overlap is the **sum** of IVO lead and EVC lag (not just one of them) — costs 2 marks. > 4. **Missing Unit:** Writing "10 BTDC" instead of "10° BTDC" — KTU is strict about units. > 5. **Confusing Strokes with Revolutions:** A 4-stroke engine cycle = 2 revolutions = 720°, NOT 360°. Mixing these up makes the entire diagram incorrect. > 6. **No valve labels on the diagram itself:** Examiner cannot award full marks if IVO/IVC/EVO/EVC labels are missing on the drawing, even if mentioned in the text. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Valve timing diagram** plots crank angle (0°–720°) on X-axis and valve lift on Y-axis for a 4-stroke engine. - The **four key events** are: **IVO (Intake Valve Opens), IVC (Intake Valve Closes), EVO (Exhaust Valve Opens), EVC (Exhaust Valve Closes)**. - IVO occurs **before TDC of suction stroke** (lead angle). - IVC occurs **after BDC of suction stroke** (lag angle). - EVO occurs **before BDC of power stroke** (lead angle). - EVC occurs **after TDC of exhaust stroke** (lag angle). - **Valve overlap** = IVO lead + EVC lag, occurring at the TDC between exhaust and suction strokes. - **Lead angle** = valve opens before dead center. - **Lag angle** = valve closes after dead center. - **Intake valve open angular duration** = 180° + IVO + IVC (typically ~225°). - **Exhaust valve open angular duration** = 180° + EVO + EVC (typically ~232°). - **Petrol engines** have larger overlap than diesel engines for better scavenging at low speeds. - **Diesel engines** have smaller overlap because they operate with excess air and need not scavenge aggressively. - **High-speed engines** benefit from VVT (Variable Valve Timing) to optimize overlap across the RPM range. - Always label the **stroke name** between each pair of dead centers, not the crank angle. - Always include **arrow direction** (↑ for opening, ↓ for closing) on every valve event. - **Standard cycle** = 720° for 4-stroke; 360° for 2-stroke.

topics.title: Economic Speed Test on IC Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL408 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Economic Speed Test on IC Engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Economic Speed Test (EST)** is a routine performance evaluation test conducted on an Internal Combustion (IC) engine to determine the **most economical operating speed** at which the engine delivers a specified brake power (or load) with the **minimum possible Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)**.

Mathematically, it identifies the rotational speed $N_{econ}$ at which:

$$\frac{d(BSFC)}{dN} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{d^{2}(BSFC)}{dN^{2}} > 0$$

In simpler board-friendly terms, the test determines the engine speed corresponding to the **lowest point of the BSFC vs. Speed curve** at a given constant brake load.

### Key Terminology Used in KTU Valuation

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|------|--------|------------|
| Brake Power | $BP$ | Useful power delivered at the engine shaft (kW) |
| Fuel Consumption Rate | $\dot{m}_f$ | Mass of fuel burned per unit time (kg/s or kg/hr) |
| Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | $BSFC$ | Fuel consumed per unit brake power per hour (kg/kW·hr) |
| Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{bth}$ | Ratio of heat equivalent of BP to heat supplied by fuel |
| Economic Speed | $N_{econ}$ | The engine rpm at which BSFC attains its minimum value |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Car-Cruising Analogy"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition (for First-Time Learners)**

Imagine you are driving a motorcycle on a flat highway. You fix your throttle to deliver a particular load (say, cruising with a passenger). If you shift gears such that the engine revs too low, the bike "struggles" — fuel consumption shoots up because the engine is labouring. If you rev it too high, fuel is wasted due to high frictional and pumping losses.

There exists **one sweet-spot gear/engine speed** where the engine runs *silky smooth*, the bike feels effortless, and the fuel gauge needle barely drops. **That speed, for that particular load, is the Economic Speed.**

The test essentially maps this "sweet spot" quantitatively for the engine on the test bed.

---

## 1.3 Why This Test Matters — Engineering Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Automotive Design:** Vehicle manufacturers (Tata, Maruti, Toyota) tune their gear ratios so that the most-frequently-used cruising speed of the vehicle coincides with $N_{econ}$ of the engine.
> - **Generator Sets:** Diesel generator sets are rated at the economic speed for maximum fuel efficiency during continuous power generation.
> - **Marine & Industrial Engines:** Long-hour running engines are always operated near $N_{econ}$ to minimize operating cost (fuel is the largest recurring expense).
> - **Hybrid Powertrains:** Modern hybrid systems (Toyota Prius, Hyundai Ioniq) actively use this principle to switch between engine and motor for optimal BSFC.

The **economic speed is NOT the speed of maximum power**. It is the speed of **minimum BSFC for a given load** — which is why it is called *economic*, not *power*.

---

## 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics Used in This Experiment

> [!NOTE]
> - **Density of petrol:** $\rho_f \approx 0.72 \text{ to } 0.78 \text{ kg/litre}$ (typically taken as **0.78 kg/litre** in KTU labs)
> - **Density of diesel:** $\rho_f \approx 0.83 \text{ to } 0.86 \text{ kg/litre}$ (typically **0.85 kg/litre**)
> - **Calorific Value of petrol:** $CV \approx 44{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (used in $\eta_{bth}$ calculation)
> - **Calorific Value of diesel:** $CV \approx 43{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}$
> - **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (in rope brake calculations)
> - **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/mol·K}$ (used only in air-standard cycle comparisons)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Governing Principle Behind Economic Speed Test

The BSFC of an IC engine is influenced by two opposing trends as engine speed changes:

**Trend 1 — Time-Related Losses (Dominant at LOW speeds):**
At low speeds, the combustion process has more wall-contact time. Heat transfer to the cooling jacket is higher, friction is relatively higher per cycle, and combustion completeness reduces. This **increases BSFC** at low speeds.

**Trend 2 — Speed-Related Losses (Dominant at HIGH speeds):**
At high speeds, friction (bearing drag, piston ring drag), pumping losses, and incomplete combustion (less time for mixture preparation) all rise sharply. This **also increases BSFC** at high speeds.

**Result:** Between these two extremes lies a **minimum BSFC point** — this is the *economic speed*. The BSFC vs. Speed curve is therefore a **U-shaped (parabolic-like) curve**.

---

## 2.2 Logical Step-by-Step Operation of the Experiment

1. **Engine Warm-Up:** Run the engine at no-load for 5–10 minutes to reach steady operating temperature.
2. **Fix the Load:** Apply a fixed brake load using the rope-brake / hydraulic / eddy-current dynamometer.
3. **Vary the Speed:** By adjusting the throttle (for SI engines) or fuel pump rack (for CI engines), vary the engine speed across a range (e.g., 1000, 1200, 1400, 1600, 1800, 2000 rpm).
4. **Record Observations at Each Speed Setting:**
   - Spring balance readings (dead weight + live weight) → $W_d$, $W_l$
   - Time for $N$ revolutions of the brake drum → $t$ seconds
   - Volume of fuel consumed in a fixed time interval → $V$ ml or cc
5. **Calculate BP, $\dot{m}_f$, and BSFC** for each speed.
6. **Plot Graph:** BSFC (Y-axis) vs. Engine Speed in rpm (X-axis).
7. **Identify Minimum:** The rpm at the bottom of the U-curve is the **Economic Speed** for that load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial KTU Point:** The Economic Speed depends on the **load**. The test must be repeated for different loads to construct a family of BSFC vs. Speed curves, and an "Economic Speed Map" is obtained.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Net Load on Brake | $W = W_d - W_l$ | N (or kgf) | $W_d$ = dead weight, $W_l$ = live weight |
| 2 | Effective Brake Radius | $R = \dfrac{D + d}{2}$ | m | $D$ = drum diameter, $d$ = rope diameter |
| 3 | Brake Torque | $T_b = W \times R \times g$ | N·m | Multiply by 9.81 if $W$ is in kgf |
| 4 | Brake Power | $BP = \dfrac{2 \pi N T_b}{60 \times 1000}$ | kW | $N$ in rpm |
| 5 | Brake Power (Alternate form) | $BP = \dfrac{W \times L \times N}{t \times 60}$ | kW | $L$ = effective rope circumference, $t$ in sec |
| 6 | Volume flow rate of fuel | $\dot{V}_f = \dfrac{V}{t_f}$ | ml/s or cc/s | $V$ in ml, $t_f$ in s |
| 7 | Mass flow rate of fuel | $\dot{m}_f = \dot{V}_f \times \rho_f$ | kg/s | $\rho_f$ in kg/ml or kg/litre |
| 8 | Fuel consumption per hour | $m_{f,hr} = \dot{m}_f \times 3600$ | kg/hr | — |
| 9 | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | $BSFC = \dfrac{m_{f,hr}}{BP} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \times 3600}{BP}$ | kg/kW·hr | The KEY output of the test |
| 10 | Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{BP \times 3600}{\dot{m}_f \times CV}$ | dimensionless (or %) | $CV$ in kJ/kg |
| 11 | Equivalent BSFC formula (most used in KTU labs) | $BSFC = \dfrac{V \times \rho_f \times 60}{BP \times t_f}$ | kg/kW·hr | When $V$ is in litres and $t_f$ in minutes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Watch-out for the absolute value notation (KTU Board trap):** Never use $\vert x \vert$ in markdown tables. Always use $\mid x \vert$ or simply state the magnitude of $W$ directly as $W = (W_d - W_l)$ assuming $W_d > W_l$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of the BSFC Map

The BSFC map (also called a "Willans-type fuel map" or "BSFC contour map") is the **single most important performance map** used in:

- **Engine Management System (EMS) Calibration:** Modern ECUs use pre-stored BSFC maps to choose the optimal air-fuel ratio and spark timing for any (speed, load) operating point.
- **Hybrid Vehicle Energy Management:** The supervisory controller in a Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive uses the BSFC map to decide when to run the ICE, when to run the motor, and when to run both.
- **Fuel Economy Certification:** The ARAI (India) and EPA (USA) test cycles use the BSFC map to estimate real-world fuel economy.
- **Marine & Stationary Engine Sizing:** Continuous-duty engines are sized such that the **design point coincides with the minimum BSFC region** of the map.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** If a student draws a BSFC vs Speed curve that is *monotonically decreasing* or *monotonically increasing*, it is treated as a **conceptual error** — marks are deducted. The curve **must show a clear U-shape with a minimum** for the answer to be considered correct.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of All Governing Equations

### 3.1.1 Derivation of Brake Power from Rope-Brake Dynamometer

The rope brake is a friction-type dynamometer. A rope is wrapped around a rotating brake drum and tangential friction force is opposed by two spring balances.

**Step 1 — Net tangential force on the drum surface:**
The drum is pulled in the direction of rotation by the dead weight side and resisted by the live weight side. Net tangential force is:

$$F_{net} = (W_d - W_l) \quad \text{[Newtons]}$$

**Step 2 — Conversion to load in kgf (KTU lab convention):**
Many lab dynamometers display spring balance readings in kgf. To convert:

$$F_{net} = (W_d - W_l) \times g = (W_d - W_l) \times 9.81 \quad \text{[N]}$$

> **Conversion logic:** Weight is force, so we multiply the mass reading by gravitational acceleration $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Step 3 — Effective radius of the brake drum:**
The rope has a finite thickness $d$, so the effective radius is the average of the drum radius and (drum radius + rope radius):

$$R_{eff} = \frac{D}{2} + \frac{d}{2} = \frac{D + d}{2} \quad \text{[metres]}$$

> **Conversion logic:** The rope's centre line lies midway between the drum surface and the outer rope surface. This effective radius is where the friction force acts.

**Step 4 — Brake Torque:**
Torque is the product of tangential force and the moment arm (effective radius):

$$T_b = F_{net} \times R_{eff} = (W_d - W_l) \times g \times \left(\frac{D+d}{2}\right) \quad \text{[N·m]}$$

**Step 5 — Angular velocity of the drum:**
The drum rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute. Converting to rad/s:

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} \quad \text{[rad/s]}$$

> **Conversion logic:** 1 revolution = $2\pi$ radians, 1 minute = 60 seconds.

**Step 6 — Brake Power:**
Power is torque times angular velocity:

$$BP = T_b \times \omega = (W_d - W_l) \times g \times \left(\frac{D+d}{2}\right) \times \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

Simplifying (the factor of 2 cancels with the 2 in the denominator of the radius):

$$BP = \frac{\pi \times N \times (W_d - W_l) \times g \times (D + d)}{60} \quad \text{[Watts]}$$

Converting to kilowatts:

$$\boxed{BP = \frac{\pi \times N \times (W_d - W_l) \times g \times (D + d)}{60 \times 1000} \quad \text{[kW]}}$$

**Step 7 — Alternate form using rope length:**
The effective rope length (circumference of effective radius) is $L = \pi(D+d)$. Substituting:

$$BP = \frac{N \times (W_d - W_l) \times g \times L}{60 \times 1000} \quad \text{[kW]}$$

If we measure the **time $t$ for $N$ revolutions**, then linear rope speed is $L \times N / t$ m/s and power is force × speed:

$$\boxed{BP = \frac{(W_d - W_l) \times g \times L \times N}{t \times 60} \quad \text{[kW], when } t \text{ is in seconds}}$$

---

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Fuel Mass Flow Rate

**Step 1 — Volume flow rate:**
A measuring cylinder (burette) collects $V$ ml of fuel in time $t_f$ seconds:

$$\dot{V}_f = \frac{V}{t_f} \quad \text{[ml/s]}$$

**Step 2 — Mass flow rate using fuel density:**
Since $1 \text{ litre} = 1000 \text{ ml}$ and density is in kg/litre:

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{\dot{V}_f}{1000} \times \rho_f = \frac{V \times \rho_f}{1000 \times t_f} \quad \text{[kg/s]}$$

> **Conversion logic:** Volume in ml divided by 1000 gives litres; multiplying by $\rho_f$ (kg/litre) gives kg.

**Step 3 — Fuel consumption per hour (for BSFC):**
Multiply by 3600 s/hr:

$$m_{f,hr} = \frac{V \times \rho_f \times 3600}{1000 \times t_f} = \frac{V \times \rho_f \times 3.6}{t_f} \quad \text{[kg/hr]}$$

> **Conversion logic:** 3600/1000 = 3.6 — a useful KTU-lab shortcut.

---

### 3.1.3 Derivation of BSFC

BSFC is *fuel consumed per hour divided by brake power delivered per hour*:

$$BSFC = \frac{m_{f,hr}}{BP}$$

Substituting:

$$BSFC = \frac{\dfrac{V \times \rho_f \times 3.6}{t_f}}{\dfrac{(W_d - W_l) \times g \times L \times N}{t \times 60}}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{BSFC = \frac{V \times \rho_f \times 3.6 \times t \times 60}{t_f \times (W_d - W_l) \times g \times L \times N} \quad \text{[kg/kW·hr]}}$$

**Special Case (Most common in KTU lab):** If fuel volume $V$ is measured in **litres** and time $t_f$ in **minutes**, then:

$$m_{f,hr} = \frac{V \text{ (litres)} \times \rho_f \text{ (kg/litre)} \times 60 \text{ (min/hr)}}{t_f \text{ (min)}}$$

$$BSFC = \frac{V \times \rho_f \times 60}{BP \times t_f} \quad \text{[kg/kW·hr]}$$

---

## 3.2 Complete Worked-Out Numerical Problem (Model Question Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU University Exam Style — January 2024 Pattern**

**Problem Statement:**
During an economic speed test on a 4-stroke single-cylinder petrol engine, the following observations were recorded at a constant brake load of **20 N** (net, with dynamometer constant $C = 6000$ where $BP = C \times \text{load} \times N / 10^6$ kW). The fuel consumption was measured for 30 seconds at each speed.

| Trial | Speed (rpm) | Fuel consumed in 30 s (ml) | Spring balance (kgf) |
|-------|-------------|---------------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | 1000 | 22 | 4.08 / 2.04 |
| 2 | 1200 | 24 | 3.92 / 1.96 |
| 3 | 1400 | 25 | 3.70 / 1.85 |
| 4 | 1600 | 27 | 3.50 / 1.75 |
| 5 | 1800 | 30 | 3.30 / 1.65 |
| 6 | 2000 | 34 | 3.10 / 1.55 |

Given: $\rho_f = 0.78$ kg/litre, $g = 9.81$ m/s², effective brake drum diameter = 600 mm.

**Find:**
(a) BSFC at each speed.
(b) Plot BSFC vs. Speed.
(c) Determine the economic speed.

---

### Step 1 — Net Load $W$ at Each Trial

$$W = (W_d - W_l) \text{ in kgf, then multiply by } g$$

| Trial | $W_d - W_l$ (kgf) | $W$ (N) |
|-------|-------------------|---------|
| 1 | 2.04 | 20.01 |
| 2 | 1.96 | 19.23 |
| 3 | 1.85 | 18.15 |
| 4 | 1.75 | 17.17 |
| 5 | 1.65 | 16.19 |
| 6 | 1.55 | 15.21 |

> **Logic:** As speed increases, the engine is throttled more, friction increases, and net load on the brake drum decreases (assuming throttle-driven speed change with constant dynamometer excitation). Real test beds maintain load via dynamometer controller — this dataset represents a *throttle-varied, controlled-load* test.

### Step 2 — Effective Brake Radius

$$R_{eff} = \frac{D}{2} = \frac{0.600}{2} = 0.300 \text{ m}$$

### Step 3 — Brake Torque $T_b$ and Brake Power $BP$

$$T_b = W \times R_{eff} = W \times 0.300 \quad \text{[N·m]}$$

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi N T_b}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{\pi N T_b}{30000} \quad \text{[kW]}$$

**Trial 1 (N = 1000 rpm):**

$$T_b = 20.01 \times 0.300 = 6.003 \text{ N·m}$$

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi \times 1000 \times 6.003}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{37699}{60000} = 0.6283 \text{ kW}$$

**Trial 2 (N = 1200 rpm):**

$$T_b = 19.23 \times 0.300 = 5.769 \text{ N·m}$$

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi \times 1200 \times 5.769}{60 \times 1000} = 0.7251 \text{ kW}$$

**Trial 3 (N = 1400 rpm):**

$$T_b = 18.15 \times 0.300 = 5.445 \text{ N·m}$$

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi \times 1400 \times 5.445}{60 \times 1000} = 0.7984 \text{ kW}$$

**Trial 4 (N = 1600 rpm):**

$$T_b = 17.17 \times 0.300 = 5.151 \text{ N·m}$$

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi \times 1600 \times 5.151}{60 \times 1000} = 0.8630 \text{ kW}$$

**Trial 5 (N = 1800 rpm):**

$$T_b = 16.19 \times 0.300 = 4.857 \text{ N·m}$$

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi \times 1800 \times 4.857}{60 \times 1000} = 0.9153 \text{ kW}$$

**Trial 6 (N = 2000 rpm):**

$$T_b = 15.21 \times 0.300 = 4.563 \text{ N·m}$$

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi \times 2000 \times 4.563}{60 \times 1000} = 0.9556 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 4 — Fuel Mass Flow Rate

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{V \text{ (ml)}}{1000 \times t_f \text{ (s)}} \times \rho_f = \frac{V \times 0.78}{30 \times 1000} = \frac{V \times 0.78}{30000} \quad \text{[kg/s]}$$

**Trial 1:** $\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{22 \times 0.78}{30000} = 5.72 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/s

**Trial 2:** $\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{24 \times 0.78}{30000} = 6.24 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/s

**Trial 3:** $\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{25 \times 0.78}{30000} = 6.50 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/s

**Trial 4:** $\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{27 \times 0.78}{30000} = 7.02 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/s

**Trial 5:** $\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{30 \times 0.78}{30000} = 7.80 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/s

**Trial 6:** $\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{34 \times 0.78}{30000} = 8.84 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/s

### Step 5 — BSFC Calculation

$$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f \times 3600}{BP} \quad \text{[kg/kW·hr]}$$

| Trial | $N$ (rpm) | $BP$ (kW) | $\dot{m}_f$ (kg/s) | $BSFC$ (kg/kW·hr) |
|-------|-----------|-----------|---------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | 1000 | 0.6283 | 5.72×10⁻⁴ | **3.278** |
| 2 | 1200 | 0.7251 | 6.24×10⁻⁴ | **3.098** |
| 3 | 1400 | 0.7984 | 6.50×10⁻⁴ | **2.930** |
| 4 | 1600 | 0.8630 | 7.02×10⁻⁴ | **2.928** ← minimum |
| 5 | 1800 | 0.9153 | 7.80×10⁻⁴ | **3.068** |
| 6 | 2000 | 0.9556 | 8.84×10⁻⁴ | **3.331** |

**Trial 1 calculation (worked out):**

$$BSFC = \frac{5.72 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600}{0.6283} = \frac{2.0592}{0.6283} = 3.278 \text{ kg/kW·hr}$$

**Trial 4 calculation (worked out):**

$$BSFC = \frac{7.02 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600}{0.8630} = \frac{2.5272}{0.8630} = 2.928 \text{ kg/kW·hr}$$

### Step 6 — Plot and Economic Speed Determination

Plotting BSFC (Y-axis) against Speed (X-axis), the curve is U-shaped:

- The curve **decreases** from 1000 to 1600 rpm (low-speed combustion losses dominate).
- The curve **reaches a minimum** at **1600 rpm** (BSFC = 2.928 kg/kW·hr).
- The curve **rises** beyond 1600 rpm (high-speed friction/pumping losses dominate).

$$\boxed{N_{econ} \approx 1600 \text{ rpm at the given load}}$$

### Step 7 — Interpretation

> The engine is most fuel-efficient (lowest BSFC) at 1600 rpm. If this engine were used in a vehicle, the gearbox should be designed so that the typical cruising condition corresponds to this speed. The corresponding **Brake Thermal Efficiency** is:

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP \times 3600}{\dot{m}_f \times 3600 \times CV} = \frac{0.8630}{7.02 \times 10^{-4} \times 44000} = \frac{0.8630}{30.888} = 0.02794$$

$$\eta_{bth} \approx 2.79\%$$

(This is unrealistically low because the dataset is illustrative; real small engines have $\eta_{bth} \approx 20\text{–}30\%$. The methodology remains the same.)

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Sources of Error and Their Effect on BSFC

| Source of Error | Effect on $BP$ | Effect on $\dot{m}_f$ | Net Effect on $BSFC$ |
|-----------------|----------------|------------------------|----------------------|
| Inertia correction ignored | Underestimates $BP$ | No effect | $BSFC$ appears higher |
| Spring balance not zeroed | $T_b$ offset | No effect | $BSFC$ shifts uniformly |
| Fuel leak in burette | No effect | Overestimates $\dot{m}_f$ | $BSFC$ appears higher |
| Engine not warmed up | Lower $BP$ | Higher $\dot{m}_f$ (rich mixture) | $BSFC$ much higher |
| Speed not stable (fluctuating) | Random error in $T_b$ | Random error in $V$ | Scattered data points |
| Incorrect $\rho_f$ value | No effect on $BP$ | Proportional error | Proportional error in $BSFC$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Insight:** The "**inertia correction**" is a frequent exam question. When the engine is accelerating or decelerating during the test, the indicated power differs from the brake power by the rate of change of kinetic energy of the flywheel. The corrected brake power is:

$$BP_{corrected} = BP_{indicated} \pm I \cdot \alpha \cdot \omega$$

where $I$ is the moment of inertia of the flywheel, $\alpha$ is angular acceleration, and $\omega$ is angular velocity. The '+' sign is used when the engine is **accelerating** (energy stored in flywheel); the '−' sign when **decelerating**.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Experimental Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IC Engine Under Test<br>4-Stroke Petrol / Diesel] --> B[Coupling Flange]
    B --> C[Rope Brake Drum<br>or Eddy Current Dynamometer]
    C --> D1[Spring Balance Wd<br>Dead Weight Side]
    C --> D2[Spring Balance Wl<br>Live Weight Side]
    A --> E[Throttle / Fuel Pump Rack<br>Speed Control]
    A --> F[Fuel Burette<br>Volumetric Measurement]
    A --> G[Tachometer<br>Speed Display rpm]
    A --> H[Thermocouples<br>Coolant and Exhaust Temp]
    F --> I[Stop Watch<br>Fuel Time t_f]
    C --> J[Stop Watch<br>Revolutions Time t]
    D1 --> K[Data Acquisition<br>Tabulation Sheet]
    D2 --> K
    G --> K
    F --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Calculation Module<br>BP, mf, BSFC]
    L --> M[Graph Plotter<br>BSFC vs Speed]
    M --> N[Economic Speed<br>Minimum Point]
    H --> O[Steady State Monitor]
    O --> A
```

> **Block Legend:** The engine (A) is the prime mover. Dynamometer (C) loads it. Two spring balances (D1, D2) measure tangential force. Fuel measurement is via burette (F) with timing watch (I). Speed is monitored via tachometer (G). All data flows to the calculation module (L) which produces BSFC and feeds the graph plotter (M). The minimum of the graph (N) is the desired economic speed.

---

## 4.2 Procedural Flowchart of the Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Start] --> P1[Inspect Engine<br>and Check Oil Level]
    P1 --> P2[Connect Cooling Water<br>and Fuel Lines]
    P2 --> P3[Start Engine<br>at No-Load]
    P3 --> P4[Warm-Up<br>5 to 10 Minutes]
    P4 --> P5[Apply Fixed Load<br>on Dynamometer]
    P5 --> P6[Set Engine to<br>Lowest Test Speed]
    P6 --> P7[Record Wd, Wl, N<br>V, t_f, t]
    P7 --> P8{More Speeds<br>to Test?}
    P8 -->|Yes| P9[Increase Speed<br>by Throttle]
    P9 --> P7
    P8 -->|No| P10[Tabulate All<br>Observations]
    P10 --> P11[Calculate BP<br>at Each Speed]
    P11 --> P12[Calculate mf<br>at Each Speed]
    P12 --> P13[Calculate BSFC<br>at Each Speed]
    P13 --> P14[Plot BSFC<br>vs Speed Graph]
    P14 --> P15[Identify Minimum<br>Point on Curve]
    P15 --> P16[Economic Speed<br>Necon Identified]
    P16 --> P17[Repeat for Other<br>Loads if Required]
    P17 --> P18[Stop Engine<br>and Report]
```

> **Process Logic:** The flowchart enforces sequential data collection at multiple speeds, calculation of derived quantities, and graphical identification of the economic speed. The "More Speeds?" decision block (P8) is the iterative control point.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram of BSFC Behaviour and Engine Loss Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Fuel Energy Input] --> B[Combustion Losses]
    A --> C[Useful Brake Power]
    A --> D[Heat to Coolant]
    A --> E[Exhaust Energy]
    A --> F[Friction Power]
    A --> G[Pumping Loss]
    A --> H[Incomplete Combustion]
    C --> I[Brake Power Output BP]
    F --> J[Increases with Speed N^1.5]
    G --> K[Increases with Speed N^2]
    H --> L[Increases at Very High Speed]
    B --> M[Increases at Very Low Speed]
    I --> N[BSFC = Fuel Rate / BP]
    J --> N
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[BSFC vs Speed Curve]
    O --> P[U-Shaped Curve]
    P --> Q[Minimum at N econ]
```

> **Insight:** The four loss components (F, G, H, M) collectively shape the U-shaped BSFC curve. Friction rises as $N^{1.5}$, pumping loss rises as $N^2$ (these dominate at high N). Combustion loss dominates at low N due to high heat transfer per cycle. The balance produces the minimum at $N_{econ}$.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Define Economic Speed of an IC engine. Why does every engine have a unique economic speed?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Economic speed of an IC engine is the rotational speed at which the **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC) is minimum** for a given brake load.

> **Reason for uniqueness (2 marks):** BSFC is influenced by combustion efficiency, friction loss, pumping loss, and heat loss — all of which depend on engine speed in opposing ways. At low speeds, prolonged heat transfer and incomplete combustion raise BSFC. At high speeds, friction and pumping losses raise BSFC. The opposing trends produce a **single minimum BSFC** at one particular speed, making every engine have a unique economic speed for a given load.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — December 2022]**
**What is Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)? Mention its units and significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** BSFC is the mass of fuel consumed per unit brake power output per unit time.

> **Mathematical expression (1 mark):**
> $$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f \times 3600}{BP} \quad \text{[kg/kW·hr]}$$

> **Significance (1 mark):** BSFC is a **direct measure of engine fuel economy**. A lower BSFC indicates better fuel efficiency. It is the primary metric used in engine performance evaluation, vehicle certification, and hybrid energy management systems.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a) Explain with a neat sketch the procedure to conduct an economic speed test on a multi-cylinder petrol engine. Also draw a typical BSFC vs. speed curve and label the economic speed. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Apparatus Required (1.5 marks):**
- Multi-cylinder petrol engine (test bed)
- Rope brake / Eddy current dynamometer with two spring balances
- Fuel measuring burette with stop cock
- Stop watch (2 nos.)
- Tachometer
- Fuel tank with overflow arrangement
- Manometer, thermocouples (for monitoring)

**Experimental Procedure (3.5 marks):**

1. **Inspection:** Check engine oil, fuel, cooling water, and lubricating oil levels. Inspect all connections.
