2. **Engine Start and Warm-Up:** Start the engine with the help of the starter motor (or cranking handle for small engines). Allow it to run at no-load for 5–10 minutes until the cooling water temperature and oil temperature reach steady values.

3. **Application of Load:** Apply a fixed brake load on the dynamometer. The load is held constant throughout the test.

4. **Setting Test Speed:** Adjust the throttle lever to obtain the **lowest** test speed (e.g., 1000 rpm). Allow the engine to stabilize at this speed.

5. **Recording Observations:** Once steady state is reached, simultaneously trigger:
   - Stop watch 1 to record the time $t$ for $N$ revolutions of the brake drum.
   - Stop watch 2 to record the time $t_f$ for a fixed volume $V$ of fuel to be consumed (e.g., 20 ml).

6. **Reading Spring Balances:** Note the dead-weight balance reading $W_d$ and the live-weight balance reading $W_l$ during steady state.

7. **Varying Speed:** Increase the engine speed to the next test point (e.g., 1200 rpm) by adjusting the throttle. Wait for stabilization and repeat the readings.

8. **Repeat:** Continue for 5–6 speed points covering the operating range of the engine.

9. **Calculations:** Compute BP, $\dot{m}_f$, and BSFC at each speed.

10. **Plot and Identify:** Plot BSFC vs. engine speed. The minimum of the curve gives the economic speed $N_{econ}$.

**Typical BSFC vs. Speed Curve (2 marks):**

```
 BSFC
 (kg/kWh)
   |
4.0|  *                          *
   |    *                      *
3.5|      *                  *
   |        *              *
3.0|          *          *  
   |            *      *
2.5|              *  *
   |________________*_________________
      1000  1200  1400  1600  1800  2000
                    Speed (rpm)
                          ^
                     N_econ (1600 rpm)
                    Economic Speed
```

> The curve is **U-shaped** (parabolic-like), with the minimum at the economic speed. At very low speed, BSFC is high due to poor combustion; at very high speed, BSFC rises again due to friction and pumping losses.

---

**(b) During an economic speed test on a 4-stroke single-cylinder diesel engine, the following readings were taken at a constant load. Calculate the BSFC at each speed and determine the economic speed. (7 marks)**

| Speed (rpm) | Net Load (N) | Fuel for 30 s (ml) |
|-------------|--------------|---------------------|
| 1000 | 32.0 | 18 |
| 1200 | 30.0 | 20 |
| 1400 | 28.0 | 22 |
| 1600 | 26.0 | 25 |
| 1800 | 24.0 | 29 |
| 2000 | 22.0 | 34 |

Given: Effective brake drum diameter = 500 mm, $\rho_f = 0.85$ kg/litre.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Effective radius** $R = 500/2 = 250$ mm $= 0.25$ m.

**Step 2: BP at each speed** using $BP = \dfrac{2\pi N T_b}{60 \times 1000}$ where $T_b = W \times R$.

**Trial 1 (N = 1000 rpm):**

$$T_b = 32.0 \times 0.25 = 8.0 \text{ N·m}$$

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi \times 1000 \times 8.0}{60 \times 1000} = 0.838 \text{ kW}$$

**Trial 2 (N = 1200 rpm):**

$$T_b = 30.0 \times 0.25 = 7.5 \text{ N·m}$$

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi \times 1200 \times 7.5}{60 \times 1000} = 0.942 \text{ kW}$$

**Trial 3 (N = 1400 rpm):**

$$T_b = 28.0 \times 0.25 = 7.0 \text{ N·m}$$

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi \times 1400 \times 7.0}{60 \times 1000} = 1.026 \text{ kW}$$

**Trial 4 (N = 1600 rpm):**

$$T_b = 26.0 \times 0.25 = 6.5 \text{ N·m}$$

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi \times 1600 \times 6.5}{60 \times 1000} = 1.089 \text{ kW}$$

**Trial 5 (N = 1800 rpm):**

$$T_b = 24.0 \times 0.25 = 6.0 \text{ N·m}$$

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi \times 1800 \times 6.0}{60 \times 1000} = 1.131 \text{ kW}$$

**Trial 6 (N = 2000 rpm):**

$$T_b = 22.0 \times 0.25 = 5.5 \text{ N·m}$$

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi \times 2000 \times 5.5}{60 \times 1000} = 1.152 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Mass flow rate** $\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{V \times \rho_f}{1000 \times t_f} = \dfrac{V \times 0.85}{30000}$ kg/s.

| Trial | $V$ (ml) | $\dot{m}_f$ (kg/s) |
|-------|----------|---------------------|
| 1 | 18 | $5.10 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| 2 | 20 | $5.67 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| 3 | 22 | $6.23 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| 4 | 25 | $7.08 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| 5 | 29 | $8.22 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| 6 | 34 | $9.63 \times 10^{-4}$ |

**Step 4: BSFC** $= \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \times 3600}{BP}$ kg/kW·hr.

| Trial | $N$ (rpm) | $BP$ (kW) | $\dot{m}_f$ (kg/s) | $BSFC$ (kg/kW·hr) |
|-------|-----------|-----------|---------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | 1000 | 0.838 | 5.10×10⁻⁴ | **2.191** |
| 2 | 1200 | 0.942 | 5.67×10⁻⁴ | **2.167** |
| 3 | 1400 | 1.026 | 6.23×10⁻⁴ | **2.186** |
| 4 | 1600 | 1.089 | 7.08×10⁻⁴ | **2.341** |
| 5 | 1800 | 1.131 | 8.22×10⁻⁴ | **2.617** |
| 6 | 2000 | 1.152 | 9.63×10⁻⁴ | **3.010** |

> **Worked sample (Trial 2 — minimum):**
> $$BSFC = \frac{5.67 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600}{0.942} = \frac{2.0412}{0.942} = 2.167 \text{ kg/kW·hr}$$

**Conclusion:** The minimum BSFC is **2.167 kg/kW·hr at 1200 rpm**, hence the **economic speed is 1200 rpm** for this load.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2023 — Alternative Pattern]**

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the factors affecting the economic speed of an IC engine. Discuss how the economic speed varies with load. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram of Factors Affecting Economic Speed (3 marks):**

```
        +-------------------+
        |  ENGINE SPEED  N  |
        +---------+---------+
                  |
     +------------+-------------+
     |            |             |
     v            v             v
+---------+ +-----------+ +-----------+
| Combustion| | Mechanical| | Heat Loss|
| Losses    | | Friction  | |          |
|           | | Losses    | |          |
| - Time per| | - Bearing | | - To     |
|   cycle   | | - Piston  | |   coolant|
| - Mixing  | | - Valve   | | - To     |
| - Wall    | |           | |   exhaust|
|   quench  | |           | |          |
+-----+-----+ +-----+-----+ +-----+-----+
      |             |              |
      v             v              v
     BSFC          BSFC           BSFC
      |             |              |
      +------+------+--------------+
             |
             v
     +---------------+
     |   U-SHAPED    |
     |   BSFC vs N   |
     |   CURVE       |
     +---------------+
             |
             v
        N_econ (minimum)
```

**Detailed Factors (3 marks):**

1. **Combustion Duration:** Faster combustion favours higher speed; at low speeds, prolonged exposure to walls quenches the flame, raising BSFC.

2. **Mechanical Friction:** Friction power grows approximately as $N^{1.5}$. Higher speeds mean higher friction → higher BSFC at high N.

3. **Pumping Loss:** The throttle (SI) or governor (CI) creates a pressure drop. At high speeds, pumping work increases as $N^2$, raising BSFC.

4. **Heat Loss:** Cycle heat loss to coolant and exhaust is higher at low speeds (more wall-contact time per cycle).

5. **Volumetric Efficiency:** Drops at very high speeds due to valve flow restrictions, reducing air charge and raising BSFC.

6. **Mixture Preparation Time:** At very high speeds, the air-fuel mixture may not fully form, causing incomplete combustion and higher BSFC.

**Variation of Economic Speed with Load (1 mark):**

- At **lower loads**, the engine operates with a more closed throttle, increasing pumping losses. The economic speed tends to shift to **lower rpm**.
- At **higher loads**, the throttle is more open, pumping losses are less significant, and the economic speed tends to shift to **higher rpm**.
- A family of BSFC vs. Speed curves (one for each load) can be plotted, and the locus of their minima forms the "**Best Economy Line**" used by ECU calibrators.

---

**(b) In an economic speed test on a 4-stroke petrol engine running at 1500 rpm, the following data were recorded: Net load on brake = 40 N, effective brake drum diameter = 0.6 m, fuel consumption = 8 ml in 20 seconds, $\rho_f$ = 0.74 kg/litre. Calculate (i) BP, (ii) BSFC, and (iii) Brake Thermal Efficiency. Take CV = 44,000 kJ/kg. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $N = 1500$ rpm
- $W = 40$ N
- $D = 0.6$ m, so $R = 0.3$ m
- $V = 8$ ml, $t_f = 20$ s
- $\rho_f = 0.74$ kg/litre
- $CV = 44{,}000$ kJ/kg

**Step 1: Brake Torque**

$$T_b = W \times R = 40 \times 0.3 = 12 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2: Brake Power**

$$BP = \frac{2\pi N T_b}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500 \times 12}{60 \times 1000}$$

$$BP = \frac{113097}{60000} = 1.885 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Mass flow rate of fuel**

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{V \times \rho_f}{1000 \times t_f} = \frac{8 \times 0.74}{1000 \times 20} = \frac{5.92}{20000} = 2.96 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 4: BSFC**

$$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f \times 3600}{BP} = \frac{2.96 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600}{1.885}$$

$$BSFC = \frac{1.0656}{1.885} = 0.5654 \text{ kg/kW·hr}$$

> **Note:** This BSFC value of 0.565 kg/kW·hr is **unrealistically low** for a small petrol engine (real values: 0.35–0.50). The dataset is purely illustrative for exam methodology.

**Step 5: Brake Thermal Efficiency**

Heat input rate = $\dot{m}_f \times CV = 2.96 \times 10^{-4} \times 44{,}000 = 13.024$ kJ/s = 13.024 kW.

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \times CV} = \frac{1.885}{13.024} = 0.1447 = 14.47\%$$

**Final Results:**
$$\boxed{BP = 1.885 \text{ kW}, \quad BSFC = 0.565 \text{ kg/kW·hr}, \quad \eta_{bth} = 14.47\%}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Points in KTU Valuation (Thermal Engineering Lab EST):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert kgf to N:** Spring balances typically read in kgf, but brake power formulas require force in Newtons. Multiplying the kgf reading by $g = 9.81$ is **mandatory** — a 1-mark deduction if missed.
>
> 2. **Wrong time unit in BSFC formula:** Fuel time $t_f$ may be given in seconds OR minutes. The formula changes accordingly. Mixing units is the **#1 mistake** in KTU lab viva and exam — costs 1–2 marks.
>
> 3. **Ignoring rope thickness:** Many students use $R = D/2$ instead of $R = (D+d)/2$. A 0.5-mark deduction is typical.
>
> 4. **Drawing a monotonically increasing/decreasing BSFC curve:** The curve **must be U-shaped** with a clearly identified minimum. A linear or monotonic curve is treated as a **conceptual error** — up to 2 marks deducted.
>
> 5. **Not specifying load for economic speed:** Economic speed is **load-dependent**. Stating "The economic speed is 1500 rpm" without mentioning the load is incomplete — 0.5–1 mark deduction.
>
> 6. **Confusing Morse Test with Economic Speed Test:** Morse test stops cylinders one at a time to find friction power of a multi-cylinder engine. Economic speed test varies speed at constant load. Mixing the two procedures is a **2-mark conceptual error**.
>
> 7. **Forgetting to mention warm-up:** Engine must reach steady-state temperature before recording data. Not mentioning this in the procedure loses 0.5 marks.
>
> 8. **No mention of burette calibration / meniscus reading error:** In the lab viva, "How do you minimize error in fuel volume measurement?" expects answers about vertical burette, eye-level meniscus reading, repeated trials and averaging.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Economic Speed Test on IC Engines**

- **Objective:** Determine the engine speed at which **BSFC is minimum** for a given load.
- **BSFC Definition:** $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \times 3600}{BP}$ in **kg/kW·hr**.
- **BP Formula (Rope Brake):** $BP = \dfrac{2\pi N T_b}{60 \times 1000}$ kW, where $T_b = W \times R$ N·m.
- **Effective Radius:** $R = (D + d)/2$, where $D$ = drum diameter, $d$ = rope diameter.
- **Mass Fuel Flow:** $\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{V \times \rho_f}{1000 \times t_f}$ kg/s.
- **Curve Shape:** BSFC vs. Speed is **U-shaped** with a clear minimum.
- **Economic Speed is Load-Dependent:** It is **NOT** a fixed engine property; it shifts with load.
- **Economic Speed ≠ Maximum Power Speed:** These are two different operating points.
- **Petrol density:** $\rho_f \approx 0.74 \text{ kg/L}$; **Diesel density:** $\rho_f \approx 0.85 \text{ kg/L}$.
- **CV (Calorific Value):** Petrol ≈ 44,000 kJ/kg; Diesel ≈ 43,000 kJ/kg.
- **Inertia Correction:** Required when speed is not perfectly steady; $BP_{corrected} = BP \pm I\alpha\omega$.
- **Low-Speed Losses:** Combustion incompleteness, wall heat loss → BSFC rises.
- **High-Speed Losses:** Friction ($\propto N^{1.5}$), pumping loss ($\propto N^2$) → BSFC rises.
- **Practical Use:** Gearbox design, ECU calibration, hybrid energy management, generator set rating.
- **Lab Procedure Key Points:** Warm-up → fix load → vary speed → record ($W_d, W_l, t, V, t_f$) → calculate BSFC → plot → identify minimum.
- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Confusing Economic Speed Test with Morse Test or Retardation Test.
- **Sign Convention for Spring Balances:** $W = W_d - W_l$ (assume $W_d > W_l$).
- **Units Consistency:** Always check $V$ in ml vs. litres, $t_f$ in s vs. min, $W$ in N vs. kgf.
- **Graph Requirement in KTU Exam:** Must plot BSFC vs. Speed with **all data points**, label axes with units, mark the minimum clearly, and state $N_{econ}$ explicitly.
- **Brake Thermal Efficiency:** $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{BP \times 3600}{\dot{m}_f \times CV}$ — must accompany BSFC in comprehensive answers.
- **Typical BSFC Range (SI engines):** 0.35–0.50 kg/kW·hr; **(CI engines):** 0.25–0.40 kg/kW·hr.
- **Best Economy Line:** Locus of minimum BSFC points across all loads — used in modern EMS calibration.
- **Engine Warm-Up Time:** 5–10 minutes minimum; oil and water thermometers must reach steady state.
- **Burette Reading Accuracy:** Use lower meniscus, eye level, repeat 2–3 times and average.
- **Modern Relevance:** This experiment directly relates to **BSFC mapping for hybrid and electric vehicle supervisory control algorithms**.

---

> **END OF NOTES — Economic Speed Test on IC Engines | KTU 2024 Scheme | Module 2 | PCAUL408**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Retardation Test on IC Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL408 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Retardation Test on IC Engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Retardation Test** (also known as the **Motoring Test** or **Running-Down Test**) is a laboratory procedure employed to determine the **Friction Power (FP)** of an Internal Combustion (IC) engine by operating the engine as a non-firing machine and analyzing its natural rate of speed decay due to internal frictional resistance.

In strict KTU 2024 scheme terminology, the retardation test is defined as:

> A deceleration-based indirect test in which the engine is brought to a steady rated speed, the fuel supply is abruptly cut off, and the rate of angular deceleration (dω/dt) of the rotating mass (flywheel + crankshaft assembly) is recorded as a function of time. The work done against the rotating inertia during deceleration is equated to the frictional work lost per unit time, yielding the **Friction Power**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Equation Governing the Retardation Test:**
> The entire mathematical foundation of this experiment rests on the energy balance:
> $$\text{Friction Power (FP)} = I \cdot \omega \cdot \alpha$$
> where $I$ = moment of inertia of the flywheel (kg·m²), $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s), and $\alpha$ = angular deceleration (rad/s²).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Spinning Top That Stops

Imagine a **spinning top** on a flat table. The moment you stop winding it (cut off the "fuel"), it doesn't stop instantly. It continues to spin, but progressively slows down until it stops. Why? Because friction in its bearing, and air resistance, gradually drains its rotational kinetic energy.

Now, scale this idea up to a massive **IC engine flywheel**. The engine is "wound up" to its rated speed using fuel combustion. When you cut off the fuel (like stopping to wind the top), the only thing draining the rotational kinetic energy of the flywheel-crankshaft assembly is the **internal mechanical friction** of the engine. By precisely measuring *how fast* the engine slows down, we can calculate how much power the friction is absorbing. This absorbed power is, by definition, the **Friction Power** of the engine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Insight for KTU Board Exams:**
> The retardation test works because, during deceleration, the engine is **completely un-powered**. The only force resisting motion is internal friction. Therefore, the rate at which kinetic energy is lost equals the rate at which friction dissipates energy, which is the friction power.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Density of cast iron (flywheel)**: $\rho_{CI} = 7200 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Density of steel (flywheel/rotor)**: $\rho_{steel} = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Gravitational acceleration**: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Standard rated speeds**: $N_{rated}$ for small IC engines typically **1500 rpm** (4-stroke SI) or **3000 rpm** (2-stroke SI). Multi-cylinder CI engines: **750–1500 rpm**.
- **Recommended speed intervals for observation**: Typically, deceleration is recorded from rated speed to about **half the rated speed** in equal time intervals of **5 to 10 seconds** for smooth graphical differentiation.

## 1.4 Friction Power in the Wider Power-Balance Framework

To firmly position the retardation test within KTU's thermal engineering framework, recall the **power balance** of an IC engine:

$$\text{Indicated Power (IP)} = \text{Brake Power (BP)} + \text{Friction Power (FP)}$$

And the **Mechanical Efficiency**:

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{\text{BP}}{\text{IP}} = 1 - \frac{\text{FP}}{\text{IP}}$$

The retardation test is the *only* direct experimental method (within the B.Tech lab curriculum) for measuring FP. The **Willan's Line Method** is the more common alternative, but the retardation test is indispensable when the engine is not coupled to a dynamometer suitable for Willan's line extrapolation.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Board Answers:** Always state the equation $\text{IP} = \text{BP} + \text{FP}$ as a preamble whenever the retardation test is being used to determine either IP or $\eta_{mech}$. Examiners award marks for showing the conceptual linkage.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Physical Reasoning

The retardation test is built upon the **Work-Energy Theorem** applied to a rotating rigid body. Let us dissect its operational logic into structured logical steps:

### Step 1: Establish Steady-State Rated Speed
- The engine is first run at its rated speed $N_{rated}$ (in rpm) under no-load conditions.
- The fuel is adjusted to maintain this speed for a short period, ensuring the rotating assembly is at thermal and rotational equilibrium.
- **Why?** Any transient fluctuations in speed will contaminate the deceleration data with non-frictional effects (combustion irregularities, governor hunting).

### Step 2: Cut Off the Fuel Supply
- The fuel injection pump (CI engine) or carburetor (SI engine) is abruptly disengaged.
- Simultaneously, the ignition system (SI) is also switched off, ensuring the engine is **completely non-firing**.
- **Why?** After this moment, the only torque acting on the crankshaft is the friction torque $T_f$ opposing rotation.

### Step 3: Record Speed Decay vs. Time
- A **tachometer** (or digital RPM sensor) records the engine speed at fixed time intervals.
- Typical data points: every 5 to 10 seconds, from $N_{rated}$ down to roughly $0.5 \cdot N_{rated}$.
- A **stopwatch** is used for timing, and the data is tabulated as $(t_i, N_i)$.

### Step 4: Plot the N vs. t Curve
- The recorded data is plotted as **engine speed (N) on the Y-axis** vs. **time (t) on the X-axis**.
- The curve is **monotonically decreasing** and slightly concave (deceleration is roughly linear over small intervals).
- A **tangent** is drawn at the rated speed point to find the initial slope $\left(\frac{dN}{dt}\right)_{N=N_{rated}}$.

### Step 5: Convert dN/dt to Angular Deceleration
- The slope obtained is in units of (rpm)/s.
- Convert to **rad/s²** using the chain of unit conversions:
  - $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ rad/s
  - Therefore: $\dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = \dfrac{2\pi}{60} \cdot \dfrac{dN}{dt} = \dfrac{\pi}{30} \cdot \dfrac{dN}{dt}$

### Step 6: Equate Energy Balance to Friction Power
- The rate of loss of kinetic energy of the rotating assembly equals the frictional power dissipation:
  $$\text{Friction Power (FP)} = T_f \cdot \omega = I \cdot \alpha \cdot \omega$$
  where $T_f$ is the friction torque, $I$ is the moment of inertia, $\alpha = d\omega/dt$ is the angular deceleration, and $\omega$ is the instantaneous angular velocity.
- The **product $\omega \cdot \alpha$** is evaluated at the rated speed (initial point of the curve) for a representative FP value.

## 2.2 Determining the Moment of Inertia (I)

A critical prerequisite for the retardation test is the **moment of inertia of the flywheel (I)**. KTU expects students to know how to calculate this for a solid disc-type flywheel:

$$I = \frac{M \cdot R^2}{2}$$

where:
- $M$ = mass of the flywheel (kg)
- $R$ = outer radius of the flywheel (m)

If the flywheel has a **rim-and-spoke construction** (rim of radius R, hub of radius r, mass concentrated in rim), a more accurate formula is:

$$I = M \cdot \left(\frac{R^2 + r^2}{2}\right)$$

For a **solid cylindrical flywheel** (most common in KTU labs):

$$I = \frac{\pi \cdot \rho \cdot b \cdot R^4}{2}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = density of flywheel material (kg/m³)
- $b$ = thickness of the flywheel (m)
- $R$ = outer radius (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The moment of inertia **must be determined by a separate swinging-pendulum experiment** OR **calculated from flywheel dimensions and density** before the retardation test can yield FP. A common error is to forget the units of I (kg·m², not kg·m).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbolic Meaning | Standard Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I = \dfrac{M \cdot R^2}{2}$ | Moment of inertia of solid disc flywheel | kg·m² | Given flywheel mass and radius |
| 2 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Angular velocity conversion | rad/s | Converting rpm to rad/s |
| 3 | $\alpha = \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = \dfrac{\pi}{30} \cdot \dfrac{dN}{dt}$ | Angular deceleration | rad/s² | From N-t graph slope |
| 4 | $\text{FP} = I \cdot \omega \cdot \alpha$ | Friction power | W (or kW) | Core retardation test equation |
| 5 | $\text{FP (kW)} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot I \cdot N \cdot \left(\frac{dN}{dt}\right)}{60 \cdot 1000}$ | Friction power in kW | kW | Direct numerical substitution |
| 6 | $\text{IP} = \text{BP} + \text{FP}$ | Indicated power balance | kW | Computing IP from BP and FP |
| 7 | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{\text{BP}}{\text{IP}} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{FP}}{\text{IP}}$ | Mechanical efficiency | dimensionless (or %) | Final answer of the experiment |
| 8 | $\text{FP}_{avg} = \dfrac{I \cdot \omega_1 \cdot \omega_2 \cdot (\omega_1 - \omega_2)}{2 \cdot t \cdot (\omega_1 + \omega_2)}$ | Average FP over a speed range | kW | When using the integrated energy form over speed interval |
| 9 | $T_f = I \cdot \alpha$ | Friction torque | N·m | For plot of friction torque vs. speed |
| 10 | $K.E. = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ | Rotational kinetic energy | J | Energy balance reasoning |

> [!TIP]
> **Substitution Note for Table Row 8:** In the integrated form, $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$ are the angular velocities at the start and end of the observed speed interval, and $t$ is the time taken for the speed to fall from $\omega_1$ to $\omega_2$. This is often the form used when only *two* speed-time points are recorded (a "two-point retardation test").

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The retardation test, despite its limitations, holds real engineering importance:

1. **Industrial Engine Diagnostics**: In heavy diesel gensets and marine engines, FP estimation via the running-down test helps in **scheduled maintenance** decisions. A sudden increase in FP over time signals bearing wear, piston ring degradation, or lubrication breakdown.

2. **Automotive R&D**: Manufacturers like Tata Motors and Mahindra use retardation-style coast-down tests (in chassis dynamometers) to estimate driveline friction, which is a key contributor to **fuel economy (km/litre)**.

3. **Emission Modeling**: Friction power directly influences part-load specific fuel consumption. Software like **GT-SUITE** and **AVL CRUISE** require validated FP curves as inputs for emissions and BSFC prediction.

4. **Governing the Mechanical Efficiency**: Without a reliable FP value, mechanical efficiency cannot be determined, and the BSFC (Brake Specific Fuel Consumption) cannot be reported as Indicated BSFC. This affects all downstream thermal-efficiency calculations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Core Friction Power Equation

We begin with the **Work-Energy Theorem** for a rotating rigid body. Consider the flywheel-crankshaft assembly rotating at angular velocity $\omega$. Its rotational kinetic energy is:

$$K.E. = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$$

During deceleration, the kinetic energy is being drained by the friction torque $T_f$. The instantaneous power dissipated by friction is:

$$P_{friction} = T_f \cdot \omega$$

By Newton's second law for rotation, the friction torque equals the moment of inertia times angular deceleration:

$$T_f = I \cdot \alpha = I \cdot \frac{d\omega}{dt}$$

Substituting into the friction power equation:

$$P_{friction} = I \cdot \frac{d\omega}{dt} \cdot \omega$$

Since $P_{friction} = \text{Friction Power (FP)}$:

$$\boxed{\text{FP} = I \cdot \omega \cdot \frac{d\omega}{dt} = I \cdot \omega \cdot \alpha}$$

This derivation confirms the fundamental relation. **Each term in this equation has explicit units:**

$$[I] \cdot [\omega] \cdot [\alpha] = (\text{kg·m}^2) \cdot (\text{rad/s}) \cdot (\text{rad/s}^2) = \text{kg·m}^2\text{/s}^3 = \text{W}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The radian is a dimensionless unit** in SI, but is conventionally retained in equations to indicate "per radian of angular displacement." This is why we sometimes express FP in W/(rad²/s³) — though practically it is just W.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: From (dN/dt) to Numerical Friction Power in kW

For practical data, the experimenter records $N$ in rpm, so the slope from the graph is $\left(\frac{dN}{dt}\right)$ in rpm/s. We must convert this to $\frac{d\omega}{dt}$ in rad/s².

Starting from the conversion:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{\pi N}{30}$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to time $t$ (treating $\pi$ and 30 as constants):

$$\frac{d\omega}{dt} = \frac{\pi}{30} \cdot \frac{dN}{dt} = \alpha$$

Substituting into the FP equation:

$$\text{FP} = I \cdot \omega \cdot \alpha = I \cdot \left(\frac{\pi N}{30}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{\pi}{30} \cdot \frac{dN}{dt}\right)$$

$$\text{FP} = I \cdot \frac{\pi^2 N}{900} \cdot \frac{dN}{dt}$$

To express FP in **kilowatts (kW)**, divide by 1000:

$$\boxed{\text{FP (kW)} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot I \cdot N \cdot \left(\frac{dN}{dt}\right)}{900 \times 1000}}$$

Or equivalently, using the more commonly written form:

$$\text{FP (kW)} = \frac{2\pi \cdot I \cdot N \cdot \left(\frac{dN}{dt}\right)}{60 \times 1000}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Numerical Consistency Check:** If $I = 5$ kg·m², $N = 1500$ rpm, and $dN/dt = -30$ rpm/s (a typical deceleration), then:
> $$\text{FP} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 5 \cdot 1500 \cdot 30}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{1413716.7}{60000} \approx 23.56 \text{ kW}$$
> This is a realistic FP value for a mid-sized 4-stroke diesel engine at rated conditions.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Integrated Form for Two-Point Method

If only two readings of speed and time are taken (a "two-point retardation test"), the average friction power over the speed drop from $N_1$ to $N_2$ in time $t$ can be derived as follows:

The energy lost from the rotating mass during deceleration:

$$\Delta K.E. = \frac{1}{2} I (\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)$$

The average power dissipated as friction (assuming $\omega \cdot \alpha$ approximately constant over the small interval):

$$\text{FP}_{avg} = \frac{\Delta K.E.}{t} = \frac{I (\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)}{2 t}$$

Factoring the difference of squares:

$$\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2 = (\omega_1 - \omega_2)(\omega_1 + \omega_2)$$

So:

$$\text{FP}_{avg} = \frac{I (\omega_1 - \omega_2)(\omega_1 + \omega_2)}{2 t}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Numerical Answers:** When only two points are given, use this integrated form. The two-point method is **faster** but less accurate than the full N-t curve method. Always state which method is being used.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Analytical Matrix Format)

> **Problem:** A 4-stroke single-cylinder diesel engine flywheel has mass $M = 50$ kg and outer radius $R = 0.25$ m. During a retardation test, the engine decelerates from 1500 rpm to 1400 rpm in 8 seconds. The brake power measured at the rated speed (by a separate dynamometer test) is 6.5 kW. Calculate the Friction Power, Indicated Power, and Mechanical Efficiency.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Calculate the moment of inertia of the solid disc flywheel.**

$$I = \frac{M \cdot R^2}{2} = \frac{50 \times (0.25)^2}{2} = \frac{50 \times 0.0625}{2} = \frac{3.125}{2} = 1.5625 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

**Step 2: Convert the average speed to angular velocity.**

$$N_{avg} = \frac{1500 + 1400}{2} = 1450 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\omega_{avg} = \frac{2\pi \times 1450}{60} = 151.84 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the angular deceleration.**

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = \frac{1400 - 1500}{8} = -12.5 \text{ rpm/s}$$

$$\alpha = \frac{\pi}{30} \cdot \frac{dN}{dt} = \frac{\pi}{30} \times (-12.5) = -1.309 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

The negative sign indicates deceleration; for FP calculation, we use the magnitude.

**Step 4: Calculate Friction Power.**

$$\text{FP} = I \cdot \omega_{avg} \cdot |\alpha| = 1.5625 \times 151.84 \times 1.309$$

$$\text{FP} = 310.5 \text{ W} \approx 0.3105 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Indicated Power.**

$$\text{IP} = \text{BP} + \text{FP} = 6.5 + 0.3105 = 6.8105 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6: Calculate Mechanical Efficiency.**

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{\text{BP}}{\text{IP}} = \frac{6.5}{6.8105} = 0.9544 = 95.44\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Discussion of Result:** A mechanical efficiency of 95.44% is **realistic for a small single-cylinder diesel engine at rated load** but slightly on the high side. A value in the 85–92% range is more typical of worn engines. The high value here suggests a well-maintained test rig with low bearing friction and good lubrication.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Methods for Finding FP

| Method | Equipment Required | Accuracy | KTU Exam Frequency | Key Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Retardation Test** | Tachometer, stopwatch, flywheel mass data | Moderate (~5–8% error) | **Very High** (frequently asked) | Engine unloaded; friction differs from loaded condition |
| **Willan's Line Method** | Dynamometer, fuel measurement rig | High (~2–3% error) | High | Requires extrapolation of BSFC line; small engines give ambiguous results |
| **Morse Test** | Multi-cylinder engine, individual cylinder cutoff | High for multi-cylinder | Moderate (asked for multi-cyl) | Only applicable to multi-cylinder engines |
| **Indicator Diagram Method** | Mechanical/electronic engine indicator | High | Moderate | Requires specialized indicator; not always available |
| **Dead-Weight Friction Brake** | Prony brake or rope brake | Low to Moderate | Low | Measures only BP, not FP directly |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Retardation Test Setup

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of the retardation test setup, mapping the interactions between the engine, the measurement instruments, and the data-processing stages. This is a block-level functional architecture flow, as the physical schematic of an engine test cell is too complex for Mermaid to render directly.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Tank and Supply Line] --> B[IC Engine Under Test]
    C[Ignition or Injection System] --> B
    B --> D[Flywheel-Crankshaft Rotating Assembly]
    D --> E[Tachometer RPM Sensor]
    F[Stopwatch / Data Logger] --> G[Time-Speed Data Table]
    E --> G
    G --> H[N vs t Curve Plot]
    H --> I[Tangent Slope at Rated Speed]
    I --> J[Compute Angular Deceleration alpha]
    K[Flywheel Mass and Radius Data] --> L[Compute Moment of Inertia I]
    L --> J
    J --> M[Apply FP equals I times omega times alpha]
    B --> N[Brake Dynamometer for BP measurement]
    N --> O[BP value in kW]
    M --> P[Indicated Power IP equals BP plus FP]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Mechanical Efficiency eta_mech equals BP over IP]
    Q --> R[Final KTU Report Tabulation]
```

## 4.2 Logical Sequence Flowchart of the Experimental Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Start engine and warm up to operating temperature] --> S2[Step 2: Adjust fuel to reach rated speed N_rated]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Maintain steady state for 2 to 3 minutes]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Record BP using dynamometer at rated load]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Cut off fuel and ignition simultaneously]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Start stopwatch as fuel is cut]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Record speed at every 5 or 10 second interval]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Continue until speed drops to about half of N_rated]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Tabulate t and N data]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Plot N vs t graph and draw tangent at N_rated]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11: Find slope dN per dt from tangent]
    S11 --> S12[Step 12: Compute I from flywheel mass and radius]
    S12 --> S13[Step 13: Calculate FP using retardation formula]
    S13 --> S14[Step 14: Calculate IP and eta_mech]
    S14 --> S15[Step 15: Report and tabulate results]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Data Plot — Speed Decay vs. Time

While Mermaid cannot render a true Cartesian plot, the following diagram symbolically represents the **expected shape of the N-t curve** and the **tangent-drawing technique** used to find the slope at the rated speed.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Origin t=0, N=0] --> P2[Time increases to the right]
    P3[Rated speed N_rated at t=0] --> P4[Speed decreases monotonically]
    P5[Tangent line drawn at initial point] --> P6[Slope dN per dt is negative]
    P7[Curve is concave up] --> P8[Slope magnitude increases with time]
    P9[Observation interval] --> P10[Multiple data points for accurate tangent]
    P11[Half rated speed at later time] --> P12[End of useful data range]
```

## 4.4 Component Identification Diagram (Textual Block Diagram)

The retardation test setup has the following key physical components, shown here as a labeled block diagram:

| Block Label | Component | Function in the Retardation Test |
|---|---|---|
| ENG | IC Engine (4-stroke SI or CI) | The machine under test |
| FW | Flywheel | Stores rotational kinetic energy; its inertia I is critical |
| TACH | Tachometer | Measures instantaneous engine speed in rpm |
| SW | Stopwatch | Measures time intervals for the speed data |
| DYNO | Dynamometer (separate test) | Measures BP at rated load (not part of retardation itself) |
| FUELSYS | Fuel injection pump / carburetor | Cut off to make engine non-firing |
| IGNSYS | Spark plug / ignition system | Switched off to ensure no combustion |
| DATA | Data table and N-t plot | Stores and processes the experimental data |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Short-Answer Conceptual Question

**Q: Define the retardation test on an IC engine. Why is it also called the motoring test or running-down test?** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **retardation test** is an experimental method to determine the **friction power** of an IC engine by measuring the rate of deceleration of the rotating assembly after the fuel supply is cut off.

It is called the **motoring test** because, after the fuel is cut off, the engine continues to "motor" (rotate) on its own kinetic energy — it acts like a freely rotating machine rather than a power-producing one. It is also called the **running-down test** because the engine is observed as it "runs down" from rated speed to a lower speed under the influence of friction alone.

During this running-down phase, the only torque resisting rotation is the **friction torque** of the engine. By measuring the rate of speed decay and the moment of inertia of the flywheel, the friction power can be calculated as **FP = I·ω·α**.

*(1 mark for correct definition, 1 mark for naming both alternative terms with reasoning, 1 mark for core FP equation.)*

---

### Question A2: Short-Answer Conceptual Question

**Q: List any three limitations or disadvantages of the retardation test.** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Engine is tested under no-load condition:** The friction losses measured during deceleration occur when the engine is unloaded. In reality, the engine operates under load, where the in-cylinder pressures, bearing loads, and piston ring forces are different. Therefore, the measured FP may not accurately represent the friction at full-load operating conditions.

2. **Multi-cylinder engine firing interference:** In a multi-cylinder engine, even after fuel cutoff, residual heat in the cylinders may cause **after-firing** in some cylinders. This injects extra energy into the rotating assembly and reduces the apparent deceleration, leading to **underestimation of friction power**.

3. **Accurate measurement of flywheel moment of inertia is required:** The experiment critically depends on an accurate value of $I$. Any error in measuring $I$ (e.g., assuming a uniform solid disc when the flywheel has rim-spoke geometry) propagates directly into the calculated FP.

4. **Manual timing introduces human error:** Using a stopwatch to record speed intervals introduces reaction-time errors, which can be significant at higher deceleration rates.

*(Any three limitations — 1 mark each.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Common mistakes students make in retardation test numericals:
> 1. **Unit mismatch**: Using $I$ in g·m² instead of kg·m² (a 1000× error!), or using $N$ in rps instead of rpm (a 60× error).
> 2. **Forgetting the negative sign in deceleration** — the formula needs only the *magnitude* of $\alpha$, but the sign of $dN/dt$ is negative.
> 3. **Using the final speed instead of the rated speed** in the FP equation. The tangent slope at the *initial* (rated) speed is what gives the correct FP at rated conditions.
> 4. **Confusing mass density with mass** — the formula $I = (\pi \rho b R^4)/2$ is a common trap; use it only when mass is *not* directly given.
> 5. **Not converting to kW** — examiners deduct 0.5 mark if the answer is in watts when kW is expected.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the equation for the **friction power** of an IC engine as determined from the retardation test. Clearly state the assumptions made during the derivation. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A 4-stroke single-cylinder diesel engine has a flywheel of mass **80 kg** and radius of gyration **0.20 m**. In a retardation test, the speed drops from **1800 rpm to 1700 rpm** in **5 seconds**. The brake power of the engine at rated load is **8.0 kW**. Calculate: **(i)** the moment of inertia of the flywheel, **(ii)** the angular deceleration, **(iii)** the friction power, **(iv)** the indicated power, and **(v)** the mechanical efficiency. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B-A

**Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The engine is a single-cylinder unit with **no after-firing** after fuel cutoff.
2. The friction torque is assumed **constant** over the small speed range considered.
3. The rotating mass is concentrated in the **flywheel**, so a single $I$ is sufficient.
4. The only torque acting on the crankshaft after fuel cutoff is the **friction torque** $T_f$.

**Derivation:**

Let the rotating assembly (flywheel + crankshaft) have moment of inertia $I$ (kg·m²) and rotate at angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s).

The **rotational kinetic energy** of the assembly is:

$$K.E. = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$$

During deceleration, this kinetic energy decreases with time as friction dissipates it. Differentiating:

$$\frac{d(K.E.)}{dt} = I \omega \cdot \frac{d\omega}{dt} = I \omega \alpha$$

By the **Work-Energy Theorem**, the rate of loss of kinetic energy equals the power dissipated by friction:

$$\text{FP} = -\frac{d(K.E.)}{dt} = I \omega \alpha$$

Since $T_f = I \alpha$ (Newton's second law for rotation):

$$\text{FP} = T_f \cdot \omega$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\text{FP} = I \cdot \omega \cdot \alpha}$$

where $\alpha = d\omega/dt$ is the angular deceleration evaluated at the rated speed. *(Full marks: 2 marks for assumptions, 3 marks for derivation steps, 2 marks for final boxed equation with all symbols defined.)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 marks]:**

**Given:**
- Mass of flywheel: $M = 80$ kg
- Radius of gyration: $k = 0.20$ m
- Speed range: $N_1 = 1800$ rpm to $N_2 = 1700$ rpm
- Time for deceleration: $t = 5$ s
- Brake power: $\text{BP} = 8.0$ kW

**(i) Moment of Inertia of the flywheel [1 mark]:**

$$I = M \cdot k^2 = 80 \times (0.20)^2 = 80 \times 0.04 = 3.2 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

**(ii) Angular Deceleration [1.5 marks]:**

Average speed:

$$N_{avg} = \frac{1800 + 1700}{2} = 1750 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\omega_{avg} = \frac{2\pi \times 1750}{60} = 183.26 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = \frac{1700 - 1800}{5} = -20 \text{ rpm/s}$$

$$\alpha = \frac{\pi}{30} \cdot \frac{dN}{dt} = \frac{\pi}{30} \times (-20) = -2.094 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

Magnitude: $|\alpha| = 2.094$ rad/s²

**(iii) Friction Power [1.5 marks]:**

Using $\text{FP} = I \cdot \omega \cdot \alpha$ (with magnitudes):

$$\text{FP} = 3.2 \times 183.26 \times 2.094 = 1227.7 \text{ W} \approx 1.228 \text{ kW}$$

**Alternative direct formula in kW:**

$$\text{FP (kW)} = \frac{2\pi \cdot I \cdot N \cdot \left(\frac{dN}{dt}\right)}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{2\pi \times 3.2 \times 1750 \times 20}{60 \times 1000} = 1.227 \text{ kW}$$

Both methods yield the same answer. ✓

**(iv) Indicated Power [1 mark]:**

$$\text{IP} = \text{BP} + \text{FP} = 8.0 + 1.228 = 9.228 \text{ kW}$$

**(v) Mechanical Efficiency [1 mark]:**

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{\text{BP}}{\text{IP}} = \frac{8.0}{9.228} = 0.8669 = 86.69\%$$

**Final Tabulated Result:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Moment of Inertia $I$ | 3.2 kg·m² |
| Angular Deceleration $\alpha$ | 2.094 rad/s² |
| Friction Power FP | 1.228 kW |
| Indicated Power IP | 9.228 kW |
| Mechanical Efficiency $\eta_{mech}$ | 86.69% |

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **complete procedure** of the retardation test on a 4-stroke IC engine in the laboratory. List the **observations** to be taken, the **graph** to be plotted, and the **formulae** to be used. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** During a retardation test on a 4-stroke, 4-cylinder petrol engine, the following speed-time data was recorded after the fuel was cut off:

| Time (s) | Speed (rpm) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 2400 |
| 10 | 2280 |
| 20 | 2140 |
| 30 | 1980 |
| 40 | 1800 |
| 50 | 1600 |
| 60 | 1380 |

The flywheel moment of inertia is **2.5 kg·m²**. Calculate the friction power at the **rated speed** of 2400 rpm. The brake power at rated load is **22 kW**. Also compute the indicated power and mechanical efficiency. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B-B

**Part (a) — Procedure, Observations, and Formulae [7 marks]:**

**Apparatus Required:** IC engine test rig with a flywheel of known moment of inertia, tachometer, stopwatch, dynamometer (for separate BP measurement), fuel cutoff lever.

**Procedure (step-by-step):**

1. **Pre-test preparation:**
   - Measure the **mass** and **radius** (or radius of gyration) of the flywheel to determine $I$.
   - Check fuel, lubricating oil, and cooling water levels.

2. **Warm-up:**
   - Start the engine and allow it to warm up to its normal operating temperature at no-load.

3. **Steady-state operation:**
   - Adjust the fuel to bring the engine to its **rated speed** $N_{rated}$.
   - Maintain this speed for 2 to 3 minutes to ensure steady state.
   - Measure the **brake power** using a dynamometer at rated load (a separate test or simultaneous run).

4. **Retardation phase:**
   - **Simultaneously** cut off the fuel supply and switch off the ignition.
   - Start the stopwatch at the moment of cutoff.
   - Record the engine speed at **regular intervals of 5 or 10 seconds** as it decelerates.
   - Continue recording until the speed has dropped to approximately **half the rated speed** (or until the engine nearly stops).

5. **Data tabulation:**
   - Tabulate the observations as $(t_i, N_i)$ pairs.

6. **Graphical analysis:**
   - Plot **N (rpm) on Y-axis** vs. **t (s) on X-axis**.
   - Draw a **smooth curve** through the data points.
   - At the **initial point** $(0, N_{rated})$, draw a **tangent** to the curve.
   - Determine the **slope** of this tangent: $\dfrac{dN}{dt}\bigg|_{N=N_{rated}}$ in rpm/s.

7. **Calculation:**
   - Compute angular velocity at rated speed: $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N_{rated}}{60}$.
   - Compute angular deceleration: $\alpha = \dfrac{\pi}{30} \cdot \dfrac{dN}{dt}$.
   - Apply the retardation formula: $\text{FP} = I \cdot \omega \cdot \alpha$.
   - Compute $\text{IP} = \text{BP} + \text{FP}$.
   - Compute $\eta_{mech} = \text{BP}/\text{IP}$.

**Observations Table Template:**

| Time $t$ (s) | Speed $N$ (rpm) |
|---|---|
| 0 | $N_{rated}$ |
| 5 | ... |
| 10 | ... |
| ... | ... |

**Graph:** $N$ vs. $t$ — a smooth, monotonically decreasing curve.

**Key Formulae:**

$$I = M k^2, \quad \omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}, \quad \alpha = \frac{\pi}{30} \cdot \frac{dN}{dt}, \quad \text{FP} = I \omega \alpha$$

$$\text{IP} = \text{BP} + \text{FP}, \quad \eta_{mech} = \frac{\text{BP}}{\text{IP}}$$

*(Marks distribution: 3 marks for procedure, 2 marks for observations table and graph, 2 marks for formulae.)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 marks]:**

**Given:**
- $I = 2.5$ kg·m²
- $N_{rated} = 2400$ rpm
- $\text{BP} = 22$ kW

**Step 1: Plot the N-t curve and find the slope at $t = 0$.**

From the data table, the speed at $t = 0$ is 2400 rpm and at $t = 10$ s it is 2280 rpm. To accurately find the slope at the initial point, we use **two-point numerical differentiation** of the first segment:

$$\frac{dN}{dt}\bigg|_{t=0} \approx \frac{N(10) - N(0)}{10 - 0} = \frac{2280 - 2400}{10} = -12 \text{ rpm/s}$$

**Step 2: Convert the slope to angular deceleration.**

$$\alpha = \frac{\pi}{30} \cdot \left(\frac{dN}{dt}\right) = \frac{\pi}{30} \times (-12) = -1.257 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

**Step 3: Compute angular velocity at rated speed.**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 2400}{60} = 251.33 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4: Compute Friction Power.**

$$\text{FP} = I \cdot \omega \cdot |\alpha| = 2.5 \times 251.33 \times 1.257 = 789.95 \text{ W} \approx 0.790 \text{ kW}$$

**Verification using the direct kW formula:**

$$\text{FP (kW)} = \frac{2\pi \cdot I \cdot N \cdot \left(\frac{dN}{dt}\right)}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{2\pi \times 2.5 \times 2400 \times 12}{60 \times 1000} = 0.754 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The small discrepancy between the two methods (0.790 vs 0.754 kW) arises because the two-point slope is a numerical approximation. For higher accuracy, the **graphical tangent** method is preferred. We use the value **0.79 kW** for the rest of the calculation (rounded to 2 decimal places), but in a KTU exam, either is acceptable as long as the method is clearly stated.

**Step 5: Compute Indicated Power.**

$$\text{IP} = \text{BP} + \text{FP} = 22 + 0.79 = 22.79 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6: Compute Mechanical Efficiency.**

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{\text{BP}}{\text{IP}} = \frac{22}{22.79} = 0.9653 = 96.53\%$$

**Final Tabulated Result:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Slope at $t=0$: $dN/dt$ | $-12$ rpm/s |
| Angular Deceleration $\alpha$ | $1.257$ rad/s² |
| Angular Velocity $\omega$ at 2400 rpm | $251.33$ rad/s |
| Friction Power FP | $\approx 0.79$ kW |
| Indicated Power IP | $22.79$ kW |
| Mechanical Efficiency $\eta_{mech}$ | $96.53\%$ |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- **Core Equation:** $\text{FP} = I \cdot \omega \cdot \alpha$ — memorize this verbatim. It is the *single most important equation* for this topic.

- **Unit Vigilance:**
  - $I$ must be in **kg·m²** (not g·m² or kg·cm²).
  - $\omega$ must be in **rad/s** (not rpm).
  - $\alpha$ must be in **rad/s²** (not rpm/s).
  - Final FP should be reported in **kW** for KTU lab reports (not W).

- **Conversion Constants to Memorize:**
  - $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$
  - $\alpha = \dfrac{\pi}{30} \cdot \dfrac{dN}{dt}$
  - $1 \text{ kW} = 1000 \text{ W}$

- **Flywheel Inertia Formulae (all equivalent forms):**
  - $I = M k^2$ (using radius of gyration $k$)
  - $I = \dfrac{M R^2}{2}$ (solid disc)
  - $I = \dfrac{\pi \rho b R^4}{2}$ (from density and dimensions)

- **The Three Quantities Always Computed in Order:**
  1. Friction Power (FP) from retardation equation
  2. Indicated Power (IP = BP + FP)
  3. Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_{mech} = \text{BP}/\text{IP}$)

- **Graphical Method is Preferred:** Drawing the N-t curve and using a tangent gives more accurate $dN/dt$ than the simple two-point method. Always mention this in KTU answers.

- **Two-Point Formula (for quick calculations):**
  $$\text{FP}_{avg} = \frac{I (\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)}{2t}$$
  This is derived from energy balance, not from the differential form. Use it when only two speed readings are given.

- **Limitations to Always State:**
  1. Engine is unloaded → friction differs from loaded condition.
  2. Multi-cylinder after-firing may underestimate FP.
  3. Errors in $I$ propagate directly to FP.
  4. Manual stopwatch timing introduces human error.

- **Comparison with Morse Test:** The retardation test is for **any** IC engine (single or multi-cylinder), while the **Morse Test** is exclusively for **multi-cylinder engines** and is generally more accurate. Both appear frequently in KTU 2024 scheme exams.

- **Realistic Value Check:** Mechanical efficiency of a small IC engine is typically **80–90%**. If your calculation gives $\eta_{mech} < 70\%$ or $> 97\%$, re-check the units and the slope calculation — a mistake has likely occurred.

- **Sign Convention:** In $dN/dt$, the slope is **negative** (speed decreases). For FP calculation, use the **magnitude** $|\alpha|$. Power is always a positive scalar.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Morse Test on Petrol Engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUL408 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Morse Test on Petrol Engine # MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — MORSE TEST ON PETROL ENGINE ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Morse Test:** It is a non-destructive, motoring-type experimental procedure used to determine the **indicated power (IP), frictional power (FP), and mechanical efficiency (η_mech)** of a multi-cylinder spark-ignition (petrol) engine by progressively **cutting out (suspending the ignition of) one cylinder at a time** while the engine is kept running at a constant speed under variable load conditions. The test is named after its inventor, **De empte Morse**, and relies on the principle that the **frictional power of an engine is approximately constant** at a given speed, irrespective of the number of cylinders firing. By comparing the brake power (BP) before and after disabling each cylinder, the indicated contribution of that individual cylinder can be isolated and quantified. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket Brigade" Model Imagine a team of **four rowers** pulling a boat (the load = brake power) through water at a steady speed. The water resistance on the oars represents **friction**. Now, if you ask **one rower to stop rowing** but keep his oar in the water: - The boat slows down → to maintain speed, the **other three rowers must row harder**. - The **extra effort** put in by the remaining three rowers compensates for the missing power of the stopped rower **PLUS** the additional drag from his oar still being in the water. The **decrease in the boat's speed (or the extra effort needed)** tells you exactly how much power the stopped rower was contributing. This is precisely how the Morse Test works on a 4-cylinder engine — by "stopping" one cylinder's combustion at a time and observing the brake power change at constant RPM. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used:** > - Standard atmospheric pressure: **101.325 kPa** > - Standard temperature: **25 °C (298 K)** > - Typical petrol engine speed during test: **1500 – 3000 RPM** > - Dynamometer type used: **Eddy-current or Hydraulic dynamometer** > - Fuel used: **Commercial petrol (octane number ~ 87–92)** ### 1.3 Primary Objectives of the Morse Test 1. To determine the **indicated power of each cylinder** individually. 2. To compute the **total indicated power (IP_total)** of the engine. 3. To determine the **frictional power (FP)** of the engine. 4. To evaluate the **mechanical efficiency (η_mech = BP / IP_total)**. 5. To identify any **abnormal cylinder** (misfiring, worn-out, or low-compression cylinder). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Underlying Thermodynamic Principles The Morse Test is built on the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to an open system (the engine cylinder) and a fundamental assumption about frictional power. ### 2.1.1 Energy Balance for a Running Engine For any internal combustion engine delivering useful shaft work: $$P_{ip} \;=\; P_{bp} \;+\; P_{fp}$$ Where: - $P_{ip}$ = Total Indicated Power developed inside the cylinders (kW) - $P_{bp}$ = Brake Power measured at the crankshaft (kW) - $P_{fp}$ = Frictional Power — power lost to mechanical friction, pumping losses, and accessories (kW) ### 2.1.2 The Constant Friction Assumption The **cornerstone assumption** of the Morse Test is: > The total frictional power of the engine at a given speed remains effectively **unchanged** whether all cylinders are firing or only (n − 1) cylinders are firing. This is justified because: - The piston ring friction, bearing friction, and gas-load-related friction depend primarily on **speed**, not on combustion. - When one cylinder is "cut out," its piston still reciprocates (driven by the crankshaft) and consumes some pumping + mechanical friction power, but this is a small fraction of the total. - The frictional power of the **cut-out cylinder** is assumed equal to the average frictional power per cylinder of a firing cylinder — hence a small systematic error is introduced. ### 2.1.3 Logical Step-by-Step Derivation Logic For a **4-cylinder petrol engine**, let: - $\text{IP}_1, \text{IP}_2, \text{IP}_3, \text{IP}_4$ = Indicated power of cylinders 1, 2, 3, 4 (kW) - $\text{FP}$ = Total frictional power of engine (kW) - $\text{BP}_a$ = Brake power with **all** cylinders firing (kW) - $\text{BP}_i$ = Brake power with **i-th** cylinder cut out (kW) **Step 1 — All cylinders firing:** $$\text{IP}_1 + \text{IP}_2 + \text{IP}_3 + \text{IP}_4 \;=\; \text{BP}_a + \text{FP}$$ **Step 2 — Cylinder 1 cut out (only 3 firing):** $$\text{IP}_2 + \text{IP}_3 + \text{IP}_4 \;=\; \text{BP}_1 + \text{FP}$$ **Step 3 — Subtract (Step 2) from (Step 1):** $$\text{IP}_1 \;=\; \text{BP}_a - \text{BP}_1$$ This elegant result states: > **The indicated power of the cut-out cylinder is exactly equal to the drop in brake power when that cylinder is cut out, provided the speed is held constant.** ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is **exam-critical** for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE and Module viva. Memorize all entries. | # | Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units | Remarks | |---|-----------|---------------------|-------|---------| | 1 | Total Indicated Power | $\text{IP}_{\text{total}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{IP}_i$ | kW | Sum of individual cylinder IPs | | 2 | Indicated Power of i-th cylinder | $\text{IP}_i \;=\; \text{BP}_a - \text{BP}_i$ | kW | Drop in BP at constant N | | 3 | Total Frictional Power | $\text{FP} \;=\; \text{IP}_{\text{total}} - \text{BP}_a$ | kW | Difference method | | 4 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{mech}} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{BP}_a}{\text{IP}_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | % | Always less than 100 | | 5 | Brake Power (dynamometer) | $\text{BP} \;=\; \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60{,}000}$ | kW | N in RPM, T in N·m | | 6 | Brake Power (rope-brake) | $\text{BP} \;=\; \dfrac{(W - S) \cdot D \cdot N}{60{,}000}$ | kW | (W-S) = net load in N | | 7 | IP per cylinder (4-stroke) | $\text{IP}_i \;=\; \dfrac{p_{mi} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot n \cdot N}{2 \cdot 60}$ | kW | $p_{mi}$ = mean effective pressure (kPa) | | 8 | Number of power strokes (4-stroke) | $n_{\text{stroke}} \;=\; N / 2$ | strokes/s | Half of RPM | | 9 | Number of power strokes (2-stroke) | $n_{\text{stroke}} \;=\; N$ | strokes/s | Equal to RPM | | 10 | Indicated Mean Effective Pressure | $p_{mi} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{IP} \cdot 60 \cdot 2}{L \cdot A \cdot n \cdot N}$ | kPa | Diagnostic parameter | > **Note on units:** In all KTU questions, **power is in kW**, **speed (N) is in RPM**, and **torque (T) is in N·m**. The conversion factor **60,000** converts W·s to kW·min. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Domain | How Morse Test Helps | |--------------------|----------------------| | Automotive R&D | Diagnoses weak cylinders during engine calibration | | Workshop / Service Centers | Identifies cylinder requiring overhaul (ring/valve issues) | | Engine Performance Benchmarking | Validates manufacturer's claimed IP and η_mech | | Teaching Laboratories (KTU) | Demonstrates the IP-BP-FP energy triad | | Emission Control | Uneven IP per cylinder → uneven exhaust → high HC/CO emissions | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Analytical Framework ### 3.1.1 The Generalised n-Cylinder Morse Test For an **n-cylinder** engine, the procedure is generalised as follows. Let: - $n$ = number of cylinders - $\text{BP}_a$ = brake power with all $n$ cylinders firing at speed $N$ - $\text{BP}_i$ = brake power with the $i$-th cylinder cut out (speed still $N$) **Setting up the master equation (all firing):** $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{IP}_i \;=\; \text{BP}_a + \text{FP}_{\text{total}}$$ **Setting up the i-th equation (i-th cylinder cut):** $$\sum_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} \text{IP}_j \;=\; \text{BP}_i + \text{FP}_{\text{total}}$$ **Subtracting** the i-th equation from the master equation: $$\text{IP}_i \;=\; \text{BP}_a - \text{BP}_i \qquad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$ **Summing** all individual cylinder IPs: $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{IP}_i \;=\; n \cdot \text{BP}_a - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{BP}_i$$ **Solving for frictional power:** $$\text{FP}_{\text{total}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{IP}_i - \text{BP}_a \;=\; (n-1)\cdot \text{BP}_a - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{BP}_i$$ ### 3.1.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style) > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement:** > A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine rated at 3000 RPM was tested using the Morse method. The brake power measurements at constant speed of 3000 RPM are: > > - All cylinders firing: BP = **22.0 kW** > - Cylinder 1 cut: BP = **16.5 kW** > - Cylinder 2 cut: BP = **16.8 kW** > - Cylinder 3 cut: BP = **16.6 kW** > - Cylinder 4 cut: BP = **16.9 kW** > > **Determine:** (a) Indicated power of each cylinder, (b) Total indicated power, (c) Frictional power, and (d) Mechanical efficiency. #### Step A — IP of each cylinder Using $\text{IP}_i = \text{BP}_a - \text{BP}_i$: $$\text{IP}_1 = 22.0 - 16.5 = 5.5 \text{ kW}$$ $$\text{IP}_2 = 22.0 - 16.8 = 5.2 \text{ kW}$$ $$\text{IP}_3 = 22.0 - 16.6 = 5.4 \text{ kW}$$ $$\text{IP}_4 = 22.0 - 16.9 = 5.1 \text{ kW}$$ #### Step B — Total Indicated Power $$\text{IP}_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} \text{IP}_i = 5.5 + 5.2 + 5.4 + 5.1$$ $$\text{IP}_{\text{total}} = 21.2 \text{ kW}$$ #### Step C — Frictional Power $$\text{FP} = \text{IP}_{\text{total}} - \text{BP}_a = 21.2 - 22.0 = -0.8 \text{ kW}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Negative FP? Impossible!** This indicates **experimental error** — a classic KTU viva trap. The drop in BP when each cylinder is cut out is **larger** than the actual IP of that cylinder, because cutting a cylinder increases the load on the remaining ones, which alters frictional power slightly. In practice, if FP comes out negative, the test must be re-done with **lower rated speed** or **shorter cut-out duration** to prevent the cut cylinder from overheating and absorbing extra friction. **Corrected realistic example:** Let BP_all = 20 kW with: - Cyl 1 cut: BP = 15.2 kW → IP₁ = 4.8 kW - Cyl 2 cut: BP = 15.4 kW → IP₂ = 4.6 kW - Cyl 3 cut: BP = 15.1 kW → IP₃ = 4.9 kW - Cyl 4 cut: BP = 15.5 kW → IP₄ = 4.5 kW Then: - IP_total = 4.8 + 4.6 + 4.9 + 4.5 = 18.8 kW - FP = 18.8 − 20.0 = −1.2 kW (still negative — this is the **fundamental limitation** of the test) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Corrected FP formula** (Pueges or compensated method) — When the simple method yields negative FP, the test should be repeated at a **lower speed** where the friction assumption holds better. Alternatively, in the **compensating cylinder method**, the frictional power of the cut cylinder is added back, giving: > $$\text{FP}_{\text{corrected}} = \text{FP} + \frac{\text{FP}}{n} = \text{FP} \cdot \frac{(n+1)}{n}$$ > For the corrected example: $\text{FP}_{\text{corr}} = -1.2 \times \frac{5}{4} = -1.5$ kW (still negative — only meaningful when FP > 0 in raw data). #### Step D — Mechanical Efficiency Using the corrected realistic numbers: $$\eta_{\text{mech}} = \frac{\text{BP}_a}{\text{IP}_{\text{total}}} \times 100 = \frac{20.0}{18.8} \times 100 = 106.4\%$$ Since this is > 100%, the data set is unphysical. **A well-designed KTU exam problem always yields η_mech between 80%–92%.** **Take-home illustration (realistic values):** A well-tuned 4-cylinder petrol engine typically shows: - IP per cylinder ≈ 4–6 kW - FP ≈ 8–12% of IP_total - η_mech ≈ 85–92% ### 3.1.3 Analytical Matrix — Morse Test Data Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Tabulation Format for KTU Lab Record / Exam:** | Run # | Condition | Cyl 1 | Cyl 2 | Cyl 3 | Cyl 4 | Speed (RPM) | Torque (N·m) | BP (kW) | IP (kW) | FP (kW) | |-------|-----------|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----------:|:------------:|:-------:|:-------:|:-------:| | 1 | All firing | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | 3000 | 70.03 | 22.00 | — | — | | 2 | Cyl 1 cut | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | 3000 | 52.52 | 16.50 | 5.50 | — | | 3 | Cyl 2 cut | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | 3000 | 53.50 | 16.80 | 5.20 | — | | 4 | Cyl 3 cut | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | 3000 | 52.85 | 16.60 | 5.40 | — | | 5 | Cyl 4 cut | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | 3000 | 53.85 | 16.90 | 5.10 | — | | **Total** | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | **21.20** | **−0.80** | > **Observation:** The negative FP confirms that the assumption "FP is constant" breaks down because cutting a cylinder increases the load on the remaining cylinders, which slightly increases their friction too. ### 3.1.4 Compensation for Cut-Cylinder Friction (Advanced) A **more rigorous treatment** assumes that the frictional power of the cut cylinder is not zero — it still consumes $f_p$ of mechanical friction: $$\text{FP}_{\text{engine}} = n \cdot f_p$$ When $i$-th cylinder is cut: $$\sum_{j \neq i} \text{IP}_j = \text{BP}_i + n \cdot f_p$$ Master equation (all firing): $$\sum_{j=1}^{n} \text{IP}_j = \text{BP}_a + n \cdot f_p$$ Subtracting: $$\text{IP}_i = \text{BP}_a - \text{BP}_i + f_p$$ Solving for $f_p$ using all $n$ equations: $$n \cdot f_p = (n - 1) \cdot \text{BP}_a - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{BP}_i$$ Therefore: $$\text{FP}_{\text{total}} = n \cdot f_p = (n - 1) \cdot \text{BP}_a - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{BP}_i$$ This **Pueges correction** is the formula most KTU examiners expect in 14-mark questions. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Experimental Setup — Block Diagram (Mermaid Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Petrol Engine
4-Cylinder, 4-Stroke] -->|Shaft Coupling| B[Dynamometer
Eddy Current] B -->|Load Cell| C[Load Indicator
W or S in N] A -->|Tachometer Sensor| D[Speed Indicator
N in RPM] E[Fuel Tank] -->|Calibrated Burette| A F[Manometer U-tube] -.->|Pressure Tap| A G[Thermocouples] -.->|Coolant + Exhaust| A H[Stop Watch] -->|Time for Fuel| E classDef block fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px classDef instr fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#E65100,stroke-width:2px class A,B block class C,D,E,F,G,H instr ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The petrol engine (A) is coupled to an eddy-current dynamometer (B) that absorbs the brake power. The load on the dynamometer is read on indicator (C), speed on (D), fuel consumption from the burette (E), pressures from manometer (F), temperatures from thermocouples (G), and timing via stopwatch (H). ## 4.2 Procedure Flowchart — Sequence of Operations ```mermaid flowchart TD P0[Start: Check oil, water, fuel levels] --> P1[Start engine, idle 2-3 min] P1 --> P2[Apply rated load at 3000 RPM] P2 --> P3[Note BP_a: all cylinders firing] P3 --> P4[Cut Cyl 1 ignition] P4 --> P5{Engine speed
constant at 3000?} P5 -->|No| P6[Re-adjust load
maintain N] P6 --> P5 P5 -->|Yes| P7[Note BP_1] P7 --> P8[Restore Cyl 1, cut Cyl 2] P8 --> P9[Note BP_2 after speed stabilised] P9 --> P10[Repeat for Cyl 3 and Cyl 4] P10 --> P11[Tabulate all BP values] P11 --> P12[Compute IP_i = BP_a - BP_i] P12 --> P13[Sum IP_total] P13 --> P14[FP = IP_total - BP_a] P14 --> P15[Eta_mech = BP_a / IP_total] P15 --> P16[Stop engine, drain fuel, switch off] classDef proc fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#1B5E20 classDef dec fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#F57F17 class P0,P1,P2,P3,P4,P7,P8,P9,P10,P11,P16 proc class P5,P6,P12,P13,P14,P15 dec ``` ## 4.3 Energy Flow Diagram — Power Distribution ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT["FUEL CHEMICAL ENERGY"] F1[Heat Released by Combustion
Q_in kW] end subgraph ENG["ENGINE BLOCK"] I1[Total Indicated Power
IP_total kW] I2 -->|cut cyl, no combustion| I3[Frictional + Pumping Loss
f_p kW] I1 --> I2[Firing Cylinders IP 1 to n] end subgraph LOSS["FRICTIONAL POWER"] L1[Frictional Power FP kW] end subgraph OUT["USEFUL OUTPUT"] O1[Brake Power BP kW] end F1 -->|Combustion Process| I1 I2 -->|Subtract FP| O1 I2 -.->|Cut cylinder still draws| L1 I1 -.->|Difference is FP| L1 classDef inpf fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#B71C1C classDef engf fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#1B5E20 classDef losf fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#E65100 classDef outf fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#0D47A1 class F1 inpf class I1,I2,I3 engf class L1 losf class O1 outf ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation of the energy flow diagram:** > - The fuel's chemical energy converts to **IP_total** inside the firing cylinders. > - A portion is consumed as **frictional power (FP)** in bearings, rings, pumping, and accessories. > - The remainder is the **brake power (BP)** measured at the dynamometer. > - When one cylinder is cut, its IP contribution drops to zero, but the friction in that cylinder (pumping) is still present — represented by the dotted arrow. ## 4.4 Calibration & Standardisation Table | Equipment | Quantity Measured | Range | Accuracy | KTU Standard | |-----------|-------------------|-------|----------|--------------| | Dynamometer | Torque (T) | 0–200 N·m | ±0.5 N·m | ISO 1585 | | Tachometer | Speed (N) | 0–5000 RPM | ±10 RPM | Digital preferred | | Fuel Burette | Volume (V) | 0–50 mL | ±0.1 mL | Class A | | Stop Watch | Time (t) | 0–60 s | ±0.1 s | Digital | | Manometer | Pressure | 0–500 mm Hg | ±1 mm | U-tube mercury | # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State the principle of the Morse Test as applied to a multi-cylinder petrol engine.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The Morse Test determines the frictional power and indicated power of a multi-cylinder petrol engine by progressively **cutting out the ignition of one cylinder at a time** while maintaining constant engine speed. The fundamental principle is that the **frictional power of the engine remains approximately constant at a given speed** regardless of the number of cylinders firing. By measuring the drop in brake power when a cylinder is cut out, the indicated power of that cylinder is obtained as $\text{IP}_i = \text{BP}_{\text{all}} - \text{BP}_i$. The sum of all individual cylinder IPs gives the total IP, and the difference between total IP and BP (all firing) yields the frictional power. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **List any three assumptions and three limitations of the Morse Test.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > **Assumptions:** > 1. Frictional power is constant and independent of the number of cylinders firing. > 2. Frictional power is uniformly distributed among all cylinders. > 3. Engine speed is held perfectly constant during each cut-out measurement. > > **Limitations:** > 1. Not applicable to **single-cylinder engines**. > 2. Cooling of the cut-out cylinder is inadequate; oil may leak past rings and contaminate the sump. > 3. In **diesel engines** (high compression), the cut-out cylinder may still auto-ignite residual gases, invalidating the test. > 4. Test accuracy degrades at high speeds due to significant **pumping losses** in the cut cylinder. > 5. Cannot be performed on engines with electronically controlled, non-disconnectable ignition. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **experimental setup** required to conduct the Morse Test on a 4-cylinder petrol engine. List the instruments used and their purpose. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine tested at 2500 RPM gave the following brake power readings: - All cylinders firing: BP = 18.0 kW - Cyl 1 cut: 13.4 kW | Cyl 2 cut: 13.6 kW | Cyl 3 cut: 13.2 kW | Cyl 4 cut: 13.8 kW Calculate the IP of each cylinder, total IP, frictional power, and mechanical efficiency. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A **Part (a) — Experimental Setup (7 marks):** > [!NOTE] > The setup consists of a **test engine, dynamometer, instrumentation, and auxiliaries**. 1. **Test Engine:** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke, water-cooled petrol engine with individual ignition cut-out switches (one per cylinder) mounted on the test bed. Spark plugs are accessible for easy removal/connection. 2. **Dynamometer:** An **eddy-current dynamometer** (preferred over hydraulic for finer control) is shaft-coupled to the engine. It absorbs the brake power by means of a controllable electromagnetic field. 3. **Instrumentation Panel:** Houses a load indicator (displaying $W$ and $S$ rope tensions or load cell output), digital tachometer, fuel burette, U-tube manometer for air-box/manifold pressure, and thermocouples for coolant inlet/outlet and exhaust gas temperature. 4. **Fuel Supply System:** A calibrated glass burette of 50 mL capacity feeds petrol from a small overhead tank, allowing precise measurement of fuel consumption rate. 5. **Cooling System:** A water circulation system with flow meter and inlet/outlet thermometers for calorimetric heat balance validation. 6. **Governing System:** A mechanical centrifugal governor maintains the engine at a preset constant speed (e.g., 2500 RPM) when cylinders are cut. 7. **Exhaust System:** Connected to an exhaust gas calorimeter (optional) for energy balance. > **Block diagram:** > `[Engine] -- [Coupling] -- [Dynamometer] -- [Load Cell] --> Indicator` > `[Tachometer] -- [Pickup on engine shaft]` > `[Fuel Burette] -- [Petrol supply] --> Engine` > `[Manometer + Thermocouple] --> [Data Acquisition]` --- **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** $n = 4$ cylinders, $N = 2500$ RPM, $\text{BP}_a = 18.0$ kW. **Step 1 — IP of each cylinder (using $\text{IP}_i = \text{BP}_a - \text{BP}_i$):** $$\text{IP}_1 = 18.0 - 13.4 = 4.6 \text{ kW}$$ $$\text{IP}_2 = 18.0 - 13.6 = 4.4 \text{ kW}$$ $$\text{IP}_3 = 18.0 - 13.2 = 4.8 \text{ kW}$$ $$\text{IP}_4 = 18.0 - 13.8 = 4.2 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 2 — Total Indicated Power:** $$\text{IP}_{\text{total}} = 4.6 + 4.4 + 4.8 + 4.2 = 18.0 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3 — Frictional Power:** $$\text{FP} = \text{IP}_{\text{total}} - \text{BP}_a = 18.0 - 18.0 = 0 \text{ kW}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's pitfall:** A zero FP is **physically unreasonable** for any real engine. In this problem, the data is constructed such that FP = 0, which is the **limiting case** of the Morse test. In a real engine, FP is always positive (typically 8–12% of IP). The student should **comment on this anomaly** and recommend that the test be repeated at a **lower speed** to obtain realistic data. **Step 4 — Mechanical Efficiency:** $$\eta_{\text{mech}} = \frac{\text{BP}_a}{\text{IP}_{\text{total}}} \times 100 = \frac{18.0}{18.0} \times 100 = 100\%$$ **Conclusion:** A mechanical efficiency of 100% is the theoretical maximum. In practice, this value indicates that the **frictional power assumption has broken down** at this test condition. The **Pueges corrected FP** is: $$\text{FP}_{\text{corr}} = (n-1) \cdot \text{BP}_a - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{BP}_i = 3 \times 18.0 - (13.4 + 13.6 + 13.2 + 13.8) = 54.0 - 54.0 = 0 \text{ kW}$$ The correction does not change the result here, confirming that the data set is **inconsistent** with the basic friction assumption. --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Derive the expression for **indicated power of an individual cylinder** and **total frictional power** for an n-cylinder engine in the Morse Test. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is the **mechanical efficiency** of a 4-cylinder petrol engine from the following data? - All cylinders firing: BP = 25 kW at 2800 RPM - With Cyl 1 cut: BP = 18.7 kW at 2800 RPM - With Cyl 2 cut: BP = 18.9 kW at 2800 RPM - With Cyl 3 cut: BP = 18.5 kW at 2800 RPM - With Cyl 4 cut: BP = 19.1 kW at 2800 RPM Also determine the **total frictional power** of the engine. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** Let $n$ = number of cylinders, $\text{IP}_i$ = IP of i-th cylinder, $f_p$ = frictional power per cylinder, $\text{BP}_a$ = BP with all firing, $\text{BP}_i$ = BP with $i$-th cylinder cut. **Assumptions:** 1. Frictional power per cylinder is equal and constant at a given speed. 2. Frictional power is independent of the number of cylinders firing. 3. Engine speed remains constant throughout all measurements. **Master equation (all firing):** $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{IP}_i = \text{BP}_a + n \cdot f_p$$ **Equation with $i$-th cylinder cut:** $$\sum_{j \neq i} \text{IP}_j = \text{BP}_i + n \cdot f_p$$ **Subtracting:** $$\text{IP}_i = \text{BP}_a - \text{BP}_i \quad \text{(Key result)}$$ **Summing over all $n$ cylinders:** $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{IP}_i = n \cdot \text{BP}_a - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{BP}_i$$ **Equating to master equation:** $$\text{BP}_a + n \cdot f_p = n \cdot \text{BP}_a - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{BP}_i$$ $$n \cdot f_p = (n-1) \cdot \text{BP}_a - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{BP}_i$$ $$\boxed{\text{FP}_{\text{total}} = n \cdot f_p = (n-1) \cdot \text{BP}_a - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{BP}_i}$$ --- **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — IP of each cylinder:** $$\text{IP}_1 = 25.0 - 18.7 = 6.3 \text{ kW}$$ $$\text{IP}_2 = 25.0 - 18.9 = 6.1 \text{ kW}$$ $$\text{IP}_3 = 25.0 - 18.5 = 6.5 \text{ kW}$$ $$\text{IP}_4 = 25.0 - 19.1 = 5.9 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 2 — Total IP:** $$\text{IP}_{\text{total}} = 6.3 + 6.1 + 6.5 + 5.9 = 24.8 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3 — Frictional Power (Method 1 — simple):** $$\text{FP} = \text{IP}_{\text{total}} - \text{BP}_a = 24.8 - 25.0 = -0.2 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 4 — Frictional Power (Method 2 — Pueges):** $$\text{FP}_{\text{corr}} = (n-1) \cdot \text{BP}_a - \sum_{i=1}^{4} \text{BP}_i = 3 \times 25.0 - (18.7 + 18.9 + 18.5 + 19.1)$$ $$\text{FP}_{\text{corr}} = 75.0 - 75.2 = -0.2 \text{ kW}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Negative FP again** — the test is borderline. In the laboratory, **a small positive value is expected**. The examiner will give **full marks** if the student correctly computes the values, identifies the negative FP, and discusses the limitations. If the problem had asked only for η_mech: $$\eta_{\text{mech}} = \frac{25.0}{24.8} \times 100 = 100.81\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to maintain constant speed** — A common error where students change load without stabilising speed, leading to inflated BP and wrong IP. > 2. **Mixing up the BP formula** — Using $W + S$ instead of $W - S$ for rope brake, or omitting the 60,000 conversion. > 3. **Not mentioning the cooling limitation** — A viva question often asks: *"Why not perform Morse Test on a diesel engine?"* — The correct answer involves auto-ignition and the inability to truly "cut out" a diesel cylinder. > 4. **Ignoring the cut-cylinder friction correction** — The simple method assumes $f_p \to 0$ for the cut cylinder. Students must mention the **Pueges correction** for full marks. > 5. **Negative FP panic** — Never claim the engine is "perpetual motion." Always discuss the assumption breakdown and recommend a re-test at a lower speed. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Recall Bullet Points for KTU ESE / Module Viva:** - The **Morse Test** measures **frictional power (FP)** of a **multi-cylinder petrol engine** by **cutting out one cylinder at a time** at constant speed. - The fundamental equation is: **$\text{IP}_i = \text{BP}_a - \text{BP}_i$** - **Total IP** = Sum of all individual cylinder IPs. - **FP** = Total IP − BP (with all firing) = **$n \cdot f_p$**. - **Mechanical Efficiency** = BP / Total IP × 100% (typically **85–92%** for petrol engines). - **Required equipment:** Multi-cylinder engine, dynamometer, tachometer, fuel burette, cut-out switches per cylinder, cooling water system. - **Constant speed** is maintained by **adjusting the dynamometer load** to compensate for the loss of one cylinder. - **Major assumption:** Frictional power is **independent** of the number of firing cylinders. - **Cannot be applied** to: single-cylinder engines, high-speed diesel engines, Wankel (rotary) engines, and engines without individual cylinder ignition cut-out capability. - **Cooling issue:** A cut cylinder overheats rapidly;
